Given my significant chip lead and the favorable price, I will call to see a flop rather than folding or bloating the pot with a marginal hand out of position.

I was the preflop aggressor and the flop (7c Td 3d) gives me a strong semi-bluff: 6c8c has an open‑ended straight draw (5 or 9 = 8 outs, ~31% to hit by river) plus a backdoor club flush (~10→9/47 * 8/46 ≈ 4.4% for both turn+river clubs). The board is relatively dry and misses a big part of Flash’s calling range, so a continuation bet both gets fold equity and builds the pot when I hit. I’ll size to about half the pot to balance fold equity and pot building (pot = 400 → bet 200). If Flash raises big I’ll re-evaluate (often fold to a large 3‑bet, or call/continue against small sizing depending on range); if he calls I can continue barreling on favorable turns or pot‑control when a scary card comes.

On a flop of 7c-Td-3d, my Qh 6d has completely missed. I have only one overcard to the board that is likely to be good (the Queen), and no significant draws. Minni has been playing aggressively, and calling a half-pot bet with total air out of position is a losing play in the long run. Given my substantial chip lead, there is no reason to defend such a weak holding against a continuation bet on a board that favors a typical opening range.

I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind). On the button in a 3-handed pot I want to take the initiative — 9s6h is marginal but fully reasonable to open from the button rather than limp. A 3x sizing gives solid fold equity against both blinds, denies good odds to call, and keeps the pot playable if called. If Flash (SB) 3-bets I can fold this hand; if Claude (BB) calls I can play the flop in position and c-bet favorable textures or fold to strong resistance. Given our stack depth (~108bb) this sizing maximizes expected value here.

I am holding 8d 2c in the Small Blind. The button (Minni) has raised to 300. This is a very weak hand to play out of position, and there is no reason to defend or bluff in this situation against a standard button open. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Folding 8-5 suited from the big blind. While the hand is suited, it's still marginal with limited showdown value. Out of position against an aggressive button raiser, and with a relatively short 73.5 BB stack, this is not a profitable spot to contest. Need to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ace-Five suited (Ah 5h), which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. I have the largest stack at the table. My strategy is to open with a raise to put pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a hand that has both high card value and good post-flop potential (flushes and wheel straights). A standard 3x raise to 300 is the most effective play here.

3-10 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't warrant further investment. Even though I'm in the small blind, this is well below the threshold for defending against Flash's raise. Key factors: (1) 3h-Tc is essentially unplayable - no real draw potential, poor high card; (2) I'm out of position post-flop if I call, facing a raiser with significant chip advantage (Flash has 21.3k vs my 7.3k); (3) With 250 to call into a 450 pot, I'm not getting sufficient odds to justify calling with this hand; (4) Stack preservation is important - I should wait for a stronger hand and better spot rather than bleeding chips with marginal holdings. Clean fold here.

I’m in the big blind with 4♦2♠ facing a button open to 300 (to call 200). Pot is 450 — calling 200 to win 650 requires ~30.8% equity. 4-2 offsuit has very poor playability, is frequently dominated by a button’s opening range, and has terrible reverse implied odds postflop. I could consider a 3-bet bluff in extremely exploitative spots, but with this hand and a standard opener size folding is the +EV play — preserve my deep stack for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with 9d7s, facing a 100 chip call to stay in the hand. While position is valuable, 97o is a weak, non-connected hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially in a multiway pot against two opponents where Flash has shown aggression throughout the session. With a moderate stack of 7,300 (73 big blinds), I can afford to be selective. I'll fold and wait for a premium hand or better situation where I have position with stronger holdings.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Kc Js — a strong hand in HU. I want to take the initiative and use my positional advantage postflop, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) rather than just completing. This sizing avoids giving the big blind easy pot odds with marginal hands and keeps my opening range strong. If Flash 3-bets significantly I’ll be prepared to fold KJ offsuit; if he calls I’ll play the flop last, c-bet favorable textures and control the pot on draw-heavy boards.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, Queen-Four offsuit. Minni raised from the Small Blind to 300. Although I would have position on Minni post-flop, this hand is easily dominated and does not play well. Given my significant chip lead, there is no reason to defend with marginal or sub-par holdings in this spot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I'm on the button with 6♠8♠ (suited) and effective stacks are deep (~110bb for me, 72bb for the BB). This is a good spot to seize initiative — suited connectors have strong playability in position and gain value from fold equity. A standard 3xBB open (to 300 total) applies pressure to the blinds without overcommitting; it prices out some weak defenders and gives me favorable postflop position if called. If I face a large 3-bet I'll fold this hand; if I get single calls I'll play postflop aggressively with plans to c-bet favorable flops and realize equity on draws. Therefore I open-raise to 300 to maximize expected value from this position and hand.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. Minni has raised from the button, which is a standard play. While King-high has some raw value, K5o is a weak hand to play out of position against a raise. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that Claude in the Big Blind has a shorter stack and might be looking to shove or defend, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary post-flop complications with a marginal hand.

3d-9d is a weak hand (9-high with a poor kicker), and I'm out of position in the big blind. Minni made a standard 3x raise from the button, and while I only need 200 to call into a 450 pot, this hand plays poorly out of position postflop. With 7,200 chips, I'm not in immediate jeopardy and can afford to wait for better spots. Folding preserves my stack for premium hands where I have better equity and positional advantage. This is a straightforward fold.

In this 3-handed situation, I am on the button with 97 offsuit. While button aggression is important in short-handed play, 97o is at the bottom of a standard opening range. Given my significant chip lead and Minni's recent aggressive play in the blinds, I will opt to fold this marginal hand to avoid unnecessary confrontations and maintain a disciplined image.

Heads-up situation with K7o in the small blind. While marginal, this hand has reasonable equity against a wide BB range and I gain positional advantage postflop. The cost to call (50 more) is minimal relative to pot size (150), and I cannot afford to fold too many hands from SB without bleeding chips. This is a standard heads-up small blind call.

I’m in the big blind with J♥2♣ facing a limp from the small blind (Claude called 50). This is a weak, poorly-playable offsuit hand that will often be dominated by limp-calling ranges. There’s no cost to see the flop, and with deep stacks (I ~110BB, Claude ~71BB) I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with J2o. Raising to isolate is possible, but this hand has little value as a preflop raiser and would create a tougher postflop decision set. Plan: check and see a cheap flop; if Claude checks behind we get a free board, if he bets a small c-bet we can call selectively with any backdoor outs or fold to strong pressure, and if we pair the J we can proceed/raise for value. Therefore: check.

K7o on a paired (Td Th) board is marginal at best. Betting here achieves little - I won't win many chips from worse hands, and I'm in bad shape against hands that called me from BB (likely has better). Checking allows me to see the turn cheaply and keep the pot manageable. This is a prudent line that keeps variance low and doesn't commit excess chips to a weak holding.

Board is T♦4♠T♥; I hold J♥2♣ — no pair and essentially no realistic draws (only a backdoor club). Claude limped preflop and checked the flop, which points to a wide/weak range or pot control with a medium hand; there’s also the chance of a slow-played ten but I shouldn’t build the pot with junk. Pot is small (200) and stacks are deep, so betting to bluff risks a raise or getting called by better hands I can’t outplay. Best line is to control the pot and take a free card: check and see the turn. If Claude bets, I’ll fold to meaningful pressure and only entertain calling a very tiny probe.

Turn brings the J♣, which doesn't improve my K♥7♣ at all. I remain with just King-high on a paired board (T♦ 4♠ T♥ J♣), which is a marginal holding at best. Minni checked the flop, suggesting a weak hand or a checking strategy. Given the weakness of my holding, checking the turn is prudent. This lets me see the river cheaply if Minni also checks, or allows me to evaluate whether to fold if Minni bets and shows strength. With a short stack (7,100), I cannot afford to commit chips to marginal holdings without good reason. Checking preserves my stack and minimizes variance on a hand that likely needs to improve to win.

Board T♦-4♠-T♥-J♣ and I hold J♥2♣ — I have top two pair (Jacks and Tens). Claude limp-called preflop and checked both flop and turn, which points to a wide, relatively passive calling range (broadways, small pairs, some Jx, occasional Ten). Very few limp/call hands realistically beat me (mainly trips/full houses from rare pocket Tens or JJ), so this is a good spot to extract value. Betting also denies a free river card that could pair the board (J or T) and gives poorer Jx and other single-pair hands a price to continue. Pot is 200, so I’ll make a half-pot value bet to keep worse hands in but avoid scaring them off: bet 100. If Claude check-raises I’ll re-evaluate (call smaller aggression for value, respect a large shove that more credibly represents a full house/quads).

Folding K-7 on a paired board after Minni's turn raise. My King-high has insufficient equity (~20% or less) against Minni's value range after the flop check and turn raise. With a short stack of 7,100 chips, I cannot afford marginal calls. The 50 chips already invested is a sunk cost. Preserving my stack for better spots is critical for long-term tournament survival.

8-3 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. While position on the button is advantageous, this hand lacks the fundamental qualities needed to play - no high card strength, poor kicker with the 3, and minimal draw potential post-flop. At 7100 chips, I can afford to be selective and fold marginal/weak hands. Even with positional advantage, 8-3o should be folded pre-flop in almost all circumstances. The 100 chips to call is manageable, but committing chips with such a weak hand against two opponents is poor fundamental poker. I should preserve my chip stack for premium hands where I have better equity.

Heads-up with deep effective stacks (~112BB). 8♠6♣ is a marginal hand but has decent postflop playability in heads-up play and I will have position after the flop (button). I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the BB’s wide defending range, and avoid letting Flash see a cheap flop. A 3x open (300) gives reasonable fold equity and prices weaker hands out more than a min-raise; if he calls I retain positional advantage and can play postflop proactively. If he 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand most of the time; I can defend rarely vs a small 3-bet depending on the read. Calling to 100 is a passive alternative, folding is too tight. Action: raise to 300 to seize initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 2c. Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind after Claude folded the Button. Although I have position post-flop and the pot odds are roughly 2:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), 52s is a very weak hand that realizes its equity poorly. Minni has been quite active and aggressive recently. Given my significant chip lead, there is no need to defend with the bottom of my range and potentially get involved in a difficult spot out of position or face a continuation bet on a marginal board. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

On the button with T9o I want to take the initiative and use position. A standard 3x open to 300 applies pressure to the blinds, has good fold equity, and keeps postflop options versus one or two callers. With ~113bb I can comfortably play postflop in position if called; I will c-bet favorable textures and tighten up vs a large 3-bet. Raising to 300 maximizes EV here versus just limping or calling.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 2d. The button (Minni) has raised to 300. Although the hand is suited, 8-high suited is very weak, and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. Given the current stack sizes and the relative strength of my hand against a typical button opening range, folding is the most prudent and standard play to conserve my chip lead.

I'm in the Big Blind with Ad7d (Ace-Seven of diamonds), facing Minni's raise to 300 from the Button. Flash folded from the Small Blind.

Key considerations:
1. **Hand strength**: Ad7d is a solid hand in heads-up play with good kicker value and flush potential
2. **Pot odds**: I need to call 200 more to win a 450 chip pot, giving me 2.25-to-1 odds
3. **Position**: Post-flop I'll have position on Minni
4. **Minni's tendencies**: Throughout the tournament, Minni has been a relatively aggressive raiser but has also checked down hands post-flop (see hands 17, 51, 61, etc.)
5. **Stack depth**: With 7000 chips, I need to play hands with positive expected value, and A7 clearly qualifies here

A7 is too good to fold against a single opponent's button raise. Calling allows me to:
- See the flop in position
- Win if I hit an ace, any seven, or make a flush draw
- Play efficiently given my stack size

3-betting would be overly aggressive with this hand and medium stack. Calling is the optimal play here.

Checking is optimal with Ad7d on 3s-6h-3h. I have ~11 outs with approximately 44% equity, good implied odds, and multiple ways to improve. Minni's check suggests weakness. Checking back maintains pot control, gathers information, and allows me to see the turn while preserving my stack for better spots. The board is dry with limited connectivity, so my overcard and draws are strong.

I opened preflop and have position with T9o on a very dry, low paired flop (3s 6h 3h). This texture misses a big portion of the BB’s calling range (broadways, many Ax) while only really connecting with small pairs and the occasional 6/3. I want to take the initiative, use my positional and fold equity, and keep the pot under control deep-stacked. Bet ~½ pot (300) to fold out unpaired overcards and define Claude’s range. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and barrel selectively on favorable cards; if he raises big I’ll fold — I have no pair or real equity to continue.

A7d on 3-3-6 offers approximately 44% equity against Minni's likely range. The pot odds of 3.17-to-1 require only 24% equity to break even, making this a mathematically profitable call. I have multiple outs including any diamond for the flush draw, any ace for top pair, and any seven for a pair. The call represents only 4.4% of my stack, and I'll maintain position on the turn. Against Minni's button-raise range, this hand equity justifies the investment despite the dry board.

Checking is optimal on the turn with Ad7d on 3s-6h-3h-Kd. I'm out of position with a drawing hand that has decent equity but needs further development. The King is a slightly scary card that could have helped Minni's range. Checking allows me to control pot size, see the river, and gather information from Minni's action. If they bet, I have proper odds for a call given my outs (flush draw, ace, seven). This is superior to betting, which would commit more chips while out of position with a non-made hand against an aggressive opponent.

Turn brings K♦ — a bad, high card for my T9o. Claude called my flop c-bet on 3♠6♥3♥, so his range includes small pairs (3s/6s), pocket pairs and broadway/ax cards that now often benefit from a King on the turn (or at least continue as air floats). I have no pair, no real draw (only 6 total outs to pair), and much less fold equity now that the board contains a King. Betting here would frequently be called by better hands and only fold out the very weakest holdings — it’s a negative-EV bluff. In position I should control the pot size, check and see what Claude does on the river. If he bets a small amount on the river I can decide whether to call or fold depending on sizing and the river card; if he checks back I’ll get a free card and can re-evaluate. Therefore I check to avoid bloating the pot with a bluff that has poor equity.

Ace-high on the river out of position. Minni's turn check indicates weakness or a stalled hand. Taking this to showdown is optimal - my hand beats missed draws and checking controls pot size. Betting risks additional chips against a hand that either folds (costing me value) or calls/raises (where I'm likely beat). The Queen brick doesn't change Minni's likely holdings significantly. Showdown is the mathematically sound play here.

I check. I opened and c-bet the dry 3-3-6 flop, Claude called, then both of us checked the King turn and he checked the Queen river. I hold T9o and have missed — no pair and no meaningful showdown value against Claude's calling range (small/medium pocket pairs, Kx/Qx, and some floats). A river bluff here has very little fold equity: he already called the flop and the turn/river checks indicate he'll often call a small bet with at least a pair. Betting would usually be called by better hands and only fold the absolute weakest holdings, so it's negative EV. Checking controls the pot, avoids getting raised or committing chips with almost no equity, and lets me reach showdown cheaply if he shows down a weak hand. If Claude bets after my check I will re-evaluate based on sizing and his line, but the correct action now is to check.

Holding QJ offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a strong opening candidate. With both opponents having healthy stacks (77BB and 106BB), I want to play a standard aggressive pre-flop strategy. Raising here allows me to take initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play the pot in position if called. Based on previous hands, Minni is quite active, so I expect a call from the blinds frequently, but my hand strength and position warrant a standard raise.

8-2o is one of the worst starting hands in poker and I'm in the small blind facing a button raise. With 77 BB in stack depth and no hand strength, this is a straightforward fold. The pot odds don't justify playing such a weak holding out of position.

Facing a BTN open to 300 from the big blind with 3♣2♣. Pot is 450 and I need to add 200 to call, which gives break-even equity of about 200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8%. 3-2 suited is a speculative hand with reasonable equity and good postflop playability heads-up, and stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with such a weak hand — the min‑raise to 500 doesn’t offer enough fold equity to justify turning this into a bluff. Plan: call to see a flop and proceed only with meaningful pair/flush/straight equity or use position on later streets; fold to significant aggression if I miss.

I called preflop with 3♣2♣ as a speculative, implied-odds hand. The flop T♦-4♥-J♦ completely misses me: no pair, no realistic draws. With zero clubs on the board and only two cards to come I cannot make a five‑card club flush, and the backdoor straight is extremely remote. The BTN opener will have many Tx/Jx, overcards, and diamond draws that connect here more often than I do. I’m out of position and have very little fold equity, so bluffing OOP is a poor long‑term play. Plan: check and relinquish the pot to any meaningful bet (fold to significant aggression). If the opponent checks back I’ll see a free turn and reassess only if I pick up real equity or a good bluffing opportunity on a safe runout.

On a board of Td 4h Jd, I have top pair with a Queen kicker (Qd Jh). This is a strong hand, but the board is somewhat coordinated with both flush and straight draws possible. Minni has checked to me, and since I was the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to extract value from weaker hands like middle pair (tens), draws (diamonds or straight draws), and to protect my hand. A bet of approximately 60% of the pot (400 into 650) is appropriate to charge the draws while still being small enough to get called by inferior made hands.

I called preflop with 3♣2♣ as a speculative hand, but the flop T♦-4♥-J♦ completely misses me: no pair, no club flush, and no realistic straight draw. Facing a 400 bet into a 1050 pot I need ~27.6% equity to justify a call (400/(1050+400)). My chance to hit at least a pair by the river is ~24% — below the pot odds — and even if I pair I'll be out of position versus a flop c-bet range heavy in tens, jacks and diamond draws. I also have almost no fold equity OOP to a raise, so calling or raising is a losing long-term play. Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

With T♠ Q♠ on the button, this is a premium hand that merits aggressive play. I'm the shortest stack (7,700) facing two larger opponents, so I need to apply pressure and build pots with quality holdings. T-Q suited is broadway with flush potential and has good equity. A raise to 300 (3x BB) creates fold equity and pressure on both blinds, particularly Minni in the SB who has a wider range of holdings. This is the right time to be active with a strong hand given my stack situation. The aggressive table dynamics suggest that a solid raise here is more profitable than passively calling.

I'm in the SB with 4♣9♠ facing a button open to 300. This is a very weak, poorly playable hand — easily dominated by a button raising range and dead OOP for every postflop street. Calling costs 250 to win ~700 (about 36% equity needed), and 9-4o does not have that equity vs a typical open; facing the BB or a 3-bet would make this even worse. A shove/3-bet bluff here would be high variance with poor blockers and little postflop playability. Preserve stack for better spots — fold.

I am in the big blind with 9c 2s, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude raised to 300 from the button, and Minni folded her small blind. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), 92-offsuit has extremely poor playability and equity, especially while being out of position for the remainder of the hand. With a large chip lead, there is no reason to defend such a weak range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

Button with Q♥8♥ in a 3-handed pot — suited, playable and worth opening from position. Pot is 150 with 100 to call; standard table open size has been ~300. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) to take the initiative, get fold equity against the blinds, and try to isolate one opponent while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal but playable hand. SB (Flash) is deep and aggressive so he may defend, and BB (Claude) has a medium stack and defends reasonably often; 300 is a size that balances fold equity and postflop playability. Plan: if called use positional advantage and Q-high/hearts backdoor equity to c-bet favorable boards and control pot size; if faced with a substantial 3-bet evaluate based on sizing/stack and tend to fold unless getting correct odds or holding strong equity.

I am holding Ace-King suited, one of the strongest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has opened from the button with a 3x raise. Given my position in the small blind, I should play this aggressively to build the pot and take the initiative out of position. A 3-bet is the standard and most profitable play here. I will raise to 1050, which is approximately 3.5x Minni's open, to put pressure on the button and potentially force a fold or define ranges for the post-flop play.

This is a straightforward fold. I have 2-7o, the worst starting hand in poker, in the big blind facing a 3-bet. Flash has a significant stack advantage (20675 vs my 7750) and just 3-bet from the small blind to 1050, indicating a strong range (likely premium pairs, Broadway cards, or strong hands). Minni's initial raise suggests some strength as well. With minimal equity against Flash's likely range, poor position, and the mathematical disadvantage of committing 12% of my stack with virtually no value hand, folding is the only sensible play. I need to preserve my stack and wait for better spots.

Facing a 3-bet to 1,050 after my 300 open with Q♥8♥ on the button. Stacks are deep (my 10,125 vs his 20,675) and the min 4-bet shown is 1,800. Q8s is marginal but very playable in position: suited + queen blocker gives postflop playability and the ability to realize equity and exploit position. Calling 750 more (total committed 1,050) is cheap relative to the stacks and preserves maneuverability — 4-betting as a bluff here is too thin and folding is too tight given position and the likely mixed 3-bet range from an aggressive SB. Plan: call and see a flop; if I hit a queen or hearts or a strong draw I will apply pressure, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy turn/river aggression when not improved. If Flash shows extreme strength (large 4-bet/shove) I will re-evaluate and likely fold.

I have the nut flush draw and two overcards (Ace and King) on a 6d-Td-3h board. As the pre-flop 3-better, I have a range advantage and a significant equity lead or a strong draw against most of Minni's calling range. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 800 (about 36% of the pot) to maintain initiative, build the pot for my potential nut hand, and fold out her weaker air hands.

Call. Preflop I opened and called Flash’s 3-bet to 1,050; on the 6♦ T♦ 3♥ flop I hold Q♥8♥ — no pair, only a backdoor heart draw and a queen overcard. Flash’s bet is small (800 into a 3,000 pot ≈27% pot), so calling 800 gives ~21% immediate pot odds. In position and with deep stacks I preserve maneuverability by calling: I can see a turn and either realize equity, bluff/raise if I pick up a queen/heart or a favorable runout, or fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve. A raise here would be a thin, high-variance bluff versus a preflop 3-bettor. Plan: call now (total committed = 1,050 preflop + 800 = 1,850); if I hit a queen or a heart or get a very favorable turn I’ll apply pressure, otherwise control pot size and fold to heavy action.

I have successfully completed the nut flush on the turn with the Ace of Diamonds and King of Diamonds. The pot is currently 3800, and Minni has approximately 8575 chips remaining. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and continued on the flop, a turn bet is consistent with my story and highly likely to get value from her range, which includes sets (10-10, 6-6, 3-3), two-pair hands, or lower flushes. I will bet 1900, which is exactly half the pot, to build a larger pot for a potential river shove while ensuring I don't price out her medium-strength hands.

Call 1900. I have Q♥8♥ — second pair (board: 6♦ T♦ 3♥ 8♦) with a decent queen kicker and positional advantage. Flash has been very aggressive (preflop 3-bet, flop bet, now a turn bet), so his range includes tens/sets and two-diamond combos that already make a flush, but it also contains bluffs and single-diamond hands. Facing 1900 into a 5700 pot gives me ~25% pot odds to continue; with position and real showdown value I prefer to call and see the river rather than fold a made hand or thinly raise into possible flushes/strong pairs. Plan: call and re-evaluate on the river — fold to large shove or clear flush action if I don’t improve, but realize equity and extract value/call smaller river bets if the runout is non-threatening.

I hold the nut flush with the Ace of Diamonds and King of Diamonds on a board of 6d-Td-3h-8d-9h. Since the board is not paired and there is no 7d or 9d on the board to make a straight flush likely (Minni would need to hold exactly 7d-9d), I have the absolute best hand in almost every scenario. Minni has called significant bets on both the flop and the turn, suggesting she has a strong made hand like a set (10s, 8s, or 6s), two pair, or a lower flush. With the pot at 7600 and Minni having 6675 remaining, the most profitable move is to put her all-in to maximize value. I will bet 6675, which is slightly less than a pot-sized bet, to ensure I get her entire stack if she has any hand she is willing to call with.

Fold. The pot is 14,275 and Flash just bet 6,675; calling 6,675 to win 20,950 requires ~31.9% equity (6,675 / 20,950 ≈ 0.3186). I have Q♥8♥ — second pair (8s) with a decent queen blocker, but the line is very value‑heavy: Flash 3‑betted preflop and barreled flop → turn → river. The turn brought the third diamond (6♦ T♦ 3♥ → 8♦), so many realistic 3‑bet hands have turned into made diamond flushes, or are strong values (Tx, sets, two‑pair, straights) that beat my pair of eights. My hand only beats missed overcards and pure bluffs; I don’t block diamonds and I don’t assign enough bluff frequency to justify an all‑in call for my entire stack. Given the pot odds (~32% needed), the board texture and his line/size, calling would be a high‑variance, negative‑EV commit. I fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

K8o is a solid hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. As the chip leader, I want to continue applying pressure to Claude and Minni, especially after taking a large pot from Minni in the previous hand. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either win the blinds or see a flop with positional advantage.

Raising to 900 with AhTh from SB. This premium broadway hand with flush potential warrants a 3-bet against Flash's button raise. The play accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) isolates Flash from the short-stacked Minni who will likely fold, (2) wins the pot immediately a high percentage of the time, and (3) if called, I have a strong hand that plays well post-flop. My 7700 stack is sufficient for this aggression level without overcommitting.

Facing a cold 3-bet to 900 from the small blind after a 300 open from the button. I’m on the big blind with T♦7♣ — a marginal, often dominated hand vs a 3-bet range. To call I must put in 800 more into a 1,300 pot, which gives me ~38% pot-equity requirement. T7o rarely has that equity against a typical SB 3-bet (pairs, broadways, strong Ax), and a light 4-bet would commit a meaningful chunk of my ~6,575 stack with a weak holding and risk getting peeled or 5-bet by deeper stacks. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot in this 3-handed match — fold.

K8o is a standard opening hand from the button in a three-handed game, but it does not play well against a 3-bet. Claude's raise to 900 indicates strength, and my hand is likely dominated by his 3-betting range (which likely includes better Kings and pocket pairs). While I have the chip lead and would have position, calling 600 more to play a bloated pot with a marginal hand is unnecessary. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation to utilize my stack.

8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that lacks both connectivity and suit coordination. While I'm in an excellent position on the button with decent pot odds (1.5:1 for a 100 chip call), the hand quality is simply too poor to justify playing. At 8150 chips, I have a reasonable stack and should conserve chips for premium situations rather than getting involved with marginal holdings. Minni (6525 chips) is shorter stacked but still has chips left to play, and Flash (25175) has a commanding lead. Folding here preserves chips and allows me to maintain discipline - critical in a 150-hand tournament where stack preservation matters as much as value creation.

I’m on the small blind with 2♣4♣ (a weak, low suited connector). Pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call (to complete to 100). Folding 50 here is too tight given heads-up play and the hand’s postflop playability; a min-raise to 200 would overcommit with a marginal hand out of position and open me to 3-bets. I’ll call the extra 50 (total committed 100) to see a cheap flop. Plan: proceed cautiously out of position — fold to sizable continuation bets on dry boards, but pursue clubs/low connected boards or pairs where I can extract implied odds or barrel when appropriate.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2o and the Small Blind has just completed the blind. Q2o is a weak hand, but since the 'to call' amount is 0, I can see a flop for free. There is no strategic advantage to raising here given the hand strength and the fact that I can realize my equity without further investment.

Flop is Kh Qd Kc and I hold 2♣4♣ — no pair, essentially only a backdoor club flush (need both turn+river; ~4% to hit) and very weak pair potential that will still lose to K/Q holdings. I’m out of position vs the big blind and the board heavily favors typical calling ranges, so leading or barreling OOP with virtually no equity is a losing line. I’ll check and control the pot. Plan: fold to any meaningful bet (especially ≥ ~half-pot); only consider calling a very small probe to see a clubed turn, otherwise check-fold.

I have middle pair (Queens) on a board that is heavily weighted towards Kings. In a limped pot, Minni's check on the flop [Kh Qd Kc] could be a trap with a King or just a weak hand. By checking back, I control the pot size and maintain my equity without overcommitting with a hand that is second-best to any King or a better Queen. I have plenty of showdown value and can evaluate the turn based on her action.

I hold 2♣4♣ on K♥ Q♦ K♣ 7♥. My backdoor club draw was only viable on the flop (needed both turn+river) and it missed on the turn, so I have no pair, no real draws and essentially no showdown equity vs a reasonable calling range (Kx, Qx, pocket pairs). Villain has checked flop and turn — could be weakness but could also be slowplay. Betting out of position with virtually no equity is a negative-EV bluff that just builds a pot I can't realistically win; better to preserve stack and see the river. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful bet; if checked to on the river I'll re-evaluate then for a small bluff only on a very favorable card.

On this board of Kh-Qd-Kc-7h, I hold a Queen, giving me two pair (Kings and Queens) with a 7 kicker. This is a strong medium-strength hand. Minni has checked twice, suggesting she likely doesn't have a King or is playing very cautiously. In a small, limped pot, there is little incentive to bet and risk a check-raise or build a pot that I might lose on the river. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my showdown value for free, as my hand is unlikely to get three streets of value from worse hands.

Board: K♥ Q♦ K♣ 7♥ 9♣. I hold 2♣4♣ — no pair, my backdoor club draw missed. Villain checked flop and turn, which is consistent with either weakness or a slow-played K; the river 9♣ doesn’t meaningfully improve my hand or give a credible story to represent a K. The pot is only 200 while my stack is 6,475, so risking chips with a bluff here is low EV — many hands in the opponent’s checking range (Kx, Qx, mid pairs) will call a small bet and I have virtually no showdown equity. I’ll conserve chips and check through; if I face a meaningful bet I’ll fold.

I have two pair (Kings and Queens). Given the board has a pair of Kings and I hold a Queen, I have a solid hand but it is vulnerable to any King. Minni has checked three streets in a limped pot. There is very little value to be gained by betting here, as most hands that call would likely beat me (like a better Queen or a slow-played King), and hands that are worse will simply fold. Checking back ensures I reach showdown and win the pot against her likely weak range.

I'm on the button (last to act postflop) with J♥6♣ — a marginal hand, but in a 3-handed game I should open a fairly wide range. Blinds are 50/100 and the pot is just the blinds (150). Opponents: Flash (SB) is deep and can be sticky/aggressive; Claude (BB) is mid/deep and often calls. A standard 3x-ish open here both matches table tendencies and gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. I will raise to 300 total to seize the initiative and try to steal the blinds. Plan if called: c‑bet favorable flops, proceed cautiously on coordinated boards, and fold to strong 3‑bet/shove lines with this holding.

I am the chip leader with a significant margin. 8-9 suited is a strong speculative hand in a 3-handed game and flops very well. The pot odds are favorable for a call, as I only need to commit 250 more chips to a 450-chip pot. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the strength of the hand and my stack size justify seeing a flop to look for draws or two-pair/trips potential.

5-6 unsuited is one of the worst starting hands in poker with virtually no equity. While pot odds are 3.5:1, the hand quality is too weak to justify calling. Multi-way pot against two opponents with Flash having a huge stack means limited fold equity if I miss. This is a straightforward fold to preserve chips for stronger spots.

On this board of 7s-Th-8s, I have flopped middle pair (pair of eights) with an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or Jack gives me a straight). This is a very strong flop for my 9-8 hand. Since I am out of position against Minni, a check is the standard move to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts. If Minni bets, I will likely call or potentially check-raise depending on the bet size, given my pair plus high-equity draw. For now, checking is the best way to control the pot while retaining the flexibility to realize my equity.

Flop is 7♠ 10♥ 8♠ — a very coordinated, two‑spade board. I opened wide preflop, so my range contains lots of tens, pairs and spade combos; my actual holding J♥6♣ is marginal (only a gutshot to a 9 and a few pair‑outs, no spade). Flash (SB) is deep and sticky, so a c‑bet here will often be called by better hands and by draws and can bloat a pot I’m unlikely to dominate. I’m in position, so I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than fire a bluff into a wet texture. Plan: check and see how Flash reacts — call small/medium bets to realize equity, fold to large check‑raises, and re‑evaluate for a delayed c‑bet on a safe turn or check down if we miss.

I have improved to trips (three eights) on the turn. On the flop, I checked my middle pair and open-ended straight draw, and Minni checked back, which often indicates a marginal hand or a hand that has given up. By checking again on the turn, I am employing a 'trap' line. Since the board is somewhat coordinated (straight and flush draws are possible), I want to induce Minni to bet with her bluffs or to value-bet a Ten or a Seven. If I lead out now, I might fold out her air, whereas checking allows her to commit more chips to the pot. I will look to either check-call to keep her range wide or check-raise if she uses a substantial sizing.

Board is 7♠ 10♥ 8♠ 8♦, I hold J♥6♣. I have no pair and no meaningful draw on this paired, fairly connected board. Flash checked both flop and turn; his calling preflop means his range likely contains tens, small pairs, floats and some connector combos — hands that will often call a turn bluff. With deep stacks and poor fold equity here I want pot control and to realize equity in position rather than bloating the pot with air. I’ll check and re-evaluate on the river: call small bets to see a showdown, fold to large turn/river aggression, and only pursue a bluff on a very favorable river runout or specific read.

I have trips (three eights) with a nine kicker. The board is relatively coordinated, but Minni has checked behind on both the flop and the turn after raising pre-flop. This suggests she likely has a marginal hand like a weak pair (Sevens or a low pocket pair) or perhaps a Ten that she is playing cautiously, or she has completely missed. Since I have checked twice, leading out now on the river is the best way to extract value. If I check again, she is likely to check behind with her marginal showdown value hands. A bet of around 65% of the pot (450 chips) is a good sizing to get called by a Ten or a Seven while not appearing so large as to scare off weaker hands.

I have J♥6♣ on 7♠ 10♥ 8♠ 8♦ 3♦ — no pair, no spade, no meaningful showdown value. Flash (SB) checked flop and turn and now bets 450 into what was ~700 (bet ≈64% pot). The pot is 1150 and calling 450 would make the final pot ~1600, so I am getting about 28% equity to break even. Flash called my preflop open and plays sticky; his passive line skews toward thin value (T‑x, small pairs, occasional slowplays) but he will have missed draws and some bluffs too. I have almost no fold equity so a raise would be a pure bluff with low EV, while folding concedes the pot for certain. Calling 450 is a small portion of my 6175 stack (~7%) and allows me to catch bluffs and realize equity against marginal hands, so I will call to see showdown.

I am on the button with a massive chip lead. In a three-handed game, my opening range from the button should be quite wide to put pressure on the smaller stacks in the blinds. Queen-Five suited is a speculative but playable hand in this position. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either take the blinds down immediately or play a pot in position against Claude or Minni.

I'm in the small blind with 3d-5d, one of the weakest possible holdings in poker. Flash has raised to 300 from the button, and I need to call 250 more. This is a straightforward fold for several reasons: (1) 3d-5d has virtually zero equity against a button raise - it's essentially trash with no pair, no high cards, and poor kicker potential; (2) I'm out of position postflop, which compounds the weakness of my hand; (3) Flash has shown aggressive raising patterns throughout this session, but that doesn't change the fact that I have minimal hand strength; (4) At 8000 chips (40bb), I should be selective with my marginal spots and wait for better opportunities. Folding weak hands when facing aggression is fundamental poker strategy.

I’m in the BB facing a 3x open to 300 from the button with J♦8♠ (offsuit). My stack is ~56bb so I can play postflop, but J8o is a marginal hand OOP. The open is small and the button’s range is likely wide, so calling is the lowest-variance +EV play: pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (300 total), giving me reasonable pot odds (~23% break-even). 3-betting OOP with this hand is thin and invites 4-bets or large pots I don’t want to play out of position. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and proceed aggressively only if I connect or get a favorable read — otherwise fold to heavy pressure.

I hold J♦8♠ on Q♠9♠10♥ — I’ve made an 8–9–10–J–Q straight. This is a very strong, made hand vs the button’s wide opening range; the only realistic hand that currently beats me is KJ. The board has two spades, so there are spade draws in his range I want to charge. I’ll bet for value and protection rather than check and give free cards. A ~1/2 pot lead should extract calls from Qx/Tx/pocket pairs and make spade draws pay to continue. Bet 350 into 650; if he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (likely KJ or a strong trap), if he calls I’ll pot-control and be cautious on a King turn.

I have hit top pair on a very coordinated board (Qs 9s Th). Minni's lead-out bet of 350 into a pot of 650 could signify a wide range of hands, including top pair with a better kicker, a straight draw, or even a made straight (though less likely to lead into the pre-flop raiser with the nuts). With top pair and a weak kicker, I am in a 'way ahead or way behind' situation or facing a high-equity draw. Folding would be too tight given I have the best pair, but raising might isolate me against hands that have me crushed. Calling is the best option to keep the pot controlled, maintain position, and see how the turn develops.

On the flop I had a strong made straight and led for value. The turn (Qh) pairs the board and substantially changes the hand range dynamics: many hands that would have called the flop (Qx/T9/TQ/99, etc.) now have full houses or even quads, and KJ still remains the one straight that dominates me. I’m out of position and don’t want to build a bigger pot with a hand that’s now vulnerable to several realistic river / turn monsters. Pot-control is the prudent play — check and let the button show his hand. If he fires a small bet I can call (bluffs and weaker pairs still in his range); if he barrels big I’ll fold to avoid being priced in against full houses/quads/KJ.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Queen of Hearts, giving me three of a kind. However, my kicker (5) is extremely weak. Minni led out on the flop but has now checked the turn. This board is very coordinated (Qs 9s Th Qh), meaning straights (KJ, J8) and better Queens are very possible. By checking back, I control the pot size and avoid a potential check-raise from a hand that has me beat, while still being able to see the river and potentially reach showdown with a very strong absolute hand.

I check. On the flop my J♦8♠ made a strong straight (8-9-10-J-Q) and I bet for value; Flash called. The turn Q♠→Q♥ paired the board and materially changed ranges: QQ (quads), pocket 10s/9s (sets that became full houses), and AQ (which becomes QQQAA on the river) now all beat my straight, and KJ was already a higher straight on the flop. Flash’s line (called flop, checked turn) can include both slow-played monsters and a lot of weaker holdings and missed spade draws. The river A♠ improves some of his holdings to full houses but also gives Ax hands a single pair that I still beat. Betting here would risk building a bigger pot that I’m dominated in when villain holds those rare but realistic monsters; checking controls the pot and lets villain show bluffs or value. Plan: check to showdown; if he bets a small blocker/bluff size I’ll call (we beat many one-pair/missed-draw hands), but I’ll fold to a large value shove consistent with full houses/quads/higher straights.

On the river, the board has become extremely coordinated with the Ace of Spades completing both a potential flush and various straight possibilities (KJ, J8). While I have Three of a Kind (Queens), my kicker (5) is very weak. Minni led the flop but has since checked the turn and the river. This sequence suggests she might have a marginal hand like a pair of Tens or Nines, or perhaps a missed draw. However, given how many hands beat me on this board (any King-Jack, any two spades, better Queens, or full houses), there is very little value in betting. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I risk being check-raised by a superior hand. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and reach showdown safely in a pot where I likely have the best hand, but cannot comfortably bet for value.

From the button with Q7o, I raise to 300 to apply pressure and steal the blinds. Position is my biggest asset here, and a 3x raise from the button is a standard aggression play. Even if called, I maintain position advantage postflop. The risk-reward is favorable - I likely win the blinds immediately or play postflop in position with equity.

2♥9♥ is a very weak, low-suited connector and I'm in the worst position (small blind) facing a standard 3x button open to 300. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~35.7% equity to break even — far more than 2♥9♥ realistically has versus a button raising range (and likely a defending big blind). A 3-bet with this holding is irresponsible and calling risks playing a difficult out-of-position, potentially multiway pot with very poor implied odds. Preserve the 6,375 stack for better spots — fold.

I am in the big blind with 6c 4h. The button (Claude) has raised to 300. Minni in the small blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650. While the pot odds are roughly 3.25:1, 6-4 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given my large stack and the fact that I want to preserve it for better opportunities, I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

On the button with Kh6d and ~63 BB effective. In a 3-handed pot my opening range should be wide — K6o is marginal but gains value from position and fold equity. A small standard open-raise to the min (200 total) tries to steal the blinds and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (don’t gamble with a dominated Kx), but if called use position to c-bet favorable K/low/clean boards or check/control on wet, connected boards and take a cheap showdown. Raise to 200 to apply pressure while preserving stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 3d, a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the Button to 200. Although the price to call is relatively low (150 more to win a total pot of 500), 63o performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against both the Button and the Big Blind. Given my substantial chip lead and the need to preserve my stack for better opportunities, folding is the most prudent action.

I have pocket Kings, one of the premium starting hands in poker. Minni raised to 200 from the button and Flash folded from the small blind. With KK in the big blind, I should be re-raising to build value and assert control with this premium holding. A raise to 550 (3x-4x the original raise) applies appropriate pressure, builds the pot, and is well within my 8050 chip stack. This is a standard play for a premium hand in this situation - I want to get value and control the action rather than just call. Minni will need a strong hand or premium range to continue against this 3-bet.

Claude 3-bet to 550 after my min-open to 200. With Kh6d and ~61BB effective this is a marginal hand but I have the button (positional advantage) and the 3-bet size is a medium one — not huge enough to justify folding automatically and not strong enough to 4‑bet light. I'll call (350 more, total 550) to keep the pot manageable and play the flop in position. Pot math: current pot 800, calling makes it ~1,150 and leaves me ~5,825 behind. Postflop plan: continue with top pair or strong draws, use position to float on dry boards and apply pressure; check/control and fold to heavy aggression on wet, connected boards or when clearly dominated. I will fold to any large 4-bet shove that commits a substantial portion of my stack.

With pocket Kings on a relatively dry flop (7d 4h Jd), I'm making a standard continuation bet. My KK is ahead of Minni's likely pre-flop calling range, and I want to build value while maintaining the aggression I established pre-flop. A 2/3 pot-sized bet of 750 chips is standard and keeps pressure on her holdings while I remain deep-stacked relative to remaining action.

Claude 3-bet preflop so his range is weighted to value (QQ+/AK/AQ), but his flop c-bet of 750 into a 1,900 pot is only a medium-size probe and can include bluffs. I have K♥6♦ on J♦7♦4♥: no pair, but a backdoor diamond draw and a King overcard. Calling 750 into the 1,900 pot requires ~28% equity — a cheap price to see a turn with positional advantage. Raising here would be a thin bluff vs a likely value-heavy 3-bet range and could get me too committed if Claude shows more strength; folding is too tight given the pot odds and the option to use position post‑flop. Plan: call to float and re-evaluate on the turn — continue aggressively only if I pair the K/6 or pick up meaningful diamond equity, otherwise check/control and fold to heavy aggression.

Betting 1500 on the turn with KK is the correct play. The 3c is a blank that doesn't improve Minni's likely range significantly. My hand is among the strongest in poker and the board is favorable. By betting roughly 55% of the pot, I apply substantial pressure on her shorter stack (5075 remaining) while maintaining control of the hand. This aggressive action is consistent with my pre-flop 3-bet and flop continuation bet. If she has a marginal hand, this bet forces her to make a tough decision with a significant portion of her stack. If she shoves, I'm well-positioned to call given my hand strength and the pot odds.

Fold. Claude (the preflop 3‑better) has shown steady aggression — c‑bet on the flop and another barrel on the turn — so his range is value‑heavy (QQ+/AK/AQ/AJ, some sets, occasional bluffs). Pot is 4,150 and he’s betting 1,500; to call I need ~26.5% equity (1,500/(4,150+1,500)). With K♥6♦ I only have about 6 outs (3 kings + 3 sixes) ≈ 13% raw chance to improve on the river, and many of those outs are not clean versus his range (AK/Kx/Ax combos). A raise to 3,000 is a thin bluff with limited fold equity against a 3‑bettor and risks committing a large chunk of my ~50BB stack. Math + opponent profile => negative EV to call or bluff‑raise. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button with King-Six suited (Kd 6d), which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is significantly larger than my opponents, and I want to apply pressure and take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to either win the blinds or play a pot in position.

AK is a premium non-pair hand with excellent equity preflop. In the small blind facing a button raise, I should apply pressure with this holding rather than play passively. A 3x reraise to 900 takes initiative, forces Flash to make a decision with his range, and defines my strength. I have sufficient stack depth (9,450) to make this play without overcommitting. This also prevents Minni from squeezing with a short stack from the big blind.

I’m in the big blind with 5♦6♠ facing a button open to 300 and a strong SB 3‑bet to 900. Pot is 1,300 and I must call 800 (i.e. total 900) — that gives me ~1.625:1 pot odds, so I need roughly 38–39% equity to break even immediately. Versus an SB 3‑bet range and the original BTN raiser in a 3‑way pot my offsuit 5‑6 has well under that equity and I’ll be out of position postflop. A small 4‑bet to 1,500 would be thin and a shove is too large a gamble with this hand. Preserve my ~49BB stack and fold to avoid a low‑EV, difficult multiway spot.

I started the action with a raise to 300 on the button with King-Six suited, a respectable hand in a three-handed game. Claude has 3-bet to 900 from the small blind. While Claude has shown aggression in previous hands, I have the advantage of position for the remainder of the hand and the pot odds are attractive (calling 600 to win a 1300 pot). My stack is large enough to absorb the call and see a flop, allowing me to evaluate the situation further based on the board texture and Claude's subsequent actions.

Betting AK on a dry flop after 3-betting preflop. This hand has excellent equity against Flash's calling range and can win immediately or stack with good equity. The sizing (1200) applies pressure while managing my stack appropriately. The dry 5c 2c 9s board favors aggression from the preflop aggressor.

Claude's pre-flop 3-bet and significant continuation bet on the flop indicate a strong range, likely consisting of overpairs (AA-TT) or strong Ace-high hands. My King-high (Kd 6d) has very little equity on this board (5c 2c 9s) as I have no pair and no real draw. While I have position, floating against a 3-bettor who is showing aggression on a dry board with no backdoor equity is unprofitable. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my chip lead.

5-10 offsuit is too weak to justify a call even from button position in a 3-handed game. While position is valuable, this hand lacks sufficient equity and playability against both opponents. Minni's short stack increases unpredictability, and Flash's deep stack gives him additional leverage. With a reasonable chip stack, folding weak hands remains the disciplined approach.

Heads-up preflop vs the big blind with 4♦9♠ offsuit — a marginal hand but very cheap to see a flop. Pot is 150 and I only need to put 50 more (my total commitment after calling will be 100), so the pot odds are attractive (~3:1). With ~49bb effective I don’t want to bloat the pot with a weak offsuit hand by raising to 200 and face a 3-bet or larger pot OOP. I'll complete to see a flop and play cautiously out of position: fold to heavy aggression on dry flops, continue with any pair or strong draw.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo. The Button has folded and the Small Blind (Minni) has simply completed the bet. Since I have a strong chip lead and QJo is a solid hand that will have position on the Small Blind post-flop, I could consider raising to take the pot now. However, checking allows me to see a flop for free with a hand that has good high-card value and straight potential. Given the tournament context and my current stack size, I will check and see the flop without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

I have 9♠4♦ on a paired 2♥6♥2♠ flop: no pair, no heart backdoor, no meaningful equity. I'm out of position and the pot is small relative to stacks. The board is unfavorable for bluffing (paired and two hearts), so I shouldn't bloat the pot or lead out. Best line is pot control — check and preserve chips. I'll fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if I pick up a pair or get a very cheap/free card to turn.

In a limped pot with a board of 2h-6h-2s, I hold Q-high. Minni checked from the Small Blind. While I likely have the best hand against a wide limping range, there is little value in betting here as I am unlikely to get called by worse, and a check allows me to see a free turn card while maintaining pot control. I will check behind and see how the board develops.

I have 9♠4♦ on 2♥6♥2♠K♠ — no pair, no hearts, no meaningful equity and I'm out of position. The flop was checked by both players and the turn King is a card that helps typical calling ranges (Kx, overcards that pair, pocket pairs) more than it helps me. Pot is small (200) relative to stacks (~49bb). Betting OOP here would be thin — likely called by better hands and rarely folds out enough equity to justify bloat. Best play is pot control: check and preserve chips. If Flash bets the turn I will fold to meaningful pressure since I have no showdown value or fold equity.

The board is 2h-6h-2s-Ks. I have QJ offsuit (Queen high). Minni checked the turn after also checking the flop in this limped pot. While the King is a good card to represent, Q-high still has some showdown value against her air hands, and checking allows me to see the river for free without risking a check-raise in a tiny pot. I'll check behind and see the final card.

I paired my 4 on the river (board 2h 6h 2s K s 4h), giving me a weak bottom pair (4♦9♠). Heads‑up and acting last after two passive checks, Flash's range is likely heavy in Kx/overcards and missed broadways plus some small pocket pairs — many of those hands will call a small river bet. A small value bet extracts calls from worse (Qx, Jx, Tx, small pairs, missed draws) while keeping risk limited if I’m up against Kx or a 2/6. I’ll bet half‑pot (100) to buy the showdown cheaply and get value; if I face a raise I’ll fold (he very likely has a stronger hand).

The pot is small (300 total after the bet) and I am holding only Queen-high. Minni limped from the Small Blind and checked the flop and turn before leading for 100 on the river. While the bet is small, Queen-high has almost no showdown value; I lose to any pair, any Ace-high, and even many hands that might have limped in. Since I do not have a pair or even an Ace to contest the pot, and the board has multiple straight and flush possibilities that could have been backed into, a call is not profitable. Folding preserves my significant stack lead.

I’m on the button with A♣Q♠ — a very strong, standard open-raising hand. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call; with two players in the blinds and ~50BB effective I want to take the initiative and use position. A standard 3x-ish open is balanced: enough fold equity to pick up the blinds often, but keeps post‑flop stacks comfortable against 3-bets. If I face a moderate 3-bet I can either call and play in position (against an aggressive SB) or 4-bet vs a tighter 3-bettor; I’ll fold to an unreasonably large shove if the price is wrong. Raise to 300 total (adds 200).

I am holding 9h 5s in the Small Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a very weak starting hand and playing it out of position against the button raise is not profitable in the long run. Given my substantial chip lead, there is no reason to defend with trash hands. I will fold.

With Tc7s in the big blind facing Minni's button raise, I need to evaluate this marginal situation carefully. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (450 to call 200), Tc7o is a weak hand with limited upside. Minni has been aggressive from the button, but calling here with such a weak kicker puts me in difficult post-flop situations. At 93 of 150 hands with 57 hands remaining, I should be selective and preserve my 10,400 stack for stronger holdings where I have better equity. Folding is the prudent play to maintain chip efficiency in the closing stages of the tournament.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. I have a significant chip lead over both Claude and Minni. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early.

I'm holding 9d4s in the small blind against Flash's button raise to 300. This is a very weak hand with no premium characteristics - not a strong pair, not broadway cards, not a connector. 9-4 offsuit is one of the weakest holdings in poker. While calling 250 to see the flop would give me pot odds, this hand has poor showdown value and would be difficult to play profitably from out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flash. With 10,350 chips and healthy stack depth, I can afford to be selective with my hands. Folding preserves chips for better situations.

Facing a button open to 300 from the button with Qh3c in the big blind (I have 5,125 ≈ 51 BB), this is a marginal hand but the open is likely wide. Calling 200 more into a 450 pot gives ~30% pot odds — reasonable to defend one street against a wide button range. I don’t want to 3-bet with Q3o (poor playability, many dominated/Q-broadway callers, and out of position postflop), and I won’t shove given I can pick better spots. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, continue/raise only with top pair or good draws. Preserve stack and extract value when I actually connect.

I have top pair (Qh) with a very weak 3 kicker on Qd‑5d‑Th. I’m out of position to the button who opened wide preflop, and the board has two diamonds and a ten — there are both flush and tens that want to see a free card. Betting protects my hand and extracts value from worse queens, marginal tens, and other calling hands while charging diamond draws. I’ll make a standard protection/value bet of about half the pot (~325). If Flash raises big I’ll be prepared to fold (my kicker is too weak to comfortably call large aggression); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate.

On a flop of 5d-Qd-Th, Minni has led out into me (the pre-flop aggressor) with a 'donk bet' of 325. This board texture (Q-T-x) hits a Big Blind's calling range quite well, containing many pairs and straight draws. While I have Ace-high and the 7 of diamonds for a backdoor flush draw, I don't have enough equity to justify a call or a raise against a lead, especially since Minni is relatively short-stacked and might be looking to commit. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to use my chip lead.

Position: Button | Hand: 2d-Jc (deuce-jack offsuit)

This is a clear fold situation. While the button is an excellent stealing position, 2-Joff is well below the threshold for profitable play even in late position. 

Analysis:
- 2-Jack offsuit ranks in the bottom 10% of starting hands
- Against Minni's ~40% calling range (given his short 5,525 stack) and Flash's defensive BB range, this hand has insufficient equity
- Pot odds are 150:100 (1.5:1), requiring ~40% equity, but 2-Joff has only ~30-35% equity against two random hands
- The risk of flopping poorly with this weak holding and facing aggression from either opponent makes this a losing proposition
- At hand 96 of 150, I should focus on building my stack with stronger holdings, especially with Minni's short stack creating pressure situations

This is a straightforward fold - preserve chips for premium hands where I have genuine strength.

K♣8♥ in the small blind vs only the big blind: calling 50 to complete leaves me OOP with no fold equity. I prefer to take the initiative and use a standard 3BB open to steal the blind and define the pot. With ~55BB effective, raising to 300 is a comfortable, consistent sizing that balances fold equity and pot control. Plan: if Flash folds I take the blinds; if he calls I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and play cautiously out of position; if he 3‑bets large I’ll fold K8o to avoid getting dominated.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 5c. Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While defending the Big Blind against a Small Blind open can be done with a wide range in 3-handed play, 95o is near the bottom of any reasonable defending range. Given that I am out of position and the hand has very poor playability and equity, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

Button with A♥7♥ in a three-handed game: this is a clear opening hand. I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds and use my positional advantage and suited Ace equity. Standard open in this field has been ~3x the big blind (and I’ve been opening to 300 a lot), so I’ll raise to 300 to get fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with 56BB effective. Plan: if called I’ll play aggressively in position (bet good A-highs, continue with strong draws); if I face a small 3-bet I can call and realize equity, but I’ll respect a large 3/4-bet and fold to avoid committing with a marginal ace.

I am holding Jack of Spades and Trey of Diamonds (J3o) in the Small Blind. Minni has raised from the Button. J3o is a very weak hand to play out of position, and even with my significant chip lead, there is no strategic advantage to calling or 3-betting here. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Calling 200 more (total 300 to match Minni's raise). 7-J offsuit has reasonable equity (~35-40%) against Minni's button raise range, and pot odds of 2.25:1 support the call. While out of position post-flop is a disadvantage, the relatively short stack dynamics and ability to fold weak flops make this a defensible spot. I'll take a flop and leverage my position strength and fold equity if I completely miss.

Minni has checked to me on a flop of 8h-Ad-Kh. My 7d-Jc is a weak unimproved hand with only 6 outs to a pair. I'm out of position and need to control pot size given my short stack. Checking is the optimal play here - it allows me to see another card for free, avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand, and maintain fold equity if Minni turns on the aggression. Given the weak board texture for my hand and Minni's button-raise range likely containing premium holdings, checking is defensive and correct.

Flop: 8h Ad Kh. I hold Ah7h — top pair (ace) plus the nut heart flush draw. Claude checked after calling preflop, which is consistent with a wide calling range (weak kings, medium pairs, some Axs, or speculative hands). This is a clear spot to take the initiative: betting charges hands that have equity against me, gets value from worse aces and medium pairs, and builds the pot when I have ~huge equity with both pair and a 9-card heart draw. I’ll bet ~60% pot to extract value and deny correct odds to many of Claude’s draws while keeping the pot manageable against his bigger stacks. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue on favorable cards (heart/ace); I’ll call a modest check‑raise with my combined made hand + draw but fold only to an extreme overbet that represents a very narrow, heavy value range.

Facing a 400 chip flop raise with 7d-Jc on 8h-Ad-Kh. My hand has only ~15-18% equity (gutshot draw) and needs ~22% to justify calling 3.625:1 pot odds. Out of position with a weak hand in a growing pot against an aggressive opponent. This is a clear fold - preserving chips for better spots is the correct play.

I am on the button with Q7o. While this is a borderline opening hand in 3-handed play, both Claude and Minni have shown a willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively post-flop (as seen in recent hands like #95 and #97). Given my significant chip lead, I can afford to play a tighter, more selective range to avoid unnecessary variance against shorter stacks that might be looking for a spot to shove or check-raise. Q7o doesn't play particularly well against 3-bets or on many flop textures, so I will opt for a disciplined fold.

Pocket nines is a premium hand heads-up against a short-stacked opponent. Flash has folded, leaving just me and Minni in the blind. With my 10,000 stack versus Minni's 5,925, I should apply pressure. Raising to 250 (2.5x raise) builds the pot with a strong hand, takes the initiative, and puts Minni in an uncomfortable spot given their short stack. This is both value-extracting and strategically sound.

I’m in the big blind with K♠3♥ facing Claude’s small-blind open to 250. K3o is a very weak, often-dominated holding with poor postflop playability (only one live spade and a terrible kicker). Calling 150 for pot odds (~3.3:1, ~23% equity needed) is borderline, but versus a standard SB opening range my effective equity and ability to realize it OOP are low. 3-betting as a bluff into the SB carries unnecessary risk — I don’t have good blockers and would be OOP with 59bb effective. Preserve the stack and fold to wait for a better, playable spot.

5d-9d is a weak, unsuited hand that plays poorly post-flop. While I'm in position on the button and getting 2:1 odds (100 to call into 150), this hand lacks the strength needed to profitably enter against both blinds. Minni is short-stacked at 5,875 and may shove, while Flash is the chip leader at 23,825. I should preserve my 10,150 stack for premium holdings and better spots where I have positional and card strength advantages. Folding marginal hands from the button when facing two opponents is disciplined poker strategy in tournament play.

I have T♣2♠ in the small blind — a very weak, easily dominated hand. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 more to see a flop (total commitment 100), giving roughly 3:1 immediate pot odds. Raising here to try to steal is unnecessary and risky versus Flash’s large, capable stack; I don’t want to build a bigger pot out of position with trash. I’ll complete the blind and see a flop cheaply; if I don’t connect or pick up a strong draw I’ll fold to significant aggression on later streets.

I am in the big blind holding King-Six offsuit. The button has folded, and the small blind (Minni) has limped in. I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise to try and take the pot immediately. Given my stack size and the fact that I will have position on Minni throughout the hand, checking is the most prudent play to keep the pot small with a mediocre hand while still being able to evaluate the board on the flop.

I missed this flop entirely with T♦2♠. The board is paired (8♦ K♠ 8♣) — no straight or meaningful flush draw for me (only a backdoor spade) and very little clean equity vs a reasonable checking/continuation range. I’m out of position to Flash (big stack) and there’s no reason to build the pot bluffing OOP with almost no outs. Best play is pot control: check and concede to significant aggression. If Flash leads a small probe I can consider a fold/call decision on pot odds, but I’ll generally fold to a sizable c-bet and re-evaluate if he checks back.

I have flopped top pair with a King on a paired board of Eights. In a limped pot from the small blind, my hand is currently very strong. Minni checked, which likely indicates a weak range or a speculative hand. I will place a small value bet of 100 (half the pot) to get value from weaker pairs or draws while keeping the pot manageable in case she is slow-playing a trip Eight.

I checked the flop OOP with T♦2♠ and missed completely on 8♦ K♠ 8♣. I have essentially no real draws (one spade in hand and one on board can’t make a flush; only 6 clean outs to pair a Ten or Deuce). Chance to hit by the river ≈ 24%, while calling 100 into the ~400 pot requires ≈25% equity to break even. Even if I pair, I’m often still behind Kx or a better pair and I’ll face tough decisions out of position. A bluff-raise here is too risky vs Flash’s deep stack. Preserve my stack and fold.

Button vs only blinds, pot 150, to-call 100 with 9♥7♣. In 3-handed play the button should open wide — 9‑7o is marginal but playable in position and a raise gains fold equity and initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (3xBB-style sizing common at this table) to steal the blinds or isolate heads-up; if I face a sizable 3-bet from Flash I’ll fold this hand, but I’ll continue postflop if called and can use position to realize equity or apply pressure.

I am holding pocket sixes in the small blind. Minni, on the button, has been quite active with pre-flop raises. In a three-handed game, pocket sixes are a strong hand but can be difficult to play out of position if I just call and allow the big blind to join. By raising to 1000 (a 3-bet), I'm exerting pressure on Minni and attempting to isolate the pot or win it immediately. Minni's stack is approximately 55 big blinds, so this raise also forces her to decide if she wants to commit a significant portion of her chips to see a flop. This aggressive line is consistent with my overall strategy of leveraging my chip lead.

Folding 8c-Tc from the big blind facing a 3-bet to 1,000. This is a weak hand that doesn't warrant playing against two opponents showing strength. Minni's initial raise to 300 and Flash's re-raise to 1,000 indicate reasonable hands. 8-10 offsuit has poor equity in a multi-way pot, especially out of position in the big blind. With my current stack of 10,050, I should preserve chips for premium hands and better spots. This is a straightforward fold.

I opened to 300 as button with 9♥7♣, but Flash's sizable 3‑bet to 1000 changes the spot. The pot is 1400 and calling 700 only gets me to a 2100 pot, which requires ~33% equity — 9‑7o will be a large underdog against a standard 3‑bet range from this opponent and I have poor implied odds and potential reverse implied odds. A 4‑bet shove or reraise to 1700 is too ambitious with this holding and stack depth. This is exactly the situation I planned to fold to, so I’ll fold and preserve my 5525 for better position-based spots.

I am on the button with a very weak starting hand (9d 4c). Although button strategy generally encourages opening wide to steal the blinds, 94 offsuit is significantly below the threshold of even a loose opening range. Given the current stack sizes and the fact that I am the chip leader, there is no reason to risk chips with a bottom-tier hand when more favorable spots will arise.

2-5 offsuit is the worst hand in poker. While only 50 chips costs 0.5% of stack and gives 3:1 pot odds, the hand's terrible equity and poor post-flop playability make it a clear fold. With 50 hands remaining and a healthy stack, better spots will emerge. Preservation of chips is critical at this stage.

Folding 3-Jack offsuit from the button. While position is valuable, this hand is too weak to warrant the 100-chip investment. The hand has minimal equity and poor showdown value. At this stage of the tournament (hand 102 of 150), I should maintain discipline and wait for stronger holdings. Preserving chips for better spots is the correct long-term strategy.

I’m the small blind facing only the big blind with K♠9♠ and ~55bb effective. This is a strong, playable heads-up hand with good blockers and postflop playability. Limping (calling 50) surrenders initiative and gives the BB good odds to see a cheap flop; folding is too weak. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure, and narrow Flash’s calling range, so I’ll open-raise. I size to 300 (3x BB) — a standard sizing at this table that buys fold equity, discourages marginal calls/floaters, and still leaves room to maneuver if I face a 3-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 6d, which is a strong suited hand to defend against a Small Blind raise. Minni raised to 3x, and I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call. Additionally, I will have position throughout the rest of the hand. Calling allows me to realize the equity of my hand without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a relatively short stack.

I continue my preflop aggression with a small continuation bet. K♠9♠ on T♣6♣2♠ has a single overcard (K) and only a weak backdoor spade possibility; the flop is fairly dry and misses a large portion of the BB’s calling range. A ~1/3‑pot c-bet (200) pressures unmade hands, preserves fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable with ~55bb effective. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue when I pick up equity or a safe card, pot-control or fold to heavy resistance when appropriate).

On a flop of Tc-6c-2s, I have middle pair (Sixes) with an 8 kicker. Minni leads for 200 into a pot of 600, which is a small 1/3 pot continuation bet. I am getting 4:1 odds to call and I have position for the remainder of the hand. Raising here would likely only be called by better hands (a Ten or higher pocket pair) or strong draws, whereas calling allows Minni to continue bluffing with weaker ranges or allows me to see a turn cheaply and evaluate. Given the board texture and my position, a call is the most standard and effective play.

Turn Kc gives me top pair (K9) and the pot is 1,000 with ~50bb effective. Flash’s flop-call range contains club draws, tens and medium pairs; with three clubs on board the main fear is two-club combos that already have a flush, but those are relatively few. I should continue for value and protection — charge single-club draws and get called by worse Kx/Tx/small pairs — so I’ll make a sizable turn bet of ~60% pot. Bet 600 denies correct odds to many draws, extracts thin value, and keeps the pot manageable. If Flash check-raises big I’ll fold (range strongly weighted to made flushes); if he just calls I’ll re-evaluate the river and value-bet safe cards or pot-control on club rivers.

The King of Clubs is a highly unfavorable turn card for my hand. It completes a possible flush and represents a second overcard to my pair of sixes. Minni has increased her betting frequency and sizing, leading for 60% of the pot after a small flop continuation bet. Given that Minni is playing a relatively short stack, this turn bet indicates significant strength or a committed semi-bluff that has improved. My third pair has very little showdown value on this board and almost no clean outs to improve.
