Continuing would likely require calling a shove on the river, so folding now is the best way to preserve my stack.

I’m on the Button with J♦T♥ in a 3-way hand (SB Flash deep, BB Claude ~99bb). JTo is a very playable, positionally advantaged hand — good to open and take initiative. With stacks near 60bb I want to apply pressure and avoid multiway limps, so I’ll open-raise to a standard ~3bb size. 300 is consistent with table tendencies and gives fold equity versus the blinds while keeping postflop playability if called. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet (or reassess vs sizing/actor), call small defense and play aggressively in position (c‑bet favorable flops, realize equity on connected boards).

I am holding J5 offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against a Button raise. Minni has been relatively active, but J5o does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet. As the chip leader, there is no reason to get involved in marginal spots with subpar holdings.

Folding 5-2o (the worst starting hand in poker) is a straightforward decision. I'm in the Big Blind facing a raise from Minni on the button. While the pot is decent at 450, I only need to call 200 more, but this hand has virtually no winning potential. With a healthy stack of 9,900 chips (98 big blinds), I can afford to fold weak hands and wait for better opportunities. Position is unfavorable (Big Blind), and there's no reason to play the worst hand in poker. This is a disciplined fold.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Q8o. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds, especially as the chip leader. I will raise to 300, which has been the standard opening size throughout the tournament, to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop or play in position.

9-6 offsuit is a trash hand with minimal equity. Playing out of position against Flash's aggression would require premium holdings. With 46 hands remaining and a healthy stack, I can afford to be selective and wait for better spots. Fold and preserve chips for premium opportunities.

Facing a button open to 300 with K♥Q♦ from the big blind and a 6,125 stack (~61BB). The pot is 450 and calling 200 more (300 total) gives ~3.25:1 pot odds — KQo is well within a continuing range heads-up. I'm out of position postflop, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet (min-raise to 500 is too small to gain real fold equity, a larger 3-bet invites a 4-bet from a much bigger stack). Calling keeps the pot controllable, realizes equity, and lets me proceed with a clear postflop plan: raise/lead for value on K/Q high cards, c-bet selectively on favorable textures, and check/fold or pot-control on dry boards when I miss. I’ll call to see the flop (300 total committed).

Flop: A♥ 9♦ Q♥. I hold K♥ Q♦ — second pair with a very strong kicker but there's an Ace on board and I’m out of position to the preflop raiser. Flash’s continuing range includes lots of A-x and broadway hands that beat me, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that is often behind. I’ll control the pot and keep options by checking: I can call reasonable c-bets (realizing showdown value and denying big bluffs less often), re-evaluate on the turn (raise/bet for value if a safe card like K or a blank comes, fold to heavy aggression if I’m clearly dominated). Checking maximizes long-term EV here versus barreling out of position with a medium-strength hand.

On a board of Ah-9d-Qh, I have middle pair with a weak kicker (Qs 8c). While my hand has showdown value, this board is quite 'wet' with both straight and flush possibilities, and there is an Ace present which is very likely to be in the Big Blind's calling range. By checking back, I control the pot size and avoid a potential check-raise on a coordinated board, allowing me to see a turn card and evaluate the strength of my hand relative to Minni's actions on later streets.

Check. I have K♥Q♦ — second pair with a very strong kicker but I’m out of position and both checked the flop. The 7♦ on the turn doesn’t meaningfully improve my relative hand and there are no realistic 5‑card flush/straight draws left (with one card to come I can’t make a flush), so a turn bet has very poor fold equity and will largely get called by A‑x (which beat me) or by Qx/Kx (which I beat). Betting just bloats the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Better to control the pot, realize equity, and keep the river decision flexible: if I improve (K or Q) I can value bet later; if Flash fires a small river bet I can call with my kicker, and I can fold to large river aggression that strongly represents an Ace/set. Check now to preserve stack and re‑evaluate on the river.

On the turn card 7d, Minni has checked for a second time. My hand, Queen-Eight, remains middle pair. Given the texture of the board (A-Q-9-7) with multiple flush draws (hearts and diamonds) and straight possibilities (KJ, JT), checking back is the most prudent play. I have solid showdown value and want to keep the pot small. If Minni has an Ace, she's likely to check-call or check-raise here. If she has a draw, checking allows me to realize my equity and potentially catch her bluffing on the river or check behind to see the showdown for free.

I have K♥Q♦ on A♥ 9♦ Q♥ 7♦ 7♣ — second pair (queens) with a very good kicker but I’m out of position to the preflop raiser (Flash). Both checked flop and turn and Flash checked the river as well. Flash’s checking line polarizes less toward strong, value-heavy hands here (would expect a c-bet on flop or a turn bet with many strong hands) and more toward pot-control with Ax, Qx, or speculative/missed hands. The river pairing (7s) introduces the possibility of trips/full house, but a player with a 7 would likely have played it more aggressively earlier; the lack of betting on any street reduces the chance of a heavy value-only hand firing multiple streets. Betting now has poor fold equity — a small bet will often be called by hands that beat me (A‑x) and may only get value from worse Qx combos; a larger bet risks a raise that I don’t want to face out of position. Given stack sizes and pot (650), the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and go to showdown — preserve my stack when behind and avoid bloating the pot when I have a marginal hand.

On the river, the board has paired sevens and includes an Ace. I have a pair of Queens. Minni has checked on every street after calling my pre-flop raise. While she could be trapping with an Ace, it is more likely she has a weaker pair (like a Nine), a small pocket pair, or a missed draw. Betting for value is risky because few worse hands will call, and almost all better hands (Aces or slow-played trips) will call or raise. Checking back guarantees a showdown and is the most reliable way to realize my hand's equity in this pot.

5-2 offsuit is the worst hand in poker. Despite having position on the button, this hand lacks the equity to justify a call. Even accounting for position value, 5-2o is too weak to play profitably. With 45 hands remaining and a medium stack, I should preserve chips and wait for better opportunities.

Pocket sixes (6c6s) heads-up from the small blind are a strong-ish, medium pair—best played aggressively preflop to isolate the big blind and take the initiative. The pot is small (150) and my stack (6200) is deep enough to make a standard open without overcommitting. Raising to a standard ~3xBB (to 300) achieves value vs worse hands, folds out some overcards, and keeps the pot manageable if Flash 3-bets. Limping surrenders initiative; the min-raise (200) is too small and invites cheap draws. Plan: open to 300, then proceed postflop based on texture and opponent reaction (call small 3-bets, fold or re-evaluate vs large 3-bets).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack suited (Ah Jh), which is a premier hand in a three-handed game. Minni, in the Small Blind, has opened with a raise to 300. Given that I will have post-flop position for the rest of the hand and my hand is significantly ahead of a typical small-blind opening range, a 3-bet is the standard play here. This allows me to build a pot with a strong hand and potentially take it down immediately. I will raise to 1,000 to put pressure on Minni's stack.

Facing Flash's 3-bet to 1000 after I opened to 300, pocket sixes are best played by calling here. Heads-up I keep postflop positional advantage (I’ll act last after the flop), and calling 700 into a 1300 pot (~2.85:1 immediate odds) plus strong implied odds makes this a good set-mine and allows me to realize equity against a likely wide 3-bet range. A 4-bet would bloat the pot and overcommit a medium pair; folding is too passive. Plan: call to see a flop, pot-control and extract value with sets or play cautiously if I miss.

I check. With 6♣6♠ on 8♥Q♥7♠ I missed — I’m an underpair with no heart backdoor and Flash was the preflop 3‑better, so his range contains a lot of Qx/overpairs and heart combos. Pot is already 2,000 and my stack (5,250) is large enough to keep implied odds for a set but I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. Checking controls the pot, lets Flash show aggression, and allows me to call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize equity or fold to heavy pressure. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn and consider a small bet only with a favourable card; if he C-bets small I’ll usually call to see the turn, fold to a large shove/raise.

I have the nut flush draw on a Queen-high board. After 3-betting pre-flop and being checked to, a continuation bet is standard to maintain aggression and potentially take the pot down now. If called, I still have significant equity to improve on the turn or river. With Minni's relatively short stack, a bet of 900 (slightly less than half-pot) is appropriate to keep her range wide while still exerting pressure.

Call 900. I have 6♣6♠ — an underpair to the Q on 8♥Q♥7♠. Flash was the preflop 3‑better so his range has strong Qx/overpairs but also a fair number of bluffs and heart draws. His flop bet is small (900 into a 2,900 pot ≈31%), so calling to control the pot and realize equity is the right line. Pot odds after his bet are ~4.2:1 (need ≈19% equity); my raw set-out equity (~8.4% to the river) is thin by itself, but Flash’s sizing and wide 3‑betting frequency make calling profitable to see a turn and possibly extract if I hit. Raising OOP would bloat the pot without fold equity and is too risky. Plan: call to see the turn, call small turn barrels if appropriate, and fold to heavy turn/river pressure unless I improve to a set or get a clear read to continue.

Check. The turn (8♥ Q♥ 7♠ 7♣) paired the board and I remain an underpair (6♣6♠) with essentially no draw (only ~4% to hit a set on the river). Flash was the preflop 3‑better and c‑bet the flop small; his check on the turn looks like pot‑control with Qx/medium pair or a missed/float line rather than a clear fold target. Betting now bloats a pot (3,800) I’m already largely committed to and creates a spot where a raise would put me to an extremely tough decision with very little equity. Pot‑control and seeing the river is the safest, +EV line: check and call small river bets if appropriate, fold to large river aggression unless I improve.

I have the nut flush draw and one overcard on a board that just paired the sevens. Minni checked to me after calling my flop bet. Given the current pot size and Minni's remaining stack, a bet here would likely commit most of her chips, and she is unlikely to fold a Queen or any pair. By checking, I take a free card to realize my equity and avoid being forced into a difficult decision against a potential check-shove. If I hit my flush on the river, I am in a strong position to extract the rest of her stack.

Check. I have 6♣6♠ on 8♥ Q♥ 7♠ 7♣ T♦ — an underpair with no heart or straight. Flash 3‑bet pre and c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, so his range contains a lot of Qx/overpairs, paired/8x hands and some missed draws. A river bluff here would risk a large portion of my 4,350 stack into the 3,800 pot and is likely to be called by many of his continuing hands. Pot control is the best line: check to see a showdown or induce a thin value/bluff from him. If Flash bets a very small amount (roughly ≤1/3 pot) I may call as a bluff‑catcher, but I will fold to larger river bets or a shove.

The river didn't complete my nut flush draw, leaving me with Ace-high. Minni has checked on every street after calling my pre-flop 3-bet and my flop continuation bet. While her checks could indicate weakness, they also suggest a hand that is willing to go to showdown but not lead out, such as a mid-pair or a Queen with a weak kicker. Given that the pot is nearly equal to her remaining stack, a bluff would likely need to be an all-in to be effective, and she is unlikely to fold any pair given the pot odds. My Ace-high retains some showdown value against her missed draws, so checking back is the most sensible play to minimize losses on a missed draw.

I'm on the button with Kd2s and ~81 BB effective. In 3-handed play the button should be opening a wide range — K2o is marginal but acceptable to raise for position and fold equity. I will raise to 300 (standard ~3x BB here) to pressure the blinds and take the initiative. Plan: if I face a large 3-bet I will fold (hand is easily dominated); if called I'll play postflop in position — c-bet dry textures, check/control on coordinated/draw-heavy boards to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 8c, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised from the button, and although I am the chip leader, there is little reason to defend with a marginal hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

7d-3c is the worst possible starting hand in poker. While I should defend my big blind more frequently against button raises with weaker holdings, this particular hand (seven-three offsuit) is too weak to justify a call. With a 9,750 chip stack in a 50/100 blind game, I have adequate chips to fold marginal holdings. The pot odds of 2.25:1 don't justify playing such a terrible hand. Folding is the mathematically correct play.

I am on the button with Q7o in a three-handed game. While this hand is marginal, it is typically within a standard opening range from the button to attempt a blind steal. Both Claude and Minni have been active, but as the chip leader, I can exert pressure. I will raise to 300; if I face a 3-bet from either blind, I will fold, but if called, I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand.

T4o is a weak hand that plays poorly in all situations. From the small blind facing a raise, folding is the standard play. Flash has position on me and a significant chip advantage (21650 vs my 9700). While the pot odds (450 to call 250) look tempting on the surface, my hand has poor equity against a button raiser's range and lacks playability post-flop. I need to preserve my stack and wait for better spots where I can play high-quality hands. Folding hands like this is essential for solid bankroll management.

Flash opened to 300 from the button and Claude folded. I’m in the big blind with A♥8♦. Heads-up A8o is a hand I should defend — calling 200 more (300 total) is the right play here. The pot is 450, so calling requires ~31% equity; against a typical button-opening range I have enough equity to continue. I’d rather keep the pot manageable and see a flop OOP than 3-bet and bloat the pot against a much deeper opponent who can 4-bet or call and pressure me. Plan: call to 300 total, evaluate the flop — bet/raise for value when I hit an Ace or strong draw, control the pot or fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

Check. I’m out of position with A♥8♦ on 2s-2h-Td and I have only Ace-high — no pair and no meaningful draw. Betting OOP on a paired board as a bluff is thin (Flash’s BTN opening range connects often with a T, small pairs, or overcards) and would bloat the pot out of position. Checking controls the pot and lets Flash take the lead. Plan: if Flash c-bets a small-to-medium size (~<=1/2 pot ≈ 300–350) I’ll call to realize equity and see a turn; fold to large raises/overbets that polarize to tens/trips/strong hands. If Flash checks back, we go to showdown cheaply and can re-evaluate the turn (bet for value if we pair an A or improve).

On a dry, paired board of 2s-2h-Td, I have a standard continuation bet opportunity after raising pre-flop. This board texture favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, as it is unlikely to have connected well with Minni's big blind defending range unless she has a Ten or a Deuce. By betting approximately half the pot, I can force folds from her 'air' hands (like lower cards or non-paired connectors) while maintaining the initiative. If she calls, I will have to evaluate her range on the turn, but for now, a bet of 300 is a principled play to take down the pot.

Call 300. The pot is 950 after Flash’s 300 c-bet; calling 300 builds the pot to 1250 so I’m getting about 24% equity needed to continue (300/1250). A8o has reasonable showdown value and blocks many Ax combos in the button’s range. Flash’s bet is a medium (≈1/2-pot) c-bet — not highly polarised — so there are plenty of overcards and bluffs I currently beat. Raising OOP with only Ace-high is thin and would bloat the pot into a position disadvantage; folding here gives up too much equity for the price. Plan: call to see a turn, keep the pot controlled, and fold to heavy turn aggression. If I pair my Ace on the turn I’ll start betting for value; if the turn is a blank I’ll proceed cautiously (check and fold to large pressure).

Check. I have only Ace-high (A♥8♦) on 2♠-2♥-T♦-3♣ and I’m out of position vs a button who opened preflop and c-bet the flop. The turn is a blank for me — no pair, no real draw — so betting OOP as a bluff is thin and simply bloats the pot against hands that have me crushed (T, trips, small pairs). Checking controls the pot and lets Flash continue with bluffs or marginal hands. Plan if he acts: call small-to-medium turn bets (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot ≈ ≤600–650) to realize equity and get to showdown cheaply; fold to large/ polarizing turn bets (bets/overbets that strongly represent tens/trips or a polarized value range). If he checks back I’ll go to the river and only start betting for value if I pair my Ace or otherwise improve.

The turn 3c is a complete brick and doesn't change the board state significantly. Since Minni called the flop bet on a dry 2-2-T board, her range likely includes a Ten, a pocket pair, or a strong Ace-high float. My Queen-high has very little equity against that range. By checking, I control the pot size and take a free card to the river, where I might improve to a winning hand or look for a better opportunity to bluff if the board texture changes or she show further weakness.

Check to go to showdown. Line: BTN open pre, c-bet flop (300) that I called, then both checked the turn. River 4♣ is a blank for my A♥8♦. I only have Ace‑high and the opponent’s line (cb then check) is not highly polarized — it includes Tx, 2x, and a lot of missed overcards/bluffs. A river probe-bet will pick off some missed overcards but will be called by many hands I lose to (tens, any made pair like 4x, trips) and can get me raised into a very tough decision. With pot = 1,250 and my stack = 7,700, the safest +EV line is to check and realize showdown value: if Flash checks back I see the showdown for free; if he fires a small river bet I can call thinly to catch bluffs but fold to large, polarized bets. Checking avoids bloating the pot out of position with only Ace-high and preserves stack equity for better spots.

On this dry, paired board (2s-2h-Td-3c-4c), Minni's call on the flop indicates she likely has a piece of the board, such as a Ten, a pocket pair, or a strong Ace-high float. Since the turn and river were both checked, it is highly probable she is planning to go to showdown with a marginal hand. While my Queen-high is unlikely to win at showdown against her range, betting here as a bluff is risky because her flop-calling range is likely to be 'sticky' on such a static board. I will check behind to realize what little showdown value I have and avoid losing more chips to a hand that is unlikely to fold.

Pocket threes from the button in a 3-way pot. I'm getting 1.5:1 pot odds (150 in pot, 100 to call) and have positional advantage. With only 3 players, small pairs have decent value - I can hit a set, and if I miss the flop, I can fold to aggression. The call represents only ~1% of my stack, making it a low-risk exploration. Small pairs perform well in position post-flop when I can see action before deciding further commitment.

Jd4s is a weak, easily dominated hand and I'm out of position for the whole hand. Claude has already limped and Flash (BB) can defend wide or raise — a raise from me would be thin and likely called, leaving me OOP in a multiway pot. Calling 50 to see a flop gives poor implied odds and terrible playability postflop. With ~89 BBs and plenty of spots to pick on, preserving chips and waiting for a better, positionally playable hand is the +EV line here. Fold.

I am in the big blind with 9s 6s. Claude limped from the button and Minni folded from the small blind. Since I can see the flop for free, there is no need to raise and bloat the pot with a speculative hand. I will check and evaluate based on the flop texture.

I have nine-high on a board of 4c 2c 8d. Since Claude limped pre-flop and the action is now on me in the big blind, checking is the most logical play to see a free turn card. I have no made hand and very little drawing potential, so there is no reason to build a pot or attempt a bluff against an opponent who often calls down.

Pocket threes missed the flop on 4c-2c-8d. While the board is relatively uncoordinated, I have a weak hand right now with only pair of threes. Flash checked to me, indicating potential weakness, but betting here is risky. With only 8 outs to improve (three 3s remaining), I need to see cards cheaply. If I bet and Flash raises, I'm forced to fold a vulnerable hand. If I bet and get called, the turn becomes problematic. By checking, I keep the pot manageable at 250 chips, maintain position for the turn, and give myself the opportunity to make a set relatively cheaply. This is a favorable spot to take a free card since the board texture doesn't strongly favor continuation betting with a small pair that has poor showdown value.

The turn pairs the board with a deuce. I still have nine-high and no draws (neither straight nor flush). Claude limped pre-flop and checked back the flop, which indicates a weak range, but since I have no equity, there is no reason to bet and turn my hand into a bluff in a small pot. I will check and hope to see a free river card.

With pocket 3s and only bottom pair on a 4c-2c-8d-2d board, my hand has minimal showdown value. I have 2 outs to make a set on the river. Flash checked the turn, suggesting weakness. Betting here accomplishes nothing - I can't expect Flash to fold, and if they call, I'm likely behind on the river decision. By checking, I get a free card to hit my set, keep the pot small with a weak hand, and maintain position to make a river decision based on Flash's action. This is standard small-pair strategy: take a free card and see the river cheaply.

The river is the Jack of Clubs. The board has been checked through on all streets, suggesting both players have very weak ranges. I have nine-high, which has almost zero showdown value. However, the pot is only 250 chips, and there is little incentive to bluff into a capped range where Claude might call with any Ace-high or a small pair just to see the hand. I will check and see if I can win at showdown, though it is unlikely.

River showdown decision with pocket threes on a 4c-2c-8d-2d-Jc board. My hand is a pair of threes with 4-8-J kickers—very weak on this board with a pair of deuces. Flash checked the river, indicating weakness or a checking-back strong hand. Given: (1) My hand has minimal showdown value and poor kicker quality; (2) Flash has consistently checked (flop, turn, river), suggesting either a missed draw or weak holding; (3) The pot is only 250 chips, making a bluff bet inefficient; (4) If Flash has any pair, I'm likely behind, and if Flash has nothing, they won't call a river bet anyway; (5) By checking, I go to showdown for free with a marginal hand that may still win against a complete miss. A bet accomplishes nothing here—it either gets folded by nothing (wasting money) or called and loses to better hands. Checking is the standard play: take the free showdown with a weak pair.

On the button with 3♣5♦ — a weak offsuit hand but facing only the blinds and deep stacks, this is a spot to apply pressure. The pot is 150 and it's 100 to call; a standard open to 300 is a small investment relative to my 8,900 stack and matches the table’s common raise sizing. Raising to 300 gives me fold equity to pick up the blinds, isolates the pot, and lets me play postflop in position. If I face a significant 3-bet I will fold this marginal hand; if called I'll C-bet on favorable flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated or heavy-hit boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 6s. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. 8-6 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a Button opening range. Given my significant chip lead, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand and risk losing chips unnecessarily. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Calling with K3o in the BB against Minni's button raise. This is a marginal hand, but with reasonable pot odds (450:200) and 42 hands remaining in the tournament, seeing a flop is justified. The King provides decent equity and playability. I'll evaluate the flop texture and Minni's action before making further decisions.

Checking K3o on a dry 2d-Td-5c flop out of position. This is a marginal hand that plays better from a check-see-another-card approach rather than building the pot with weak strength. Keeps things simple and allows me to make better decisions on the turn with more information. With 42 hands remaining and a reasonable stack, pot control is appropriate here.

I paired my 5 (5d) on a relatively dry T-high flop (2d Td 5c). Claude checked to me — a sign he likely missed the flop or has a marginal holding — and I have position and the initiative from preflop. This is a textbook spot to continuation-bet for value and to deny equity to overcards/draws. I’ll make a medium-sized bet (300 ≈ 46% pot) large enough to get folds from unpaired broadways and protect my pair, but small relative to stacks so I’m not committed if I face heavy resistance. If Claude check-raises big, I’ll give up (his range will strongly contain tens/2s/sets); if he simply calls, I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate based on the card and his action.

Folding K3o on the flop. This is a marginal hand that lacks sufficient equity (~24%) to justify calling out of position against Minni's strong preflop raise and flop bet. The board (2d-Td-5c) doesn't help K3, and continuing would put me in a difficult position on the turn. With 42 hands remaining and a solid stack of 9550, there's no value in building pots with weak hands out of position. Preservation and selectivity are key at this stage of the tournament.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight offsuit. While this hand is sometimes used as a steal attempt in three-handed play, my opponents, particularly Minni, have been quite active and willing to defend their blinds or 3-bet. With a significant chip lead, I prefer to fold marginal hands like this and wait for better opportunities to apply pressure or play post-flop with stronger holdings.

K5o in the small blind is a weak hand, even in a heads-up situation against the big blind. While I only need to call 50 more chips into a 150-chip pot (33% odds), the hand has poor showdown value and I'm out of position post-flop. Given my relatively short stack (9500) and the need to preserve chips for stronger hands, folding is the optimal play. Minni has been playing aggressively throughout, and K5o will struggle against her range. Better to wait for a stronger spot.

Raising K-9o from the button in 3-handed play is standard. I have positional advantage over both blinds. A 3x BB raise (300 total) is appropriately sized to apply pressure while not over-committing with a medium-strength hand. This hand has decent playability against most blind defense ranges and benefits from playing in position.

I’m in the small blind with A♦Q♠ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, to call 250). AQs is a premium hand that should be 3-bet from this seat to get value, isolate the button opener, and gain fold equity — folding is too weak and a flat-call is passive OOP and invites the big blind to enter cheaply. With ~92BB effective I’ll size my 3-bet to a meaningful amount: raise to 1000 total (i.e. add 950 chips) — large enough to discourage marginal callers but leaves plenty of stack for postflop play. Plan: fold to a very large 4-bet, call or continue vs a single caller and play for value (c-bet favorable Ace-high textures, pot-control on coordinated draws).

Facing a raise from the button and a 10x big blind 3-bet from the small blind, King-Nine suited is not strong enough to continue. Calling out of position against a 3-bet range often leads to difficult post-flop scenarios where I am dominated by hands like AK, KQ, or pocket pairs. Given my substantial chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips in a marginal spot with a high probability of being behind.

K9o is a marginal hand facing a 3-bet from the small blind. In 3-handed play, while ranges are wider, Minni's 3.3x raise indicates real strength or a strong aggressive move. With K9o, I have approximately 30-32% equity, falling just short of the 33% needed for a break-even call given 2:1 pot odds. With limited stack depth (9200 chips) and 39 hands remaining in the tournament, I should preserve chips and wait for stronger holdings. Position is a factor, but not enough to justify investing 700 chips into a marginal equity situation.

I’m on the button with 8c Tc — a playable, suited connector-type hand that benefits hugely from position. With only the two blinds to act and stacks deep (~97bb), I should not limp and give cheap odds to the blinds; I want to take the initiative and either steal the blinds or play postflop in position. A standard 3x BB open (to 300) gets fold equity, isolates or simplifies the field, and keeps the pot manageable. If I face a large 3-bet from Flash (very deep/aggressive) or Claude I’ll fold; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to realize equity (especially club backdoors). Raise to 300 is the best exploitative/GTO-leaning line here.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Nine suited is a very strong hand, especially from the Small Blind against a Button open. Minni's raise to 300 is standard. By 3-betting to 1,000, I exert pressure on the button and likely force a fold from the Big Blind, allowing me to take the lead in the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Given my chip lead, I can afford to play aggressively with premium and semi-premium holdings.

With 5-3 offsuit (worst hand in poker) in the big blind facing a 3-bet from Flash (20.1k stack) after Minni raised from button, this is a straightforward fold. While I'm getting 2.4:1 pot odds (1400 to call 900), my hand has no realistic winning potential out of position against two opponents. Flash's 3-bet from small blind indicates a stronger range, and Minni's call of 1000 suggests at least a decent hand. With 9100 chips remaining, folding weak unprofitable spots is essential for tournament survival. The odds don't justify playing the worst possible hand without any draws or high-card value.

Call 700 more (total commitment 1000). 8cTc on the button is a playable, well-connected suited hand with excellent post‑flop playability and implied odds. Flash’s 1000 3‑bet (≈10bb) looks like an aggressive isolating sized 3‑bet and often includes a lot of broadways and bluffs — so I have reasonable equity and lots of ways to realize it in position. Calling keeps the pot controlled with ~94bb effective stacks, preserves positional advantage, and lets me see a flop where I can c‑bet/raise or fold depending on texture. A small 4‑bet to 1700 is too thin here (I don’t have the blockers or a strong enough range to risk inflating the pot), and folding is too tight given the price and playability. Plan: call, use position to evaluate the flop (pressure when I connect or see club/connected boards, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards).

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, and the flop of 7s-2h-Js is relatively dry. I hold one overcard and a backdoor spade flush draw. A continuation bet here allows me to maintain initiative and represent a range that includes overpairs (QQ-AA) or strong Jacks (AJ). Given Minni's call pre-flop, they could have medium pocket pairs or broadway cards. A bet of 850 (about 40% pot) puts pressure on their range while keeping the pot manageable.

Flash’s flop bet (850 into a ~2950 pot) is a small c-bet from a wide/aggro 3‑betting range. I have 8♣10♣ and completely missed the J‑7‑2 board, but I have reasonable playability in position: 3 tens + 3 eights = 6 clean outs (~24% to pair by river) plus a small backdoor club-runner chance. To call 850 I’m getting ~22% pot odds, so calling is roughly +EV to realize equity and apply positional pressure on later streets. Raising to 1700 is too thin with no pair or strong blockers (it simply bloats the pot), and folding here surrenders too much given the price and villain’s wide betting frequency. Plan: call 850 more (total commitment 1850), evaluate the turn — if I pair or see a club I can apply pressure; if the turn is a blank and Flash barrels big, I can fold.

On the turn, the Nine of Clubs has improved my hand to second pair with an Ace kicker. While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, the board texture still contains a Jack, which is well within Minni's calling range. Checking here is a prudent move for pot control; it allows me to realize my equity and potentially catch bluffs from draws like spade flushes or straight draws (T8, 86). If Minni bets, I have a relatively easy call given my improved hand and the stack-to-pot ratio, but leading out again might bloat the pot unnecessarily against a range that still contains several Jacks.

I’ve just hit a made straight (7-8-9-10-J with my Tc8c on J-7-2-9). Flash’s line (preflop 3-bet, small flop c-bet, then check on the turn) is wide/aggressive — his check on the 9c is pot-control/weakness more often than strength. My straight is currently far ahead of his likely calling range (Jx, Tx, 9x, pocket pairs, some bluffs), so I should extract value and charge any hands that can still improve to a full house. I’ll bet for value and protection with a sizable but non-nut size: ~2/3 pot to get called by top pairs and weaker made hands while still leaving chips for later decisions. Bet 2500 (about 2/3 of the 3800 pot); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the river (and if I face a big check-raise, I’ll proceed cautiously since that line is rare and often indicates a very strong or trap hand).

On the turn, the Nine of Clubs has improved my hand to second pair with an Ace kicker. My check on the turn may have been interpreted by Minni as a sign of weakness, potentially inducing a bet from a wide range of hands including weaker Jacks, draws (like the spade flush draw), or pure bluffs. Minni's bet of 2,500 into a 3,800 pot provides me with pot odds of approximately 2.5:1. Given that I have improved my hand and still hold the chip lead, calling is a better option than folding, which would be too tight in this spot, or raising, which might only get called by hands that have me beat (like AJ or J9). I will call to see the river and evaluate.

On the river, the board has paired the Deuce (7s-2h-Js-9c-2c). I currently hold two pair, Nines and Deuces, with an Ace kicker. I am first to act. Given Minni's aggressive bet on the turn, it is highly likely she holds a Jack (which would give her Jacks and Deuces, beating my hand) or was semi-bluffing with a spade draw that has now missed. By checking, I allow Minni to check back with marginal hands that I beat (like a 7 or a lower pocket pair) or to bluff with missed draws. Leading out with a bet is risky because I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I would be forced into a difficult spot if she shoves her remaining stack. Checking for pot control and to evaluate her reaction is the most strategic move.

I have 8♣10♣ — I made the straight on the turn (7-8-9-10-J). The river (2♣) paired the board, which creates the possibility of full houses/quads (pocket 7s/9s/Js or 2s), but those combos are relatively rare. Flash’s line (3-bet pre, small flop c-bet, check on the 9-turn, call my 2,500) is consistent with a wide range: single Jx, Tx, 9x, some pocket pairs and bluffs. His river check looks like pot-control/weakness rather than strength. Checking back surrenders value from one-pair and two-pair hands that will call a river bet; betting large risks getting check‑raised by the infrequent full house. I’ll make a modest value bet to extract from worse hands while keeping the price of a check‑raise high: bet 2,000 into the 8,800 pot (~23%). If he check‑raises big I’ll treat that as a very strong/full‑house line and fold.

Minni's river bet of 2,000 into a pot of 8,800 gives me exceptional pot odds of approximately 5.4:1. This means I only need to win the hand more than 15.6% of the time to make calling profitable. While her turn bet was strong, the river deuce is a relative blank. My hand, a pair of Nines with an Ace kicker (two pair Nines and Deuces), is significantly stronger than a pure bluff or a missed draw like the spade flush or a straight draw (e.g., T8). Given the size of her bet relative to the pot, she could be betting for thin value with a weaker hand or attempting to push me off Ace-high. I will call to realize my equity at this favorable price, as folding would be too exploitable given the board texture and pot odds.

I am on the button with 8c 6c, which is a decent suited connector. The pot is currently 150 (100 from the big blind, 50 from the small blind). Although the hand history shows I have been quite active and recently involved in some large pots with Minni, a standard opening raise here is a good way to apply pressure from the button and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop in position. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing for this situation.

3-2 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. I'm in the small blind, out of position, facing a raise from Flash on the button. With a short stack (9050), committing 250 chips to the pot with this hand is unprofitable. I'm behind in stack size compared to both opponents, so I need to preserve chips for stronger holdings. This is a straightforward fold.

I’m in the big blind with AdKd (AKs) facing a standard button open to 300; Claude folded so it’s heads-up. AKs is a top-tier, position-independent hand — we want to take the initiative, get value from weaker aces and broadways, and earn fold equity against speculative hands. With deep effective stacks, a standard 3-bet to ~3x the open is appropriate: large enough to isolate and discourage flats but not so large as to commit the stacks unnecessarily. I’ll raise to 1000 total. If Flash 4-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (likely call or 5-bet shove against very large 4-bets); if called I’ll C-bet favorable A/K/diamond flops and control the pot on dry boards.

I opened the button with 8c 6c for 300, and Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the big blind. Although I have position and the pot odds are roughly 2.9 to 1, calling a 3-bet with a marginal suited gapper like 8-6 suited is risky against an aggressive opponent who currently holds the chip lead. Minni has shown a high level of aggression in recent hands (specifically Hand #112), and I would likely face significant pressure on post-flop streets. Given that I want to preserve my stack and avoid another large confrontation with the table leader right after losing a significant pot to her, folding here is the disciplined and safer professional play.

Raising to 300 (3x BB) on the button with A5o. This leverages positional advantage and healthy stack to either win the blinds outright or play a marginal hand in position. A5o plays much better with position than without it, and the ~150 chip investment gives good risk/reward for potentially winning 150 chips immediately or playing position-advantaged post-flop.

Claude opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with 3♦4♠ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with almost no postflop playability and little equity versus a button opening range. Calling costs 250 into a 450 pot (I’d need ~36% equity just to break even) and would often leave me multiway and out of position. A 3-bet bluff is too thin with a big blind behind who can defend or 4-bet, and there’s no reason to bloat the pot with a dominated hand when my stack is healthy. Fold and wait for a much better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with a suited connector (5s6s). Claude has raised to 300 from the Button. Given that I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, I am getting decent pot odds (2.25:1). Suited connectors play well post-flop, and defending the Big Blind against a Button open is a standard play with this range of hands. I will call to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 5s on a 2s 8d 9d flop. I have a backdoor straight draw and a backdoor flush draw. As the defender in the Big Blind against a Button open, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Betting out (donking) into this board texture doesn't provide much of a range advantage for me, so I will check and see how Claude reacts.

Flash checked a relatively dry flop (2s8d9d) after calling my pre-flop raise from the big blind. My Ah5c makes top pair with a weak kicker. Flash's check indicates weakness on this board—they likely have a middle pair, weaker ace, or a draw. I'm in position with a reasonable hand and should apply pressure with a standard continuation bet of approximately 350 chips (~54% of pot). This sizing is aggressive enough to win the pot immediately while not overcommitting with a hand that's vulnerable if called. If Flash has a weak ace or middle pair, they'll likely fold. If they call, I can reassess on the turn with more information about their hand strength.

On a flop of 2s 8d 9d, my 6s 5s provides only marginal backdoor equity with a backdoor straight and a backdoor flush draw. Claude's bet of 350 into a 650 pot is a standard continuation bet on a board that can connect with many parts of a Button opening range. Given that I have no direct pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw, and considering my 6 and 5 are undercards to most of the board, the most disciplined play is to fold rather than chasing thin backdoors or trying to float against an aggressor with a positional advantage.

Pot is only 150 and it costs me 100 (1 BB) to see the flop. 5h7s is a weak offsuit hand with limited equity and poor post‑flop playability, but I have positional advantage on both blinds. Raising here would risk getting 3‑bet or facing loose defense from Flash/Claude and unnecessarily bloating the pot with a marginal holding. I’ll pay the cheap price to see a flop in position and proceed cautiously — fold to strong action unless I hit a clear pair/straight draw.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Minni has limped from the button, which often indicates a wide or speculative range. I want to raise here to take control of the pot, isolate Minni, and prevent Claude in the Big Blind from realizing equity for free. A raise to 450 is a standard size to punish the limper and build the pot with a hand that is likely ahead of a limping range.

Folding with 93o (9♣ 3♦) from the Big Blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker and I'm out of position facing a raise from Flash with Minni still in the pot. There's no profitable reason to continue with such a weak hand. Preserving chips for better spots is the correct strategy.

I planned to limp to see a cheap flop, but Flash's sizable reraise to 450 changes the math. The pot is 650 and calling 350 requires ~35% equity; 5h7s offsuit has very poor equity and post‑flop playability versus a typical raising range and is frequently dominated. A bluff‑raise to 800 is an option but would bloating the pot and risks a 3‑bet from a deep, aggressive SB. With plenty of chips and better spots ahead, folding preserves my stack and avoids a marginal, high‑variance spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately, or set up a post-flop situation where I have range advantage.

As7s is a premium hand in the small blind against a button raise. Rather than passively calling, I'm raising to 800 to take control and build a pot. This puts pressure on both opponents while maintaining reasonable stack depth. The hand plays well postflop in position and has multiple ways to win (top pair, flush draw, wheel). A raise is more aggressive and profitable than a call with this holding at this stack depth in hand 116 of 150.

I’m in the big blind with 7♣6♦ facing an open to 300 (button) and a cold 3‑bet to 800 from the small blind. I’ve already posted 100 so calling costs 700 into a 1,200 pot — I’d need ~37% equity to justify a call, and in a three‑way pot out of position 7♣6♦ is unlikely to have that vs a polarized 3‑bet range and the opener’s continuing range. 7‑6o has poor playability OOP, few blockers to attempt a successful 4‑bet bluff, and the small blind’s effective stack (~8,500) means a light 4‑bet is risky. Better to conserve chips for spots where I have position or a hand with real post‑flop play. Fold.

I opened from the button with Ace-Seven offsuit, a standard move in three-handed play. Claude has responded with a 3-bet from the small blind. While I am in position and the price to call is relatively good (500 chips into a 1200 chip pot), Ace-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by Claude's 3-betting range (which likely includes stronger Aces and pocket pairs). Given that Claude has shown a tendency to 3-bet my button opens recently (e.g., Hand #86 and Hand #92), I want to avoid playing a bloated pot with a weak Ace that has poor playability and high reverse implied odds. I will fold and wait for a better spot to challenge his aggression.

2-J offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability even in position. At hand 117 of 150 in a 3-handed tournament with reasonable stacks, I should be selective. The hand lacks sufficient equity to justify calling 100 chips. Folding preserves my 9700 chip stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up from the small blind with T♥5♠ is marginal but playable — I have positional advantage postflop and can open a wide raising range. I’ll min‑raise to 200 total (2x BB) to take the initiative and generate fold equity; if Flash folds I scoop the pot, if he calls I can use position to c‑bet dry boards or control the pot. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet; if called, c‑bet appropriate flops and proceed cautiously on ace/ten or highly connected boards.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7 of spades and 4 of spades. The Small Blind (Minni) has min-raised to 200. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot of 300, giving me 4:1 pot odds. Additionally, I will have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. While 74s is a weak hand, the combination of excellent pot odds and positional advantage makes this a standard call to see if I can hit a strong flop or take the pot away later.

I raised preflop with T5 and got called; the flop is 6♦ 6♥ 6♣ — trips on board. I have no pair and no real draw. This board gives any pocket pair in the big blind an immediate full house (and the remaining 6 gives quads), so bluffing or barreling is extremely high risk with almost zero fold/equity. I’ll check to control the pot and see a reaction; fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if Flash inexplicably checks back.

The board has come out extremely dry with three-of-a-kind (Sixes). My hand, 7-high, is currently three-of-a-kind with a 7 kicker. On this board, any pocket pair held by an opponent is already a full house. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. While a bet might occasionally force a fold from hands like 54 or 32, most of Minni's range (Ace-high, King-high, or any pocket pair) will likely call a single bet or is already beating me. By checking back, I keep the pot small and see the turn for free, allowing me to potentially realize my equity or wait for a better opportunity to bluff or value bet depending on the turn card.

Flop/turn is 6♦6♥6♣ / 3♥ — I hold T5 with no pair and no realistic draw. Flash’s call preflop includes a lot of pocket pairs (now full houses) and possibly a single 6 (quads), so he’s much more likely to have a made hand than I am. There’s essentially no fold equity and bluffing here into one opponent is very high risk. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my stack; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet on the turn and only continue if Flash inexplicably checks back to the river, at which point I’ll reassess.

On a board of 6-6-6-3, I currently hold three sixes with a seven kicker. Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has checked both the flop and the turn, which indicates she likely does not have a pocket pair (which would be a full house) or an Ace-high hand she wants to protect. However, my seven-high is still very weak and loses to any Ace, King, Queen, or Jack-high hand. Since there is very little value in betting and I am unlikely to force a fold from better high-card hands on such a static board, checking back to see the river for free is the most prudent play.

Board 6♦6♥6♣ / 3♥ / T♠ — I hold T♥5♠, which picked up a lone pair on the river. However the flop of trip sixes turned the dynamics: any pocket pair in Flash’s calling range (22–AA) now makes a full house, any single 3 in his hand pairs the board 3 and makes a full house, and any Ten in his hand pairs the river Ten and also makes a full house. Flash called a min‑raise preflop and then checked down, so his range is full of hands that beat a lone pair here. Betting has virtually no fold equity and little value (the hands that call a bet are mostly full houses). With a large stack relative to the pot, the prudent line is to check to showdown and avoid bloating the pot — I will check and plan to fold to any significant raise on this street.

On a board of 6-6-6-3-T, my best 5-card hand is three-of-a-kind sixes with a ten and seven kicker (6-6-6-T-7). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked all three streets. While this passivity might suggest she is weak, she still beats my seven-high with any Ace, King, Queen, or Jack-high hand, and any pocket pair would have a full house. Given the extremely dry and static nature of the board, it is unlikely a small bluff will fold out better high-card hands that have decided to go to showdown, and the pot is too small to justify a large-scale bluff. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown for free.

Button with 7♣6♦: a speculative, playable hand but not worth folding outright. Limping (call 100) surrenders fold equity and invites multiway pots; raising leverages position and the table's tendency to fold to standard opens. I'll open to a standard 3x-ish size (300 total) — big enough to pick up the blinds often and keep the initiative, but small enough to avoid committing too much if I face resistance. Plan: if I get a single call, use position to c-bet favorable textures and realize equity; fold to a sizable 3-bet (respect strong ranges); if everyone folds I scoop the 150 pot. This balances aggression and pot control with a marginal hand.

I have Ace-King offsuit, which is a premier hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Minni's raise from the button is standard, and I should 3-bet for value and to take initiative from the Small Blind. A 3-bet to 1,000 (roughly 3.3x her raise) is a standard sizing to build the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop or isolate against one opponent while out of position.

Folding J9o to Flash's 3-bet from small blind. Out of position with a marginal hand against an aggressive player. J9 doesn't have sufficient equity against Flash's range (likely strong pairs, AK, AQ) to justify calling 900 more chips. Preserving stack for better spots.

I opened to 300 from the button with 7♣6♦ and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1,000. 7‑6o is marginal but has good playability in position and the extra cost to continue is small relative to my 16,150 stack. Calling 700 more (1,000 total) gives ~33% break‑even pot odds and lets me use position to realize equity and punish an aggressive SB 3‑betting range that likely contains bluffs. 4‑betting would be a thin bluff with a poor hand; folding surrenders too much equity for a cheap price. Plan post‑flop: proceed cautiously — continue on low/connected flops or with strong draws/pairs, check and fold to heavy pressure on hostile textures. Call to 1,000 total.

I have top pair, top kicker (AsKd) on a very dry board (Ah-8d-5s). This board is highly favorable for my pre-flop 3-betting range. I need to bet for value against weaker aces and pocket pairs that Minni might have called with pre-flop. A sizing of roughly 40% of the pot (850 chips) is standard for a continuation bet in a 3-bet pot on this board texture, allowing me to build the pot while keeping her range wide.

Call. Pot is 2,950 and Flash’s lead is only 850, so the pot after his bet is 3,800 and I need ~22.4% equity to break even on a call (850/3,800). With 7♣6♦ on A♥8♦5♠ I have a strong open‑ended straight draw (4s and 9s complete the 5‑6‑7‑8 wrap) — ~31% to make the straight by the river — plus occasional pair outs. That gives me positive expected value to see the turn in position. Flash 3‑bet preflop and made a relatively small flop bet, which is consistent with a wide c‑bet range (and some bluffs), so calling to realize equity and use position is the best line. Raising as a semi‑bluff is thin against a 3‑bettor who has many A× and strong holdings and would commit more chips unnecessarily. Plan: call 850, evaluate the turn — barrel/raise if I improve to a straight or get a strong two‑pair/top pair; fold to heavy turn pressure if I miss and the texture becomes hostile.

The turn pairs the board with the 8h. This is generally a safe card for my hand, as I still hold top pair with the best possible kicker (AK). Minni's range for calling a 3-bet and a flop continuation bet is heavily weighted towards other Aces (AQ, AJ, AT) and mid-pocket pairs. While trip eights are possible, they represent a small fraction of her range (like 89s or T8s). I will bet again to extract value from those weaker Aces and to maintain the initiative. A sizing of 1,800 is approximately 47% of the pot, which builds the pot for a potential value shove on the river while keeping her range wide enough to call with worse hands.

Facing Flash’s 1,800 turn bet into a 5,600 pot, I’m getting ~24% pot odds (1,800 / (5,600+1,800) ≈ 24.3%). With 7♣6♦ I have 8 clean outs to a straight on the river (four 4s and four 9s) — ~8/46 ≈ 17.4% — plus a few very thin pair-outs that rarely beat A-x or an 8. Pure drawing equity is slightly below the break-even pot odds, but I’m in position with good implied odds if I hit and I can fold to large river aggression if I miss. Raising as a semi‑bluff is thin here: Flash 3‑bet preflop and has shown aggression, so a raise will often be called or shoved by Ax/pairs and would commit too many chips with only ~17% to improve. The best line is to keep the pot controlled and use position — call the 1,800 to see the river, then value bet/shove if I complete the straight or fold/counter based on the river action and reads. Total committed after this call will be 3,650.

The river Queen is a significant card as it completes AQ, a hand definitely in Minni's pre-flop 3-bet calling range. While I still have top pair with the best kicker, the board has paired (8s) and now a second high card (Q) has arrived. By checking, I can control the size of the pot. If Minni has a worse Ace (like AJ or AT), she might check behind, and I win. If she has been floating with a draw that missed, she might attempt a bluff which I can then call. If she bets for value with an 8 or AQ, I can decide whether to call based on her sizing, whereas betting myself might expose me to a raise that I'd be forced to fold to or call off my stack against a superior hand.

Board A♥ 8♦ 5♠ 8♥ Q♦ — I hold 7♣6♦ and completely missed my straight. Flash was the preflop 3‑better and then barreled flop and turn, so his range is weighted toward Ax, broadway pairs and strong pocket pairs — many of which will not fold a river bluff. With no pair or legitimate showdown value, the only way to win is to bluff, but the fold equity required even for a small (~1/3 pot) bluff is on the order of ~25%+; against a 3‑bettor who has shown aggression I don’t expect him to lay down that often. Betting risks building the pot when I’m almost always behind; checking preserves my stack and keeps the pot small. I’ll check back and avoid a losing bluff here.

On the button with 8-7 offsuit, this is a standard positional opening range hand. Given I am the current chip leader, I want to maintain pressure on the blinds. Claude and Minni have both shown a willingness to defend or 3-bet recently, so if I encounter significant resistance, I can comfortably fold this speculative hand. However, opening for a standard 3x raise is the correct play to pick up the pot pre-flop or play a pot in position.

Folding 3-7 offsuit from the small blind facing a raise. This is the worst starting hand in poker, I'm out of position, and calling 250 more chips is not a sound investment. Even accounting for blind defense, the equity and position are too poor against Flash's aggressive raise and with Minni still to act. Better spots will come with a 9,550 chip stack.

Heads-up vs a button open to 3bb: I’m in the big blind with T4o. Calling 200 more into a 450 pot is cheap (puts 300 total in), and with deep stacks I can defend a wide range from the BB — T4o is marginal but can hit a ten or make two-pair/straight/runner improvements and has showdown potential. I don’t want to 3-bet this weak, offsuit hand out of position (the min-raise to 500 would bloat the pot without much fold equity). Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously post‑flop (fold to strong c-bets unless I improve, otherwise try to realize equity or apply aggression when I connect).

I have T4o on Tc Qc 2h — that’s second pair (tens) with a weak 4-kicker and two clubs on board. Flash opened from the button and will c-bet a wide range (Qx, Tx, club draws, air). With deep stacks and a high SPR (~19) I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, lets me call a standard c-bet to realize value against bluffs and weaker tens, and fold to a strong check‑raise or large bet. If Flash checks back I can re-evaluate on the turn and potentially bet a safe sizing for value/protection. Plan: check now; call small-to-medium c-bets, fold to big aggression, and only raise with a clear read or stronger holdings.

On a flop of Tc-Qc-2h, I have 8-high with no immediate draws. This board is quite connected and likely hits a significant portion of Minni's big blind defending range (Qx, Tx, and club draws). However, as the pre-flop aggressor, a standard continuation bet can often take the pot down immediately if she has completely missed with small pairs or air. I will place a half-pot bet to test her strength; if she calls or raises, I have a clear path to fold on later streets given my lack of equity.

I have top pair (T♥) with a terrible 4-kicker on a two-club board (Tc Qc 2h). Flash’s flop stab is small (300 into a 950 pot) and represents a very wide button c-betting range (club draws, Qx, weaker Tx, and lots of air). Raising out of position with a marginal TP/weak kicker is unnecessary — it bloats the pot, plays poorly against Qx and club combinations, and creates difficult turn/river decisions. Calling is cheap to realize equity, keep worse tens and bluffs in, and preserve flexibility. Plan: call the 300 to control the pot and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to strong, large turn aggression or continue/raise only if I improve or get a clear read.

I have T♥4♠ — top pair on Tc Qc 2h 9d but a very weak kicker and two clubs on board. Flash (button) opened and posted a small flop stab, so his range is wide: Qx, Tx, club combos, and a lot of air. With the turn completing a 9 (non-club), the main threats are Qx (which already beat me), two‑club hands with a one‑card flush draw (9 outs), and worse tens/air that I can get value from. Pot is 1,250 and my stack is deep (effective ≈12,200) so SPR is still high — I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal kicker, but I also don’t want to give a free card to club draws.

Plan: bet a modest protection/value size to charge club draws and extract vs worse tens while keeping the pot controllable. A ~1/3 pot stab makes sense — it folds out some pure air and charges draws, while allowing me to fold if Flash shows heavy aggression. If he raises big I’ll relinquish the pot (my kicker is too weak to force it). If he just calls, I’ll re-evaluate on the river based on his sizing and the card.

Action: bet 400 (small, protective/value bet).

On the turn, the board is Tc-Qc-2h-9d. I held 8-7 offsuit and continuation bet the flop, which Minni called. The 9d on the turn is a coordinated card that likely helps the defender's range (hands like Q9, T9, or various Jack-x combinations that now have straights or better draws). Minni has led out (donk bet) for 400 into the 1250 pot. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack), any Jack on the river also completes several higher straights (like K-J). Given that I have no made hand and my equity is low against a range that is now leading into me, the most prudent play as the chip leader is to fold and wait for a better spot, rather than calling to chase a thin draw.

I'm on the button with A3o (Ace-Three offsuit), a marginal weak hand. While the call is only 100 chips into a 150-chip pot, playing A3o against two opponents in a multi-way pot is not profitable long-term. My stack of 9550 chips is healthy and provides no urgency to gamble with weak holdings. A3o struggles significantly in multi-way pots and is out of position postflop against two opponents. With 30 hands remaining in this 150-hand tournament, this is an ideal spot to fold weak hands and wait for better opportunities. The risk-reward doesn't justify entering this pot with this specific hand composition.

I have K♦3♣ in the small blind with a 13,400 stack and only 50 more to call into a 150-chip pot. Heads-up this is a spot where an open from the SB is standard — Kx has reasonable high-card value and is cheap to play postflop if called. I’ll raise to the minimum open (200 total) to take the initiative and pressure the big blind’s wide defending range while keeping the size small relative to effective stacks. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold (I don’t want to play a big pot with K3o); if he calls I’ll C‑bet on favorable texture (or proceed cautiously on dry/overcard boards) and evaluate on the turn. The risk is small versus the potential to pick up the blind or play a controlled pot.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d6h. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, meaning I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot of 300. This 3:1 price is generally acceptable to defend a wide range in a heads-up situation, but 96o is a relatively weak hand. However, given the current tournament stage and the small raise size, I will call to see if I can hit the flop and navigate post-flop.

I was the preflop aggressor (raised to 200) and the flop J♣ T♣ 7♣ gives me K♦3♣ — a four‑to‑a‑flush plus a clean overcard (K). This is a strong semi‑bluff spot heads‑up: I have meaningful equity if called and decent fold equity against Flash’s wide BB defense (many non‑club broadways and small pairs that will fold). Betting also keeps the initiative and defines his range. I’ll size about half the pot to apply pressure without overcommitting (200 into a 400 pot). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only keep building if I improve (club/king/paired board) or the turn is favorable; if he raises large I’ll fold rather than bloat the pot with K3o.

The flop is highly coordinated with three clubs (JACK OF CLUBS, TEN OF CLUBS, SEVEN OF CLUBS). I hold NINE OF DIAMONDS and SIX OF HEARTS, giving me no pair and no clubs. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an Eight), any club on the turn or river would make my hand extremely vulnerable even if I hit. Minni has shown aggression pre-flop and on the flop; without a club or a piece of the board, calling is a losing play. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I have J♦3♥ — a weak, unsuited hand, but I’m on the button with only the blinds left to act. The pot is 150 and calling 100 is cheap, but raising gives me fold equity and the initiative from late position. With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand postflop; a standard 3x-ish open (to 300) is the right compromise: it’s large enough to fold out the weakest blind defense ranges but still keeps future decisions manageable. Plan: open to 300 to try to steal the blinds; if I face a 3-bet I’ll let it go; if I get called I’ll use position to c-bet dry/advantageous flops and play cautiously when I miss or face heavy resistance.

In Hand #121, I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Spades and Deuce of Spades. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. Although my hand is suited, it is very weak (T2s) and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against Minni, who has been playing aggressively. With a healthy chip lead of 16,750, there is no strategic reason to defend the Small Blind with such a marginal hand against a 3x open. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Folding 5-3o. This is one of poker's worst hands with no high cards, poor draw potential, and reverse implied odds. Although receiving 2.25:1 pot odds, they're insufficient for such a weak hand. With 9,450 chips at hand 121/150, I'm not desperate enough to justify playing this hand out of position. Preserving my stack for better spots is the disciplined play.

I am on the button with 6s 3c, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Given my current stack lead and the fact that there is no strategic reason to engage with such a poor hand against two capable opponents in the blinds, folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

I'm in the small blind heads-up against Minni with 5-10 offsuit. While this is a weak hand, I'm getting 3:1 odds (150 to 50) to see the flop, which makes a call reasonable from this position. Flash has already folded, making this a two-player pot. With 9400 chips remaining and still having 28 hands to play, I can afford to see a flop with marginal hands in favorable positions. My plan is to play positionally post-flop and fold if I don't improve or miss the board entirely. The pot odds justify the call despite the weak hand quality.

Holding 4♦6♣ — a weak, unconnected offsuit hand. Pot is only 200 and there is no additional money to call, so the cheapest way to see a flop is to check. Raising to 200 is too loose here: Claude has already shown willingness to call and will have a wide but often stronger range; I don’t want to build a pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll check to see a flop and only continue if I hit or get good pot odds / a favorable texture; fold or release to strong lead postflop.

Checking down the hand with a weak holding (5-10) on an Ace-high flop where I have no pair, no draw, and no equity. Minni's check suggests they may have something or are playing cautiously. A bluff bet here is too thin given my hand strength and stack size. Taking a free card to see the turn card is the correct play - if I improve, I can continue; if not, I can fold to aggression. This preserves chips in a hand where I'm likely behind.

I have 4♦6♣ on a 3♥ A♥ 9♦ flop — no pair, no heart, and only very remote backdoor straight possibilities. Pot is tiny (200) and there is no bet to call. Claude (SB) checked, and his calling preflop indicates a wide but often stronger range (including Ax). I’m out of position and have almost no equity, so the +EV line is to keep the pot small and see another card. I’ll check and re-evaluate on the turn: fold to any meaningful lead, call tiny probe bets only when the price justifies chasing backdoor outs, and consider a delayed bluff only if the turn texture and opponent actions create fold equity.

Checking with 5-high and no draws on the turn. Minni showed weakness on the flop with a check, and I have zero equity to win this pot. The correct play is to take a free card to the river rather than bluff with a hand that has no showdown value. If Minni bets here, I fold. If she checks, I'll evaluate the river action. No point throwing chips away with a bluff when my hand is this weak.

Board is 3h Ah 9d Qh, my hand 4d6c — no pair, no heart, essentially zero showdown equity and almost no meaningful backdoor outs. Claude called a small blind preflop (wide but often stronger ranges like Ax, Qx, pairs) and has checked flop and turn, showing inertia rather than weakness that a bluff would credibly target. Pot is tiny (200) and there is no bet to punish; betting now would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and limited fold equity. Best play is pot control: check and see a free river, then re-evaluate — fold to any sizable river bet, consider a light bluff only on very favorable runouts or very small river bet sizes. I’ll check to avoid bloating a pot with a marginal hand and preserve my stack for better spots.

I'm on the river with 5-10 offsuit on a board of 3h-Ah-9d-Qh-Jc. I have 5-high with absolutely no showdown value - this is literally the worst possible hand. Minni has checked down the entire hand (flop and turn), showing weakness or a marginal holding. My options are: (1) check and go to showdown if she checks, or fold if she bets, or (2) bluff bet with a hand that has zero equity. Given that I have no hand at all and Minni has demonstrated passive play, betting here would be turning a weak hand into a bluff with no credible story. The pot is only 200 chips and my stack is 9350, so there's no value in bluffing here. I should check and make a decision based on Minni's river action. If she checks, I may see showdown with the worst hand. If she bets, I have an easy fold.

Board: 3♥ A♥ 9♦ Q♥ J♣. My hand 4♦6♣ has no pair, no heart, no legitimate river outs — essentially zero showdown equity. Pot is tiny (200) and there is currently no bet. Claude (SB) called preflop and checked every street, a line consistent with a wide but often stronger holding (Ax, a pair, or a Q/J). I have poor blockers to his likely value hands and very little fold equity to make a bluff profitable. Out of position and with a healthy stack, the +EV line is to avoid bloating the pot in a marginal spot: check and fold to any meaningful bet. Therefore I will check and preserve chips for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with J4o, a weak hand that doesn't warrant aggression. However, I'm facing only 100 chips to call, and button position provides significant post-flop advantages. With 9550 chips, I can afford to see a flop cheaply and exploit positional play. Both opponents have shown tendencies to fold to pressure on later streets. J4 has some utility as it can make a straight or flush draw. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage rather than wasting the button - I'll act last throughout the hand and can control the action. This is a standard profitable button call in position against limped/checked-to blinds.

I have 7♣6♠ in the small blind facing a button limp and the big blind. This is a marginal offsuit connector — playable to see a flop but not strong enough to iso-raise given the min-raise to 200 (small raise that invites squeezes) and the fact I'll be out of position postflop versus two opponents. Calling the extra 50 (total commit 100) is cheap (≈17% pot odds) to realize equity and keep the pot small. I'll proceed cautiously postflop and fold to heavy aggression unless I hit a strong hand or clear draw.

I am in the Big Blind with a premium hand, Ace-Queen suited (Ah Qh). Both opponents (Claude and Minni) have limped into the pot. Limping is generally a sign of weakness or speculative hands. I should raise here for value and to seize control of the hand. A raise to 600 (6x the big blind) is a strong sizing that accounts for the two limpers and builds a pot while I likely hold the best hand.

Calling 500 more (600 total) with J4d on the button against Flash's raise. The hand is marginal in pure equity terms, but positional advantage, reasonable cost relative to stack (5%), and ability to fold to further aggression makes this acceptable. Flash is aggressive and I'll have excellent post-flop control with action last. A three-way pot develops here where I can make profitable plays with position. The suited aspect and straight/flush possibilities add outs.

Facing a raise to 600 from the big blind with the button calling, I’m now in a 3‑way pot and out of position. The pot is 1,300 and it costs me 500 to call, so I need ~27.8% equity to break even. 7♣6♠ offsuit has poor multiway equity and is often dominated; postflop play will be difficult as I’ll be first to act vs two opponents. Raising to isolate would commit far too many chips from the small blind with a marginal hand. Preserve my deep stack and fold to this sizable raise.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have a range advantage on this board. My hand, Ace-Queen of hearts, has two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. The board (10-9-5) is somewhat coordinated, but a standard continuation bet should be enough to take down the pot or define Claude's hand range. I will bet 650, which is 50% of the pot.

Calling 650 with J4d on 5d-Th-9d. The flush draw (9 outs) provides ~35% equity, which exceeds the 25% break-even required by 4:1 pot odds. This is a mathematically profitable call. The hand has strong semi-bluff potential with two streets to complete, and position allows me to control the action if the draw misses on the turn. Flash's bet looks like a standard continuation bet, and calling to draw is more prudent than raising with this marginal hand.

The 8c turn is a very coordinated card that likely helps Claude's range more than mine, as it connects several straight draws (like 7-6) and improves two-pair combinations. With Ah Qh, I have overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack), but my hand has lost much of its relative strength. Checking is the most prudent action here to control the pot size and see how Claude reacts to the board texture.

Flash checked the turn after raising the flop, suggesting weakness or pot control. With Jd4d on 5d-Th-9d-8c, I have 10 clean outs (flush draw + straight draw) giving me approximately 40% equity. Checking allows me to see the river for free while maintaining my remaining stack. With 8300 chips, preserving capital while holding a strong drawing hand is prudent. Flash's turn check removes the urgency to bet semi-bluff - I get to realize my equity without risking additional chips.

I hit top pair on the river with my Ace-Queen. The board is quite dangerous with several straight possibilities (any Jack makes a straight), but Claude's check on the turn suggests he likely doesn't have a monster hand. If he had a straight or strong two pair, he probably would have bet when I checked the turn. By betting now, I can extract value from a worse Queen (like Q-J or Q-10) or even a Ten that doesn't believe I have the Queen. A half-pot bet of 1300 is a standard value sizing here. If he raises, I will have to strongly consider folding given the four-to-a-straight texture of the board.

Calling 1300 on the river with J4d on 5d-Th-9d-8c-Qs, which makes an 8-high straight (8-9-10-J-Q). I'm getting 4:1 pot odds and have a strong made hand that beats most of Flash's likely holdings. Flash's turn check followed by a river bet suggests a medium-strength hand rather than a premium holding. My straight is well-positioned to win against likely holdings such as pairs, two pairs, or lower straights. This is a profitable call given both the hand strength and pot odds.

Button with J♣7♦ — marginal but very playable in position against the blinds. I want to apply pressure and steal the blinds/isolate, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300). This sizing gives fold equity and leaves me room to play postflop in position if called. Plan: continuation bet on dry, favorable flops; check or control pot on coordinated/wet boards. Fold to a substantial 3‑bet since J7o has poor equity vs 3‑bet ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 6c, which are strong suited connectors. The button (Minni) has raised to 300, a standard opening size. With nearly 140BBs, suited connectors have high implied odds and play well post-flop. I am getting a good price to call (250 more into a pot that will be at least 700), and while I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, the potential to flop strong draws or two-pair/trips justifies seeing a flop. I prefer calling over 3-betting here to keep the pot manageable and use my stack depth to navigate post-flop.

K5s is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position in a 3-way pot against two aggressive opponents. While pot odds are 3.5:1, the hand lacks sufficient equity to call profitably. Minni showed strength with the raise from the button and Flash defended from the SB, suggesting reasonable holdings. Folding preserves chips for stronger spots, especially with 26 hands remaining in the tournament where I should be selective with marginal hands.

I have 7-high on a board saturated with high cards (A, Q, T) that likely favor the pre-flop raiser's range. I have no direct straight or flush draws, only a remote backdoor flush. As the caller out of position, checking is the standard and most prudent play here.

I opened to 300 from the button with Jc7d but this flop (Qc Td Ah) is a very bad one for my specific hand and hits my opponent’s calling range hard. I have no pair and only a one-card Broadway draw to a K (4 outs) plus a few cards to make a mediocre pair — overall poor equity (~15–17% to improve). Flash (SB) is likely to have Ax, Qx, Tx, broadways or pocket pairs that will NOT fold to a pure c-bet, so bluffing has limited fold equity. I’m in position and should control the pot: check and see how SB reacts. If he checks back I get a free turn; if he bets small/medium I can call with my turn equity or fold to big aggression; if I hit a K or pair I'll proceed aggressively.

On a board of A-Q-T-8, I currently have no made hand and no draws with 7c 6c. The flop went check-check, which is surprising given the board texture; the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) would typically continuation bet with most of their range (Aces, Queens, or even straight draws).
