This check-back suggests Minni might have a marginal made hand (like a Ten or a middling pair) or is giving up with complete air. However, because the board is so heavily connected, it's very difficult to pull off a successful bluff here without any blockers to the straights or the flush draw. I will check and look to fold if faced with any significant bet, as I have zero showdown value.

I raised pre-flop with J♣7♦ and both of us checked the Q♣ T♦ A♥ flop — a flop that heavily favors Flash’s calling range (A‑x, Q‑x, T‑x, broadways, pocket pairs). The 8♥ on the turn brings another overcard and a two‑heart board, but I have no hearts and only 3–4 outs to improve to a decent hand (very low equity, ~15–17%). Flash checked the turn as well, which could be either a pot‑control with a strong made hand or a weak hand trying to see a cheap river — either way my pure bluff has very limited fold equity against his likely range. Stacking off or building a big pot here with J7o is unnecessary and risky with deep stacks and only three players remaining. In position I should control the pot and see the river for free (or call a small bet), so I will check.

The board has paired the Ace on the river (Qc Td Ah 8h Ad), and the action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn. I have seven-high with no showdown value. While Minni's checks suggest she might be weak, the board is highly coordinated, and many hands in her range (like a Ten, an Eight, or even a King-high) would beat me. Attempting a bluff here is risky because the paired Ace might encourage her to call with any marginal hand or a better high-card. Given the small pot size and my total lack of equity, I will check and concede the hand if she checks back or bets.

I have J♣7♦ on Q♣ T♦ A♥ 8♥ A♦. I missed completely — no pair and only very weak outs. Pot is 700 and both players checked the river. Flash’s calling range to my button open (and passive line here) is heavily weighted to A‑x, Q‑x, T‑x and medium/overpairs that beat me. Betting as a pure bluff has very limited fold equity versus that range, and even a small bet risks chips unnecessarily given my stack (13,300) relative to the pot. The river pairing the board with a second A makes many value hands happy to check back or call a small bluff, so EV of bluffing is negative. I’ll preserve my stack and check back to avoid folding equity on future streets and to stick to my earlier plan of pot control with this holding.

I am on the button with 7-6 offsuit. While this is a hand that can occasionally be used as a steal from the button, my opponents Claude and Minni have been quite active and willing to defend their blinds or 3-bet. Given the current tournament situation where stacks are relatively balanced and we are approaching the final hands, I will opt for a fold to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position or facing a 3-bet from the aggressive blinds.

With 4h-Ts (essentially 10-4 unsuited) from the small blind, this is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Key factors: (1) I'm out of position and will act first post-flop, (2) the hand has poor equity against any reasonable holding from Minni in the big blind, (3) even if I raise to 200, I'm likely to get called with a hand that dominates mine, (4) with 12,050 chips at 50/100 blinds, chip preservation is important at this stage of the tournament (hand 125 of 150). The 50 additional chips to fold is a worthwhile cost to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position.

T2o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Despite having positional advantage on the button in 3-handed play, the hand is too weak to justify calling 100 chips. With 24 hands remaining and a solid chip stack (12050), it's better to preserve chips and wait for stronger opportunities. This maintains my disciplined, winning approach.

7♣3♠ is one of the weakest starting hands — poor equity and terrible playability. I'm out of position vs the big blind with deep stacks, so any post‑flop spot is difficult to play and I don't want to invest chips with such a hand. Calling 50 to see a flop is cheap but long‑term EV negative; raising would be pure bluff and unnecessary here. Preserve the 14,000 stack for better opportunities and fold.

3-handed with the button I should open a wide range; position is a big advantage. J♣7♠ (Jh7s) is marginal but acceptable to raise from the button to steal the blinds and take the initiative. I'll make the small, standard open to 2x the big blind (200) — it applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable if called, and folds out a lot of blind defense. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (this hand is not strong enough to gamble out-of-position vs a reraise); if called I'll play post‑flop in position, c-betting dry boards and exercising pot control when I miss.

With Q9o in the Small Blind against a 2x raise from the Button in a 3-handed game, the price to call is very attractive (150 to win a pot that will be at least 400). While Q9o is not a premium hand, it performs reasonably well in a short-handed environment. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds, and while raising is an option to exert pressure, calling allows me to see a flop and use my stack depth to navigate post-flop play, even though I will be out of position.

K2o is too weak to play out of position against two opponents despite the 5:1 pot odds. The hand has poor post-flop playability and is unlikely to win unless I flop extremely well. With 24 hands remaining in the tournament, I should preserve chips for better opportunities rather than speculate with a marginal hand from the BB.

I have flopped middle pair (Queens) on a board containing a King. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, the standard play is to check to see how they react. My hand has decent showdown value and can certainly call a standard continuation bet, but leading out myself would likely only get called by better hands or draws. I will check and re-evaluate based on Minni's action.

Heads‑up on the button vs the small blind. I opened preflop to 200 and was called; pot is 500. Flop K♥ Q♠ 6♠ — I have J♥7♠: no pair, only a weak backdoor/spade equity and little showdown value. Flash checked, so as the preflop aggressor I should apply pressure and try to take the pot now with a small c‑bet. The board will fold out a lot of his missed broadways, weak Ax and small pocket pairs while keeping the pot manageable if he calls. I'll make a roughly 40% pot stab to buy the pot (200). Plan: if he folds I take it; if he calls I check the turn and only continue with a real improvement (pair/clear spade draw); if he raises I fold — this hand has poor equity vs aggression.

On a flop of Kh-Qs-6s, I have flopped middle pair with Qc-9h. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has made a continuation bet of 200 into a 500 pot. This is a relatively small sizing (40% pot). Given the wide ranges in a 3-handed game, middle pair is too strong to fold to a single bet, especially at these odds. I am getting 3.5:1 to call. Raising wouldn't achieve much as it mostly isolates me against Kings or better Queens, so I will call to see how the turn develops and attempt to reach a cheap showdown or improve to two pair/trips.

On the turn, the Queen of Hearts completes my trips (Queens). Having checked and called on the flop with middle pair, my hand is now significantly stronger. Being out of position against Minni, the pre-flop and flop aggressor, the best play is to check again. This allows Minni to continue betting with a King, a draw (like spades), or a pure bluff. Leading out (donk betting) here would likely reveal the strength of my hand and cause many weaker hands to fold. I will check and plan to either call or raise depending on the size of Minni's bet.

Turn pairs the board (Kh Qs 6s → Qh). I hold J♥7♠ — no pair and my backdoor spade plan is dead now. I c-bet the flop and was called, so Flash’s range is weighted to Qx/Kx/medium pairs or slow-played strong hands; my bluff equity here is very low and if called I have only ~6 outs (~13% to improve to a pair on the river). Pot is 900 and stacks are deep enough that an unsuccessful bluff costs real chips. Best line is pot control: check and give up to aggression. If Flash bets I can fold and preserve my stack; if he checks back I get a free river to try to catch one of my 6 outs or potentially steal a small pot. Action: check.

On the river, the board is Kh-Qs-6s-Qh-Jd. I have trips with Qc-9h. The action was check-call on the flop and both players checked on the turn when the board paired Queens. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have a Queen, as a player with a Queen would often bet for value or to protect against draws. However, she could have a King or a Jack that would want to go to showdown. I am first to act. If I check, she will likely check back any marginal made hands like a King. By leading out with a value bet of roughly 55% of the pot (500 into 900), I can extract value from a King (Kx) or even a Jack (Jx) that might feel my line looks like a missed draw or a thin value bet. While there are hands that beat me (AQ, QT, QJ, T9 for a straight, or boats like JJ/66), in a 3-handed game, trips are very strong, and the probability of her holding those specific combinations is lower than her holding a King or a missed draw.

The river J♦ gives me two pair (Q♠Q♥ & J♥J♦). Flash (SB) just bet 500 into a 900 pot (~55% pot) — I must call 500 to win 1400, so required equity ≈ 500/(1400+500) ≈ 26%. Flash’s line (call pre, call flop, check-turn, bet river) is weighted toward value (Kx, Qx, some pairs) but also contains a meaningful number of missed draws and thin hands that will fold to a raise or bet small. My two pair beats most bluffs/missed draws and weaker pairs, and only loses to Qx/Kx full houses/quads or the very rare pocket full-house combos. Raising here would bloat the pot and fold out bluffs I want to get value from. Given the pot odds and that my hand is likely ahead of the river betting range often enough, the +EV play is to call and collect against bluffs and thinner value bets.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit, which is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. My stack is currently the largest at the table, and with only 22 hands remaining in the tournament, I want to avoid unnecessary risks with marginal hands, especially since Minni in the Big Blind has been showing significant aggression in recent pots. Standard play for J5o from the button is a fold as it's well outside a profitable opening range.

Raising KQ in heads-up small blind. This is a premium hand that should be played for value and to maintain aggressive standards against Minni's BB. A 2.5x raise establishes hand strength and allows me to control the pot dynamics post-flop while not over-committing with a hand that's strong but not yet made. KQ plays well both against stronger and weaker holdings in this position.

Facing a standard open to 250 as the big blind with T5o. I’d have to call 150 into a 350 pot (need ~30% equity). T5 offsuit is a weak, often dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop against the raiser (Claude). A min-raise as a bluff both bloats the pot and leaves me OOP against a caller with a much stronger range. With 130BB deep, preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold the marginal hand.

Calling with T8s on the button. This is a standard playable hand with good connectivity and flush potential. I have position advantage postflop which is crucial for hand strength flexibility. Pot odds are approximately 1.5:1, and this hand can profitably see a flop from the button. Stack size allows this exploratory play, and I can make good decisions based on flop texture.

Three-handed with J♦T♠ from the small blind — a hand that plays very well heads-up and is worth taking the initiative. Claude just limped on the button; I only need 50 more to call, but calling keeps me in a multiway, out-of-position pot. I prefer to iso-raise and seize initiative. I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50, so this adds 250), a standard isolation size that pressures the limper and gives fold equity vs the big blind. With ~130bb effective I can play postflop for stacks if needed; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to very large 3-bets, call or flat larger smaller 3-bets depending on sizing). If called, plan to c-bet favorable flops and pot-control on coordinated boards. Raise to 300 maximizes EV here by isolating Claude and taking the lead in the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. Claude limped from the Button and Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand to defend, especially out of position against a player like Minni who has been active and aggressive. While it is only 200 more to call into a pot of 500, the lack of post-flop playability and being out of position makes this a clear fold.

Calling with Tc8s on the button against a small blind raise. Strong hand playability with connectivity and suited potential, favorable 2.5:1 pot odds, and significant positional advantage for postflop decisions. Stack size supports exploratory play, and the hand can profitably see a flop with opportunity to make value decisions based on flop texture.

I was the preflop aggressor and got a single caller (Claude), so heads-up on a K♦ J♣ 5♠ flop. I hold J♦ T♠ — a medium-strength hand (middle pair, decent 10-kicker) that is vulnerable to Kx, big pairs, and sets. I want to take the initiative: a small continuation bet extracts value from worse pairs/underpairs and charges backdoor draws while keeping the pot manageable given deep stacks. I size to ~1/3–35% pot (250 into a 700 pot) — large enough to fold out some pure floats and get called by worse, but small enough to allow easy fold vs a polarized raise. Plan: if Claude folds I scoop; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (pot-control on blanks, fold to significant aggression if a K/A or strong check-raise appears).

Calling with Tc8s on 5s-Kd-Jc. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) with two cards to come. Pot odds of 3.8:1 significantly exceed the ~21% equity needed to call profitably. My 32-35% equity with the OESD makes this mathematically sound. Additionally, position on the button is valuable for post-turn decisions, and stack sizes allow exploratory play. Minni's aggression (pre-flop raise, flop raise) could indicate a strong made hand or semi-bluff, but either way, my drawing equity justifies the call.

I have J♦T♠ — top pair with a medium kicker on K♦ J♣ 5♠ 7♥. Claude called my small ~1/3-pot c-bet on the flop, which gives him a fairly wide, often-passive calling range (Kx, worse Jx, underpairs, some backdoor draws). The 7♥ is a virtual blank for that range and with ~1200 in the pot I prefer pot control with this hand rather than inflating the pot out of position. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in play, lets me call a reasonable single-barrel bet for value, and avoids getting committed to a big check-raise that would represent two pair/sets/strong Kx. Plan: check. If Claude bets a small-to-medium amount (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot) I’ll call and re-evaluate the river; if he check-raises or fires a large polarized-sized bet I will fold (or seriously re-evaluate depending on sizing). If he checks back I’ll take a cheap river and choose a modest value bet or showdown check depending on the card.

Minni's turn check signals weakness or a medium-strength hand wanting to check it down. With an OESD (8 outs), I have ~17% equity and prefer taking a free river card rather than inflating the pot as a semi-bluff. My eight clean outs to the straight (any 9 or 6) combined with positional advantage make checking optimal. This preserves stack efficiency while maintaining equity to win on the river.

I hold J♦T♠ on a 5♠ K♦ J♣ 7♥ 5♥ board — top pair with a medium (10) kicker. I was the preflop aggressor (iso to 300) and c-bet the flop (250) which Claude called; both checked the turn. Claude’s line is fairly passive (limp/call preflop, call small flop, check turn) and therefore contains a lot of Kx, weaker Jx, underpairs and some floats; very strong hands (sets, big two-pair) are possible but less likely given his passivity. The river pairs the board (second 5), which can give trips/quads to rare holdings but mostly doesn’t improve the range that called my flop bet. With deep stacks and a relatively small pot (1,200), a small value bet extracts calls from worse Jx and some pocket pairs while costing little if I’m raised. I size small (~1/4 pot) to target thin calls and preserve fold equity: bet 300. If Claude folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll usually be ahead of his calling range; if he check-raises large on the river I’ll strongly favor folding (his line would be very polarized and usually beat my one-pair hand).

River call with pair of 5s (K-J-T kickers) at 5:1 pot odds. Minni's turn check suggests weakness before the river bet. My hand has solid showdown value - I beat complete air, marginal hands, and have equity against made hands. The pot odds (needing only ~17% win rate) make this a profitable call given my hand's range against Minni's likely holdings. Stack depth supports this decision, and variance is acceptable at this stage of the tournament.

3-handed on the button with Q♠7♣, I want to seize initiative and use position. This hand is marginal but playable; opening a standard short-handed raise builds the pot when we have position and applies pressure to the blinds (both capable of folding). Table tendencies show 300 is a common open size here, and 3 BB is a comfortable size — big enough to gain fold equity but small enough not to overcommit vs a 3-bet. Plan: open to 300; if called play postflop aggressively in position (c-bet good flops, pot control on bad ones). Fold to a large 3-bet, consider flatting a small 3-bet depending on sizing and opponent.

I am holding pocket Nines in the Small Blind. In 3-handed play, this is a premium hand. Minni has been quite active from the button. To maximize value and protect my hand against the Big Blind (Claude) and the Button, a 3-bet is the standard play. I will raise to 1000, which is consistent with my previous 3-bet sizing and puts pressure on Minni while making it difficult for Claude to enter with marginal holdings.

3-6o is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a 3-bet from Flash after Minni's raise, I'm in a very weak position. I'm out of position (Big Blind), have minimal equity against the likely ranges of both opponents, and 3-6 is dominated by virtually any reasonable hand they'd 3-bet with. With a healthy stack of 11,100 chips, there's no value in calling here. I should preserve chips for premium hands in better situations. This is a straightforward fold.

Flash 3‑bet to 1000 after my open to 300. I hold Q♠7♣ — an offsuit, marginal hand that is often dominated by a standard SB 3‑bet range (pocket pairs, A/K, strong Broadway hands). The pot is 1400 and calling 700 requires ~33% equity (700/(1400+700)). Q7o’s equity versus a typical polarized 3‑bet range is well below that and it has poor post‑flop playability. The 3‑bet sizing is large enough that I don’t want to gamble with a dominated hand even with position. Fold and wait for a better spot to use my position or a stronger holding.

On the button in a three-handed game, QJ offsuit is a strong hand that should be played aggressively. I have the chip lead and want to put pressure on the blinds. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either take the blinds down now or play a pot in position against Claude or Minni.

I'm in the small blind with 2d-4d, one of the worst starting hands in poker. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and I need to call 250 more to see the flop. 

While I'm getting 1.8:1 pot odds (450:250), I would need approximately 35% equity to make this a profitable call. With 2-4 offsuit, I have only about 25-30% equity against a typical button raising range, making this a negative expectation call.

Additionally, I'd be playing out of position post-flop against a positional aggressor. With such a weak holding and no equity advantage, folding is the mathematically correct decision. I have a healthy stack (11,050 chips, ~110 big blinds) and should not waste chips on marginal situations with trash hands.

Folding is the optimal play here.

I have A♦Q♥ in the big blind facing a single button open to 300. This is a strong hand that fares well vs a wide button opening range, so I want to take the initiative and isolate rather than playing a bloated pot out of position. I 3-bet to 1,000 total (~3.3x open) — big enough to charge worse broadways and small pairs and to fold out some of the weaker parts of his opening range, but small enough relative to stacks to keep post‑flop play viable if called. Plan: if Flash folds I pick up the 450 chip pot; if he calls we proceed OOP and play aggressively on A/K-high boards and cautiously on coordinated/flushy boards; if he 4-bets large I will concede and fold to preserve my stack.

I opened QJo from the Button for 3x, and Minni has 3-bet me from the Big Blind to 1000. While QJo can be dominated by a tight 3-betting range (like AQ, KQ, or AJ), in a three-handed game, ranges are generally wider. Minni has shown plenty of aggression throughout the tournament. Given I have the chip lead and the significant advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand, calling 700 more to see a flop is a profitable play. I can evaluate the board and potentially outplay Minni post-flop or fold if the board is unfavorable and she continues with strength.

I 3-bet preflop to 1,000 and Flash called, so I arrive on this dry, uncoordinated flop (8♦-4♣-3♠) with two overcards (A♦ Q♥) and the initiative. This board misses a large portion of his cold-calling range (broadways, small pairs, some suited connectors), so a continuation bet is standard to extract fold equity and deny free cards. I’ll size to roughly 60% of the pot (≈1,200) — big enough to fold out weaker made hands and single-pair/broadway hands, but small enough to keep the pot manageable if called given effective stacks. Plan if called: reassess on the turn and bet for value only if I pair an A or Q; otherwise pot-control and fold to strong aggression. If he folds I scoop the pot. Therefore I will continuation-bet to apply pressure and protect my equity.

Minni 3-bet pre-flop and is now following up with a substantial continuation bet on a very dry board (8-4-3). My hand, QJ offsuit, has completely missed this flop. I have no draws and my overcards are likely either behind a pair or dominated by her 3-betting range (like AQ or KQ). Given the bet size is over half the pot and I have no real equity to continue, I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

Calling from the button with 3♣9♣ against the blinds. While the hand is weak, I have significant positional advantage as button player. The cost to call (100 chips into a 150 pot) is minimal relative to my 11,050 stack, and I can leverage position to control the hand postflop. If the flop is unfavorable, I have fold equity and can exit cheaply. If I hit any piece or can apply position aggression, I'm well-positioned. This is better than passively folding from the best position at the table.

I'm on the small blind with 4h Js. Claude just limped (called 100) and Flash is the big blind; the pot is 250 and I must call 50 to see the flop or raise to at least 200. J4o is a weak, easily‑dominated holding and playing it three‑way out of position is a losing proposition long term. Calling 50 gives very poor multi‑way equity and will frequently put me in tough spots post‑flop; raising is thin and invites reraises or calls by much stronger hands. With deep stacks and many hands left, I preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am in the big blind with a pocket pair of fours. The button, Claude, has limped into the pot, and the small blind has folded. While a small pocket pair has value, raising here might bloat the pot out of position against a player who could be trapping or simply seeing a cheap flop. Since there is no further action required to see the flop, checking is the most prudent move. This allows me to try and hit a set (set-mining) without committing any additional chips pre-flop.

I have flopped bottom set on an Ace-high board with two clubs. Since this was a limped pot pre-flop, the pot is currently small (250 chips), and I need to start building it immediately to maximize value. An Ace is very much in Claude's limping range, and he is likely to call a bet with any Ax, any 6x, or any club draw. By betting now, I protect my hand against potential draws and begin the process of playing for Claude's remaining stack.

Folding with 3♣9♣ on Ah 6c 4c. My hand is essentially air with no pair and no draws (no flush draw with single club, no straight possibilities). Flash's flop raise after checking pre-flop indicates strength. With minimal equity and a healthy 10,950 chip stack, I should preserve chips for better opportunities. 18 hands remain in the tournament - this is not a spot to gamble with air.

Button with Kc6d in a 3-handed game: this hand is marginal but acceptable to open from the button — position + king blocker + lots of fold equity against the blinds. Limping/calling 100 hands the SB/BB behind gives a multiway pot with a weak hand; better to take the initiative. I’ll open to a standard ~3x BB (300) to apply pressure, isolate, and keep post‑flop play in position. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet, call or continue cautiously vs a small 3‑bet, and c‑bet selectively on favorable boards (use position).

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raise from the Button. While ranges are wider in 3-handed play, calling 250 more with such a marginal hand is a negative expected value play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm in the Big Blind with K♠ 2♣ against Minni's raise to 300 from the button (Flash folded). K2 offsuit is one of the weakest holdings in poker—I have minimal equity even if I call. Key problems: (1) I'm completely out of position postflop, (2) Minni is aggressive and will likely c-bet most flops, (3) Even hitting a K leaves me vulnerable to better kickers, (4) My stack is healthy at 10,850, so there's no desperate need to gamble. While pot odds are 2.25:1 (450 to call 200), hand quality and position make this a clear fold. Preserving chips for premium spots is more valuable than chasing marginal spots with weak hands.

I am on the button with 4h2h. While it is suited, the hand is extremely weak (lowest possible suited gapper). In a three-handed game, opening ranges are wider, but both Claude and Minni have shown willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively. There is no need to risk chips on a marginal steal attempt when stacks are relatively deep and I am in a strong position in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Td7s is a marginal hand from the small blind. While the immediate cost is only 50 more chips (I already have 50 in), the fundamental issue is that this is a weak, off-suit hand that plays poorly out of position against two opponents with similar stack sizes. I'm facing the big blind (Minni) and potentially Flash, both of whom have healthy stacks (14,700 and 14,350 respectively). The 50 already committed to the small blind is a sunk cost. Playing weak hands out of position in a three-way pot typically results in difficult post-flop situations where I'll be at an informational disadvantage. With 17 hands left to play (150-134=16, plus this one=17), it's better to preserve chips and wait for stronger holdings in better positions rather than play marginal hands out of position. Folding is the disciplined choice.

KJ offsuit is a solid broadway hand with reasonable value from the button. The current odds are favorable (1.5:1 to call 100 into a 150 pot). I'm in position with good upside potential to make top pair or broadway cards. Minni is in the small blind and Flash is in the big blind. Given my current stack of 10,800 and that we're on hand 135 of 150, I should be making profitable marginal calls in position rather than folding passively. Calling 100 allows me to see the flop with positional advantage while preserving my flexibility post-flop.

I have 5♠2♠ in the small blind facing a limp from the button with the big blind yet to act. Calling 50 to see a flop puts me into a likely multiway, out-of-position pot where 5-2s has very poor equity and limited playability. A minimum raise to 200 is small and unlikely to fold out the limper or the big blind — it just bloats the pot and invites calls/3-bets with much stronger ranges. With 15 hands left and a healthy stack, conserving chips and waiting for a much better spot is the highest EV line here. Fold and preserve chips for stronger situations.

I am in the big blind holding 98o. The button has limped into the pot and the small blind has folded. Given the stack sizes and the mediocre strength of my hand, there is no need to raise and build a pot out of position. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and realize the equity of my connectors without further investment.

On this flop of Ah-9d-3h, I have middle pair (nines) with an eight kicker. The pot is small (250) and the action is checked to me by Claude, who limped on the button. Since I am in the Big Blind and checked pre-flop, my range contains many weak hands, but a nine is currently quite strong in a limped pot. Checking is a viable option to keep the pot small and induce bluffs, but betting for protection and value against hands like draws or lower pairs is also reasonable. However, given Claude's tendency to call or bet when checked to, I will check to see how he reacts, as an Ace is very much in his range as a limper or mine as a BB. I'll play the hand cautiously.

KJ is weak on an A-9-3 board. Flash checked showing weakness, but the Ace is dangerous and likely to be in their range. With 10,700 chips on hand 135/150 (endgame), preserving chips is critical. Checking allows me to see the turn for free and potentially improve, rather than risking chips on a semi-bluff with marginal equity. This is a prudent play in the critical final stages.

On the turn, a King appears, which is another overcard to my pair of nines. The flop was checked through, suggesting that Claude likely does not have a strong Ace, but the King is a card that frequently fits into a button limper's range (like K-x suited or marginal King-high hands). My pair of nines currently has some showdown value in a limped pot, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, as I would likely only be called by better hands (Ax, Kx) and fold out worse ones. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially see a cheap river or catch a bluff if Claude decides to stab at the pot with air.

I hold KJ which has improved to top pair (Kings) on the turn after the King of Clubs hit. Flash has shown weakness by checking both the flop and turn. However, the Ace on the board is a significant concern - Flash could easily hold an Ace from the BB. Given critical endgame factors: (1) I'm on hand 135/150 with only 15 hands remaining, (2) My stack of 10,700 is the shortest at the table, (3) Preserving chips becomes increasingly important in the final stretch, I should avoid marginal situations where I could lose significant chips. Betting into the Ace with top pair but no kicker (against a potentially trapping opponent) risks losing more chips than necessary. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially win a smaller pot if Flash was indeed showing weakness. This is conservative chip preservation poker in a critical stage of the tournament.

The board now has three overcards to my pair of nines (Ace, King, and Queen). Since the hand has been checked through on every street (Flop and Turn), it is unlikely Claude has a strong hand like an Ace or a King. The Queen on the river is another scare card, but my pair of nines still retains showdown value in a limped pot against his range of small pairs or air. Betting would likely only result in being called by better hands (like a Queen he checked twice) or folding out worse hands that I already beat. I will check to see the showdown for free.

River value bet with top pair. Flash has checked all three streets (flop, turn, river), indicating a weak hand. My KJ gives me King pair which is very likely the best hand against Flash's range. While an Ace is possible, Flash would have bet at least once to protect that hand from free cards. In the critical final 15 hands of the tournament, I need to be extractive when holding made hands on weak opponent lines. A 150-chip bet (into a 250 pot) gets value from weaker holdings while maintaining reasonable risk/reward. This is standard value-betting poker, not reckless aggression. If Flash has slowplayed an Ace, I lose the bet anyway - but their complete passivity makes that scenario unlikely.

In this limped pot, the action has been extremely passive, with both players checking through the flop and turn. The board now contains three overcards (Ace, King, Queen) to my pair of nines. Claude's bet on the river after checking twice suggests he might have caught a piece of the river (like a Queen) or is simply attempting to steal a small pot after seeing no aggression from me. Given the pot odds (150 to call into a 400 pot) and the fact that a pair of nines is a relatively strong hand in a limped, checked-down pot, I have enough showdown value to justify a call. I lose to any Queen, King, or Ace, but those hands often would have bet earlier or might check the river to see a cheap showdown themselves. Claude's bet size is small enough to warrant a 'keeping him honest' call.

I’m on the button with K5s — a playable, suited king that does well in position. With only the blinds to act and deep stacks, opening is standard to steal the blinds and play post‑flop in position. I’ll make a standard 3xBB open to 300 (total) — big enough to narrow ranges and consistent with my table raising pattern. If called I’ll c‑bet favorable flops (K‑high, flush/straight draws); if 3‑bet I’ll evaluate based on sizing and opponent (fold to large 3‑bets, consider calling small ones for position and implied odds).

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 2d, which is a very weak hand. Minni, who has been active on the button, has raised to 300. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position for the rest of the hand against a player who has shown aggression throughout the tournament, there is no reason to defend or contest this pot. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here to conserve chips.

K♦-J♠ is a solid hand heads-up against Minni's button raise. I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds and need only 31% equity, which K-J easily has against a wide button range. Calling is preferable to 3-betting with a marginal hand, and I'll see the flop with position to act second post-flop. My stack size (11000) is healthy enough to play this hand efficiently.

Betting K♦J♠ on 3♦-8♥-T♦ is correct. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) with two live overcards and a backdoor flush draw, giving me around 50%+ equity against most of Minni's button-raise range. With Minni checking to me, I should bet to take the initiative, build the pot with a hand that has multiple ways to improve, and apply pressure heads-up. A half-pot bet of 325 is aggressive enough to gain information and fold equity while maintaining good implied odds if called.

I’m on the button with K♠5♠ on a T♦ 8♥ 3♦ flop — I’ve missed but hold one overcard (K) and a backdoor spade possibility. Claude’s lead of 325 into a 975 pot is a small c‑bet representing a fairly wide range. Calling 325 gets me roughly 5:1 on the pot (need ~16.7% equity), and with position I can float and apply pressure on the turn or realize equity if I hit a K or a spade. A raise as a bluff is too thin with this holding (I’ll face tough decisions vs a 3‑bet or a shove), and folding would be too tight given the price and my post‑flop options. Plan: call, see the turn, and either bluff‑catch / barrel/lead depending on how Claude reacts and what card comes.

Betting the turn with my strong OESD (8 clean outs) plus two overcards and backdoor flush draw. The 5♣ doesn't help Minni's likely holdings from the button. I'm applying pressure to win the pot immediately or build it with a hand that has strong equity. Against Minni's range, my equity is 35-40%, which is above the break-even threshold for this bet size (need ~31% with 1900 total pot offering 2.17:1 odds). Continuing my aggressive line from the flop is appropriate with position and stack depth in my favor.

I called the flop as a position float and the turn gives me a pair (K♠5♠ with the 5♣ on the turn). Claude’s line (call pre, c‑bet flop, barrel turn) is consistent with both value (T/8/3, medium pairs, some two‑pair/trips combos) and missed diamond draws turned into a bluff. Pot odds to call 600 into a 1900 pot require ~24% equity — my pair has enough showdown value against his bluffs and many weaker pairs. Raising here would be thin: his range includes many hands that dominate my pair (top pair or better) and a raise could commit me to a bigger pot or run into a shove. With position I prefer to call, keep the pot controlled, and re‑evaluate the river (check/call small bets, fold to large shoves, or value‑bet if I improve). Calling preserves fold equity on later streets and extracts value from worse hands, so I will call the 600.

River check with K♦J♠ on 3♦-8♥-T♦-5♣-3♣. My hand has failed to improve to anything stronger than K-high. After betting aggressively on flop (325) and turn (600), the river brick doesn't support further aggression. Checking preserves chips and gives me reasonable showdown value against Minni's calling range. If Minni checks back, my K-high likely wins. If Minni bets, I have information to make a fold/call decision. This is the most prudent river decision with a marginal holding against an active opponent who has proven willing to call down.

River completed with 3♣ giving the board 3♦‑8♥‑T♦‑5♣‑3♣. I hold K♠5♠ — second pair (fives). Claude led/bet flop and turn, then checked the river. That check is ambiguous: it can be a missed diamond draw or weaker holdings he’s giving up with, but it can also be pot‑control with a T or even a disguised trips/full‑house trap. My hand has decent showdown value against a lot of his missed/medium hands, but it’s thin vs tens or trips and I don’t want to inflate the pot or fold out worse hands by bluffing. With position and no bet to call, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and see a showdown (check to induce a bluff or get called by worse; fold to a large shove if he surprises). I’ll check and take the pot to showdown rather than thin‑value bet or bluff into a polarized range.

I am on the button with 8s 9s, which is a strong speculative hand. In a three-handed game, opening with suited connectors from the button is standard to take control of the pot and utilize positional advantage. I will make a standard 3x opening raise to 300 chips.

With 2d-Jc in the small blind, this is one of the weakest holdings in poker. Flash has raised to 300 from the button, and I'm out of position with marginal cards. The risk/reward is poor here:

1. Hand Quality: Jack-deuce is a dominated hand that plays terribly out of position
2. Position: I'm in the small blind facing an in-position aggressor
3. Stack Pressure: With 9,825 chips, I need to preserve my stack and enter pots with stronger holdings
4. Risk of Minni Re-raising: The big blind could re-raise, putting me in an even worse spot with a weak hand
5. Pot Odds: While I'd be getting roughly 2:1 to call 250 into 450, the playability of this hand post-flop is so poor that the implied odds don't justify it

This is a clear fold. There's no upside to playing this hand weakly out of position. I'll preserve chips for better spots.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with 5♣4♠ (100 already posted). Calling requires 200 more (300 total) into a 450 pot; if I call the pot will be 650, so I’m risking 200 to win 650 — I need ~30.8% equity. Heads-up the button’s opening range is wide and my hand has some low-straight/backdoor equity and showdown value, so it’s worth defending. I’ll call and see a flop, but play carefully out of position: fold to heavy turn/river aggression without improvement, call small c-bets or continue with made pairs/draws, and avoid bloating the pot with just backdoors. A 3-bet here as a bluff is unnecessary with this holding.

I have two pair (5s and 4s) on 7s-5d-4c — a very strong made hand heads-up vs the preflop raiser’s wide range. Betting for value and protection is correct: I want calls from worse pairs, single-pair hands and floats while denying equity to the rare straight-combo draws (6+3, 8+6) and making it costly for random overcard floats. I’ll size to about half the pot to extract value without bloating the pot out of position: bet 325 into 650. Plan: if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and continue extracting on safe cards; if he check-raises a small amount I’ll likely call and turn-wise re-evaluate; if he jams or raises huge (polarized to trips/full-house) I’ll fold to avoid committing too many chips to a likely dominated situation.

On a flop of 7s 5d 4c, I have an open-ended straight draw with 8s 9s. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 325 into a 650 pot. This lead often indicates a marginal made hand or a draw. Given I have solid equity with my straight draw and am in position, calling is the most prudent play to see how the turn develops. This keeps the pot manageable while I look to hit one of my 8 outs.

I hold two pair (5s and 4s) on 7s-5d-4c-Ac — a very strong heads-up hand. Flash called my flop raise, so his calling range is wide (pairs, A-x, floats, some draws); the turn Ace helps his range but most A-x combos are still behind my two pair — only A5/A4/A7, sets or straights (rare) legitimately beat me. I should extract value and protect against the small number of turn/river outs, so I’ll bet for value. With the pot at 1,300 a half‑pot bet (~650) is big enough to charge A‑highs, worse pairs and floats while keeping worse hands interested. Plan: call a small raise and re-evaluate; fold to a very large raise/jam (polarized to trips/full house).

On a turn card of the Ace of Clubs (Ac), the board is now 7s-5d-4c-Ac. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any 6 or 10). Minni has bet 650 into a pot of 1950. To call, I need to put in 650, making the total pot 2600. This gives me pot odds of 4:1 (or 20% required equity). My current equity to hit the straight on the river is approximately 17.4% (8/46). While I am slightly behind on direct pot odds, the implied odds of hitting my straight against an aggressive opponent like Minni are significant. If I hit my straight on the river, I am likely to extract at least another 500-1000 chips, which makes calling the turn bet mathematically sound in the long run. Additionally, I have position, which allows me to check back if she checks the river or potentially bluff if the straight doesn't land but the board remains favorable. I will call.

I have two pair (5s and 4s) on 7s-5d-4c-Ac-Kd. Flash opened preflop and then called my flop (325) and turn (650) bets — his line is wide but contains many A-x, Kx, single pairs and some medium pocket pairs/sets. The turn Ace improved parts of his calling range, but my 5&4 two-pair still beats most of his holdings (most A-x and Kx are single pair and will call a value bet). Betting for value is correct. I’ll size to roughly half the pot (pot = 2600) — 1300 charges worse pairs, A-highs and Kx while avoiding overcommitting if I run into a raise. Plan: call a small river raise but fold to a large jam (polarized to sets/full houses).

On the river, the board is 7s-5d-4c-Ac-Kd. My hole cards are 8s-9s, meaning I have missed my open-ended straight draw and have only 9-high. Minni has led out on every single street (flop, turn, and river). This triple-barrel line into the pre-flop aggressor is extremely strong, especially since she continued betting when the Ace fell on the turn and the King fell on the river. Given that I have zero showdown value and Minni's line suggests a very strong made hand (like an Ace, two pair, or a set), a bluff is unlikely to succeed. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Raising A7o from the button in short-handed play (3-handed). Position is a huge advantage here, and A-high has solid equity. A standard 3x raise to 300 applies pressure to the blinds while giving me control of the pot. Late tournament stage warrants aggression from the button with this holding. This raise wins the pot outright some percentage of the time, and when called, I maintain positional advantage post-flop.

I hold 2♣5♥ — a very weak, easily dominated hand. Claude opened to 300 from the button and Flash (BB) still acts; the pot is 450 and it’s 250 to call. Although the call is only 2.5 BB, calling (or making a thin squeeze to 500) would leave me out of position against at least one active opponent with almost no equity and little post‑flop playability. With deep stacks I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot — folding is the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. Claude, on the Button, has raised to 300. While the pot odds are tempting in a three-handed game, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. With only 12 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack, there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand against a Button raise.

I'm on the button with A6o in a 3-handed spot — this is a hand I want to play aggressively from position. With blinds 50/100 (pot 150) there's a lot of fold equity to be seized and playing postflop in position is a big advantage. I want a standard 3x open to apply pressure but not overcommit; raising to 300 gives good fold equity vs the SB/BB and keeps the pot manageable with a marginal kicker. Plan: if I'm called I will c-bet favorable, dry A-high or low-card flops and proceed cautiously with marginal holdings (pot-control or check/back). If I face a large 3-bet I will usually fold A6o out of position (only consider calling very small 3-bets with pot/implied odds).

In this 3-handed scenario, I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven suited. Minni, who is currently the chip leader and has been playing aggressively, has opened for 300 from the Button. While Jh7h has some potential, playing a marginal suited gapper out of position against an aggressive big stack is often unprofitable. With only 12 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to conserve my stack and wait for a situation where I have either a stronger hand or positional advantage rather than calling and being forced to play a guessing game on most flops.

KQ is a premium hand in the big blind. Minni raised to 300 from the button with a large stack, indicating a potentially wide range. I'm getting good pot odds (need to call 200 with 450 in the pot), but I have initiative with a strong hand in a late-stage tournament (139/150).

A raise puts pressure on Minni and accomplishes several goals:
1. Takes down the pot immediately against a weaker range
2. If called, I'm in a stronger position with a premium hand
3. My stack (9,875) is adequate for a meaningful re-raise
4. This hand plays well in both pre-flop confrontation or post-flop play

Raising to 700 is appropriately aggressive - not too large to fold out better hands Minni might play, but substantive enough to apply real pressure.

Claude 3-bet to 700 after my button open to 300. Heads-up and in position, A♠6♣ is marginal but playable — it blocks some strong Ax combos and has decent post‑flop playability. 4‑betting as a bluff with this hand is too thin and unnecessary with deep effective stacks; folding surrenders positional advantage. I'll call the extra 400 to see a flop (700 total committed) and use position to control the pot post‑flop: bet for value if I hit an Ace or strong draw, otherwise check/call or fold to heavy pressure. If Claude 4‑bets large or jams, I will fold.

I hold trip queens on a paired QQ2 flop after raising pre-flop. Minni checked to me after calling my 700 pre-flop re-raise. This is the ideal situation—I have a premium hand with initiative. Betting extracts value from Minni's calling range while applying pressure. My stack depth (9,275) supports a meaningful bet that won't commit me too heavily. A 700 bet continues my aggressive narrative and is appropriate for extracting value on a flop where I'm likely ahead of Minni's entire range.

Claude 3‑bet preflop, so his range is weighted to strong hands (AA/KK/QQ/AK/AQs/QQx) but a half‑pot flop bet on 2c Qc Qd still contains some hands that missed and can be peeled off. My holding A♠6♣ has almost no showdown value (only Ace‑high) but I have position, an Ace blocker and a tiny backdoor club plan. Folding here surrenders position too cheaply; a raise is too thin given my very limited equity and the likelihood Claude has a lot of value in his 3‑betting range. Calling 700 (bringing my total commitment to 1,400) is the best line: I can float in position, see how Claude reacts on the turn, and either bluff‑catch/realize fold equity or fold to heavy turn pressure. If I hit an Ace or pick up a meaningful draw I’ll value/raise; if Claude barrels big on the turn and I haven’t improved I’ll let it go.

I hold K♣Q♥ on a board of 2♣-Q♣-Q♦-6♥, giving me trip queens with a king kicker—an extremely strong hand. This is the turn card, and I maintain a significant advantage in this hand. Minni has called both my pre-flop re-raise (700) and my flop bet (700), indicating a holding that warranted continued investment. Most likely scenarios include AQ, KQ, pairs, or draws. None of these hold more than 3 outs if drawing, and my hand is ahead of everything except a set of queens (which is highly unlikely given the action). My stack (8,575) is adequate to bet at value without overcommitting before a river decision. A bet of 1,200 accomplishes multiple goals: extracts value from Minni's calling range, applies continued pressure that may earn folds from weaker holdings or missed draws, and is appropriately sized relative to the growing pot (now 2,850). This sizing maintains aggression while keeping Minni's implied odds unfavorable for draw completion. With one card to come, I want to build value now rather than allow Minni a free river.

I improved to a pair of sixes (As6c → board 2c Qc Qd 6h). Claude 3‑bet preflop and has continued with a sizable flop bet and a turn bet, so his range is weighted to strong value (QQ/AA/KK, AQ/KQ, AK, sometimes JJ/TT) but will contain some missed AK/AQ bluffs. With position, an Ace blocker and a real but vulnerable second pair, folding is too nitty and a raise is far too thin — I don’t have two pair or a draw to semi‑bluff and I won’t get many better hands to fold. Pot is 4050; calling 1200 commits a small fraction of my 15,950 stack and gives ~22.9% pot‑odds (1200 into 5250), which is reasonable to realize equity vs Claude’s polarized-ish line. Plan: call to see the river, then decide — extract value or call small river bets versus bluffs, but fold to extreme river aggression unless I improve.

River value bet with trip queens and king kicker. The Ace is a scare card, but Minni's passive call-down pattern (never raising) suggests they don't hold an Ace. Most likely holdings are KQ (chop), pairs, or missed draws. A 1,500 bet accomplishes three goals: (1) extracts value from hands that will call (KQ, small pairs), (2) gets folds from complete misses, and (3) represents hand strength appropriately without overcommitting. With 7,375 chips remaining and the pot at 5,250, this sizing is optimal for late-stage tournament play—aggressive enough to build the pot, conservative enough to maintain stack flexibility for the final 11 hands.

I hold A♠6♣ — on the river the board is 2♣ Q♣ Q♦ 6♥ A♥, so I have two pair (Aces and Sixes). Claude 3‑bet preflop and barreled flop/turn, so his range is weighted toward very strong hands (QQ, AK, AQ, big pairs). The hands that beat me are primarily any Q in his hand (trips) or full houses (Q with A/6/2 or AQ); many standard 3‑bet hands (AK, KK) are actually behind my two‑pair. His river sizing (1,500 into a ~6,750 pot) is fairly small — consistent with a thin value or blocker bet rather than a polar shove. Raising here is too thin: it will get called or shoved by hands that crush me and will fold the weaker A‑hands I want value from. Calling 1,500 is cheap (I’m getting ~18% break‑even pot odds: 1,500 to win ~8,250) and preserves information/stack size while extracting value from worse A‑hands and bluffs. I’ll call to reach showdown and fold only to heavy shove/re‑raise if faced with it.

I am on the button with King-Nine offsuit (K9o) in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand from this position. I'll raise to 300 to either take down the blinds or play the pot in position against Claude or Minni.

With 2s-Jc (deuce-jack offsuit) in the small blind facing a button raise from Flash to 300, this is a clear fold situation. 

**Key factors:**
1. **Hand Strength**: Deuce-Jack offsuit is a weak, unconnected hand with minimal value
2. **Position**: Small blind is a weak position with poor initiative
3. **Action**: Flash raised from the button (wide range), but Minni in the big blind hasn't acted yet, suggesting potential strength
4. **Stack Health**: With 14,075 chips, I don't need to get involved in marginal spots
5. **Tournament Stage**: Hand 140 of 150 - I should preserve chips for better opportunities in the final hands

This is textbook small blind discipline - folding weak hands to positional raises when there's another opponent still to act. I can easily wait for premium holdings or better spots where my position is superior.

Flash (button) opened to 300; I’m in the big blind with Kd Th. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (300 total committed). Calling 200 into a 650 pot after my call gives a break-even equity of ~30.8%, which is easily attainable versus a typical button opening range. KTo is marginal but perfectly defendable heads-up with deep stacks (~131bb): it can make top pair, has decent showdown value and some backdoor straight/flush potential. A min-raise to 500 gains little fold equity and leaves me out of position on the flop; a larger 3-bet would be overcommitting with a hand this hand. Plan is to defend and see a flop — control the pot on dry boards, value-bet when I hit top pair, and fold to clear strength if I miss and face heavy aggression. Therefore: call (total 300).

Check. I have K♦T♥ with no pair on 5♣-5♥-4♣ — the flop is very dry and I’m out of position versus the button’s wide open-raise range. Betting for value is poor (I’m behind to most pairs/pocket pairs) and a probe bet will be called or check-raised too often, bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Checking preserves pot control and lets me call a reasonable c-bet to realize equity (overcards to a pair can still win by showdown or improve on the turn). Plan: call small continuation bets and fold to large pressure; if opponent checks back, freely take a turn card to try to hit K/T or take a free stab later. Therefore I check now to keep the pot manageable and reevaluate on the turn.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a paired board (5c 5h 4c). This board texture is generally favorable for the pre-flop raiser, as it is difficult for the defender in the big blind to have a strong hand. I'll place a continuation bet of 300 to take down the pot immediately and put pressure on hands like overcards or weak draws.

Pot is 950 and Flash bets 300; calling 300 into 950 requires ≈24% equity (300/(950+300)=300/1250≈24%). My K♦T♥ has two overcards and reasonable showdown equity vs a button’s wide c-bet range (many A-highs/ K-highs/ missed broadways and bluffs). I checked to control the pot and indicated willingness to call small continuation bets — a raise OOP with KTo is thin (will get called by better and fold out only the worst), and folding here surrenders too much given the pot odds. I'll call 300 to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and reevaluate on the turn (fold to clear, large turn aggression if I miss; value-bet/higher aggression if I pair up).

Check. I hold K♦T♥ on 5♣-5♥-4♣-4♦ — I have no pair and the board is paired twice, giving many opponent hands (any 5 or 4, pocket pairs) full-house or near-full-house potential. Flash was the preflop raiser and continued on the flop, so his range includes many hands that connect well here; betting or raising OOP as a bluff is thin and will face calls or a shove from hands that already beat me. With deep stacks and a large pot (1250), my priority is pot control and avoiding bloating the pot with a worthless K-high. I’ll check and proceed to the river; plan to fold to any meaningful river aggression and only consider a very small river bluff if a blank falls and Flash shows weakness (checks) — otherwise keep chips intact.

The board has now double-paired (5-5-4-4). My King-high has some showdown value against missed draws or lower overcards, but it is unlikely to fold out a better hand (like an Ace-high or a small pocket pair) given that the board texture is so static. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and see a free river, potentially winning at showdown if the final card is a blank and the action remains checked through.

The river is 5♣-5♥-4♣-4♦-4♠ — the board itself is a full house (44455). I hold K♦T♥, which has no meaningful showdown improvement: any opponent holding a 4 (the remaining 4) has quads and any opponent holding a 5 has a higher full house. Flash was the preflop raiser and c-bet the flop, so his line is highly consistent with hands that now beat me (5x, a 4, or a pocket pair that improved). Betting here would be a pure bluff with very thin fold equity (my bet will be called by anything that beat me and only fold the absolute air), so the lowest-risk, highest-expected-value line is to check and take it to showdown. If Flash now bets, I will fold to significant aggression since my hand has essentially no river equity versus his likely holdings.

The board has completed a full house of Fours full of Fives (4-4-4-5-5). My hand, King-Nine, does not improve upon this, so I am playing the board. Any pocket pair Sixes or higher, or any hand containing a Five or the final Four, would have a superior hand. Since Minni checked, there is no value in betting; I will only be called by hands that beat me or hands that split the pot with me. I will check to take the showdown and likely split the pot.

I'm on the button with AQ offsuit against the small blind (Minni) and big blind (Flash). This is a strong hand, especially in position. With 14,075 chips and both opponents having similar stacks (~13k), I should raise to define my hand's strength and take down the pot immediately or play with positional advantage post-flop. A standard 3x BB raise is appropriate here. This gives me initiative and forces opponents to make decisions against a strong range. AQ is too strong to limp or just call—I want to build the pot when ahead and potentially win immediately.

I have QQ? Actually I hold pocket Tens (ThTs) in the small blind facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it’s 250 to call. TT is a strong hand that I want to play aggressively preflop: calling keeps too many worse hands and invites squeezes or multiway pots out of position. I want value and to isolate the button (and discourage a loose BB plug). 3‑bet sizing around 3–4x the open is appropriate with our deep stack, so I’ll raise to 1,000 total (≈3.3x the open). That builds the pot when I’m often ahead, gives decent fold equity, and keeps postflop play manageable. Plan: proceed postflop depending on texture — c‑bet favorable boards, check/defend on coordinated boards; vs a small 4‑bet I will consider calling and play postflop, vs a very large 4‑bet (near commitment) I’ll fold or re-evaluate depending on opponent tendencies.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5h 3d. Claude (Button) raised and Minni (Small Blind) 3-bet to 1000. My hand is extremely weak and faces significant action from both opponents. There is no reason to continue with this hand. I will fold.

Calling 700 more (total 1000 to match Minni's raise). AQ is a strong hand in late tournament play. By calling, I retain positional advantage on the button and keep the pot manageable while seeing the flop. This allows me to evaluate the board and make informed decisions postflop based on texture and Minni's actions. Against Minni's likely re-raise range, I'm in a reasonable spot - ahead of many hands, behind premium pairs, but with good position to control the hand.

I 3‑bet pre with TT to isolate; on this K♠‑6♣‑5♠ flop I’m first to act. My tens are a medium‑strength hand that beat most of Claude’s non‑K calling range (broadways, weaker pairs, A‑x) but lose to Kx. The board is relatively dry — a standard continuation bet is appropriate to extract value and deny free cards to backdoor draws while folding out some worse overcards. I’ll make a roughly half‑pot c‑bet (≈1000 into the 2100 pot). If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (keep betting small for value on safe cards, pot‑control or fold to heavy aggression if a K/very strong action appears).

AQ offsuit on a King-high flop (Ks 6c 5s) is a marginal holding but warrants continuation given favorable circumstances. I currently hold ace-high with two over-cards (A and Q), giving me approximately 30-40% equity against Minni's likely range of strong holdings (pairs, KQ/KJ). Pot odds are favorable at 1000 to win 4,100 total pot (4.1:1 odds). Positional advantage as button allows me to make informed decisions after seeing turn action. If I whiff, I still retain 12,075 chips with room to reassess. Folding AQ is overly tight with these odds and my equity; raising is unjustified without better board connectivity or a concrete read. By calling, I keep the pot manageable while preserving my tournament life and maintaining optionality. I expect to win this hand if an ace or queen hits the turn, or can proceed cautiously if the board stays dry.

Check. I 3‑bet pre with TT and c‑bet the flop ~1k; Claude called, so his range is wide and includes Kx, medium pairs, and some pocket pairs/draws. The 9♣ on the turn doesn’t help my tens — it actually improves parts of his range (pocket 9s to trips, and some club combos) and still leaves Kx and sets beating me. Betting here buys folds from a few worse hands but gives significant fold equity to hands I still beat only thinly while exposing me to check‑raises from hands that dominate me. With ~11k effective stacks and a 4.1k pot, I prefer pot control with a marginal but vulnerable hand. Plan: check and call reasonable small/medium bets to see a river; fold to a large check‑raise or polarizing shove unless I have a strong read to the contrary. If Claude checks back I’ll evaluate the river and look to get value on safe cards or fold to strong aggression.

Minni checked the turn after betting the flop, which indicates weakness or a hand that's uncomfortable after the 9c came. My AQ offsuit with ace-high is a strong candidate to be ahead of her likely holdings (medium pairs, AJ, KQ, K-high hands). 

Key considerations:
1. Minni's pre-flop 3-bet range (likely QQ+, AK, AQ, KK, KQ) now facing a checked turn suggests either weakness or slowplay
2. The 9c on turn is a reasonable card that doesn't obviously help her range
3. My position on the button allows me to apply pressure effectively
4. Pot odds favor a bet-fold scenario or getting called by weaker holdings
5. Stack sizes are relatively balanced (~11-12k each), so a 2,500 bet (60% of pot) represents significant but not catastrophic pressure

This bet accomplishes multiple goals:
- Wins the pot immediately against checking range weakness
- Gets value from worse holdings that call
- Maintains tournament momentum with AQ's showdown equity if called
- Preserves my tournament life with ~9,500 chips remaining if she shoves/calls and wins

The bet size of 2,500 is optimal—large enough to apply real pressure but not so large that I'm committed if she shoves. This is standard aggressive play from the button with a strong hand in position.

Fold. Line: I 3-bet pre and c‑bet the flop; Claude called both streets and now fires a large turn bet (2,500 into a 4,100 pre‑bet pot ≈61% bet). Pot math: pot before the turn bet was ~4,100, his bet makes it ~6,600; calling 2,500 would commit me to a final pot of ~9,100, so I need ~27.5% equity (2,500/9,100) to break even. My tens have only two clean outs (two remaining tens) and have very little equity vs a value‑heavy line (Kx, sets, or a turned 9 for trips). Claude’s line is highly weighted to value and my hand is reverse‑dominated; raising to 5,000 or shoving is too risky because I get called by better hands and commit a big portion of my stack with very poor equity. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

On the button with 8♣3♣ — a weak hand but suited and playable in position. Facing only the blinds (pot 150) I want to take advantage of position and fold equity rather than see a cheap multiway flop where 8-3s performs poorly. A standard 3x open (300 total) is a good size here: it’s big enough to discourage loose calls/steals, preserves postflop playability in position, and still allows an easy fold vs a 3-bet. If we get called we’ll proceed postflop in position; if we face a raise we’ll fold and preserve chips. Calling 100 gives poor multiway equity and folding is too tight, so I raise to 300 to try to steal the blinds and leverage position.

Pocket sixes is a strong hand in three-handed play, especially against a button open. I will 3-bet to 1,000 to take the initiative, put pressure on Minni, and discourage Claude from entering the pot from the big blind. This mirrors a successful play I made with the same hand in Hand #100.

I'm in the big blind with 2c4d, one of the weakest possible hands. The action has already shown significant aggression: Minni raised to 300 from the button, and Flash re-raised to 1,000 from the small blind. Now I'm facing a 900-chip call to compete in a pot that's already inflated with two aggressive opponents who likely have strong holdings.

Key considerations:
1. **Hand strength**: 2-4 offsuit is near the bottom of the poker hand rankings with virtually no premium equity
2. **Position**: Out of position post-flop, which makes this hand even more problematic
3. **Aggression**: The pre-flop action (raise + re-raise) indicates both opponents have quality hands
4. **Tournament stage**: With 9 hands remaining and a healthy 16,075 stack, there's no reason to gamble here
5. **Pot odds**: Even if favorable, they don't justify playing a hand this weak against this much aggression

This is a textbook fold. I should preserve my chip stack for better opportunities in the remaining 9 hands.

Fold. I opened to 300 with 8♣3♣ intending to use position, but Flash 3‑bet to 1000. There’s 700 to call into a 1400 pot (~33% pot odds). 8‑3s is at the very bottom of my range and performs poorly vs a SB 3‑bet range (overpairs, broadways, suited Aces, and some bluffs). Calling requires ~33% equity but 8‑3s has significantly less vs a 3‑bet range and is often dominated. A 4‑bet would be too polar and commit chips with junk (min‑raise to 1700 is nontrivial). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot in position, so fold.

In a 3-handed game, King-Four offsuit (K4o) is a standard opening hand from the button. I want to put pressure on the blinds (Claude and Minni) to pick up the pot pre-flop. A standard 3x raise is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take control of the hand with positional advantage.

6-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. From the small blind, facing an aggressive button raise, this is a straightforward fold. Flash has demonstrated aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament with frequent raises and bets. Playing out of position against a stronger range with such a marginal hand is poor risk/reward, especially with only 8 hands remaining. I need to preserve my stack and await better spots.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with J♦9♠ (offsuit). Pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total) to see the flop. With ~10.9k effective stacks this is a cheap defend: J9o has reasonable equity vs a wide button opening and good straight/broadway potential. Calling gives the right mix of pot control and implied odds — 200 into 650 after my call requires ~31% equity to break even. 3-betting OOP with this hand is too loose and folding surrenders too much dead money. Plan: call to 300, see the flop and proceed based on texture (continue with pairs/strong draws, fold to heavy pressure when missed).

I have J♦9♠ on K♣ J♣ 9♣ — that's two pair (Jacks and Nines) heads-up, which is a very strong holding. The major vulnerability is the monotone club board: any opponent with two clubs already has a made flush, and many one-club combos have a single-card flush draw (very relevant on later streets). Flash opened wide from the button, so his range contains some Q-T and club combos that can beat me, but the portion of his range that already has me crushed is small. I want to charge one-pair hands (Kx, Jx) and club draws while denying free cards, so I’ll bet for value and protection — roughly 60% pot. Bet size ~400 chips gives good pricing vs draws and keeps worse hands calling. If he jams/raises big I’ll assume a polarized range (flush/sets/straights) and re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme pressure); if he calls I’ll proceed to the turn and re-evaluate.

On a flop of 9c-Jc-Kc, I have top pair with a weak kicker (4d). Minni has led out for 400 into a 650 pot. This 'donk' bet could signify a King with a better kicker, a draw (like a flush or straight draw), or even a made hand like two pair or a set. However, 3-handed, folding top pair to a single bet is too exploitable. Raising would be unwise as it likely only gets called or raised by hands that have me beat. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the board's progression and Minni's further actions.

I have two pair (Jacks and Nines) on K♣ J♣ 9♣ T♦. That’s a strong hand but extremely vulnerable: the flop already had three clubs, so any single club in Flash’s hand is a made flush, and the turn T gives Q-combos a Broadway straight. Flash was the preflop raiser and called my flop bet, so his continuing range is weighted toward club combinations and broadway/K/J/T holdings. With ~10k effective stacks and a 1,450 pot, I don’t want to inflate the pot and face a check-raise or jam from a club-heavy polarized range. I’ll check to control the pot and keep the option to call a reasonable bet for value (extract vs Kx/Jx/9x) while avoiding committing to a bloated pot when I’m likely behind. If he checks back I’ll go to the river with pot control; if he bets a modest amount I’ll call; I’ll fold to very large, polarized raises that strongly represent a flush or straight.

The turn card (Ten of Diamonds) is extremely dangerous as it completes many possible straights (any Queen). While I have top pair, my kicker is very weak, and the board is heavily coordinated with both straight and flush possibilities. Minni's check after leading the flop could indicate she is slowing down with a marginal hand or potentially trapping with a made straight. Betting here offers little value because better hands will call or raise, and worse hands will likely fold. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and see a river for free to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

Check. I have two pair (J9) which is a strong showdown hand but very vulnerable on this runout: the board is K K J T 9 with three clubs on the flop, so a lot of plausible hands beat me — any single King in Flash’s hand is now trips, any Queen made the straight on the turn, any two clubs in hand were already a flush on the flop, and pocket 9s/Js turn into full houses against the paired board. Flash’s line (open pre, call my flop bet, check turn) is consistent with a number of these hands as well as with weaker Jx/9x hands, so a river bet would either get called/raised by hands that beat me or only get value from worse hands I can already beat at showdown. With no bet to call and a pot I don’t want to bloat further into many dominated scenarios, I’ll check and take it to showdown. If Flash now bets a small amount I will call for value; I’ll fold to an overbet jam that strongly represents a full house/flush given the board texture.

On the river, the board has paired the King, giving me three of a kind. However, the board is extremely coordinated with three clubs (flush possible) and a four-to-a-straight texture (any Queen makes a straight). Minni's check on the river, following her check on the turn, suggests she either has a marginal hand she's looking to take to showdown or she's playing a very strong hand (like a straight or flush) cautiously. Since better hands (straights, flushes, or full houses) are unlikely to fold and worse hands are unlikely to call a bet on such a dangerous board, checking back is the most prudent play to ensure I reach showdown and potentially split the pot or win against a weaker hand without risking a check-raise.

Fold 54o on the button. While position is excellent, this is a trash hand that creates unfavorable situations post-flop. With only 6 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack, I should wait for premium holdings rather than get involved with marginal trash hands. Preservation and patience are key in the final stretch.

I'm in the small blind with Ks2h and the button has folded, so it's heads-up vs the big blind (Flash). To call is 50 more (100 total committed). K2o is a marginal hand out of position — it can be dominated by Ax and better Kx and has poor playability postflop. A min-raise to 200 could steal the blind sometimes, but Flash has shown a tendency to defend/call and my raise would have limited fold equity while risking larger multi-street OOP decisions. With a healthy stack and only a small amount to complete, the +EV line is to complete to 100, see a flop cheaply, and fold to significant aggression unless I improve. I'll call the 50 to complete and proceed cautiously on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six of clubs. After the Button folds, the Small Blind limps into the pot. I have a hand with some post-flop potential and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. Checking back is a solid, low-variance play that allows me to see a flop for free and realize my equity.

I have top pair (Ks) but a very weak 2-kicker on Td-9c-Kh. Heads-up this is frequently the best hand, but there are QJ straight draws and hands with better Kx that can punish me. Flash checked from the big blind — an ambiguous range that contains worse pairs (T/9), weaker Ks, and draws. I prefer to extract value and deny free equity rather than check and give free cards. I’ll make a standard protection/value bet of 100 (≈1/2 pot). That size gets called by worse and charges QJ straight draws without bloating the pot too much out of position. Plan: fold to a large check‑raise (representing two pair/set/better K), call small raises very selectively, and if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and opponent action.

On a flop of Td-9c-Kh, I have a gutshot straight draw with my Jack (needing any Queen) and a backdoor flush draw with my two clubs. Minni bet 100 into a pot of 200, making the total pot 300. I need to call 100, which gives me 4:1 pot odds. Given that I have position and decent realization equity with my draws, calling is a standard and mathematically sound play.

Check. The A♠ on the turn significantly reduces the value of my K2: Flash called my flop c‑bet, so his calling range contains lots of hands that now beat me (A‑x, Kx with better kickers, two‑pair/set combinations and even straights like QJ). I’m out of position with a weak kicker and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal one‑pair holding. Pot control and stack preservation are the goals here — check to avoid committing chips and to let villain show their hand. Plan after this check: if Flash checks back we go to the river and re‑evaluate; if he bets I’ll fold to a sizable/clear value sizing and only continue to small probe bets that look like bluffs or thin probes (use bet size and timing to decide on a selective call).

On the turn, the board is Td-9c-Kh-As. I hold Jc-6c, giving me a gutshot straight draw (any Queen makes the Broadway straight). Minni has checked to me. While I could bet to try and take the pot down, checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity if a Queen falls. Given the connected nature of the board and the fact that an Ace is now present, Minni might have checked a variety of hands, including marginal made hands or draws. Checking back is the most cost-effective way to proceed with my current hand strength.

Check. I c‑bet the flop and was called, but the A♠ on the turn drastically reduced the value of my K2o — many hands in Flash’s calling range (Ax, Kx with better kickers, two‑pair/sets and QJ straights) now beat me. I checked the turn for pot control as planned; both players checked the river. Betting now would be thin value at best (trying to get called by TT/99) and risks getting raised or calling down by worse Kx/Ax combos. Pot control and stack preservation are the priority with a weak kicker OOP, so I’ll check through to showdown and avoid bloat or a tough raise/fold decision on the river.

The river is a 7s, making the final board Td-9c-Kh-As-7s. I currently hold Jack-high (Jc-6c), which has no showdown value. Minni has checked for the second consecutive street after betting the flop. While her checks could indicate a weak hand or a missed draw, she did show interest on the flop, suggesting she may have a marginal piece of the board like a pair of nines or tens. Given the small pot size (400) and the fact that I checked back the turn, it is difficult to represent a strong hand convincingly. Attempting a bluff here is unlikely to succeed against a 'bluff-catcher' pair, so checking back and conceding the pot is the safest way to preserve my stack as the tournament nears its end.

I’m on the button with K3 hearts (suited K) and both blinds are deep relative to my stack (~105bb). This is a clear spot to open-raise and take the initiative — K3s is marginal but has blocker value and good post‑flop playability in position. Table standard preflop sizing has been around 3x here, so I’ll raise to 300 total to apply pressure and isolate heads‑up. Plan: if I get a small 3‑bet I can consider a call depending on sizing, but I’ll fold to a large 3‑bet or shove — otherwise play postflop aggressively in position (c‑bet favorable textures, check back dry boards to control pot).

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand, especially against a Button raise. Minni has been frequently opening from the button with a wide range. By 3-betting to 1000, I take the initiative, put pressure on Minni's wide opening range, and discourage Claude in the Big Blind from entering the pot with a speculative hand. This sizing is consistent with my previous successful 3-bets against Minni.
