Calling 900 with K♦J♠ from the big blind. I have the largest stack, will have position post-flop, and the pot odds are favorable. KJ is a solid hand in short-handed endgame play, and calling allows me to see a flop while maintaining my chip lead. Flash's 3-bet from SB could contain many hands in this context.

I opened to 300 as planned, but Flash 3‑bet to 1000 and Claude cold‑called, leaving me in a 3‑bet multiway pot. The pot is 2300 and it costs 700 to continue — I’d need roughly 23% equity to justify a call. K♥3♥ is a marginal hand that is often dominated (or has poor equity) versus a 3‑bet range and a cold‑caller's calling range, and multiway pots greatly reduce its post‑flop playability. A 4‑bet here would be wildly aggressive and unnecessary with this hand and stack depths. Preserve chips for better spots — fold and wait for a cleaner spot in position or fewer opponents.

On a flop of 9d-6c-5h, holding Ace-Nine gives me top pair with the best possible kicker. This board is relatively safe, though it does have some connectivity for straight draws like 78 or 47. Since Claude called a 3-bet from the big blind, his range likely includes medium pocket pairs, suited connectors, or broadway cards. I have the range advantage as the pre-flop 3-bettor and the current best hand. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 1,150, which is half the pot, to extract value from hands like 77, 88, or any straight draws, and to protect my hand against potential overcards on the turn.

Calling with K♦J♠ on 9♦ 6♣ 5♥. Excellent pot odds (3:1) and solid equity against Flash's 3-bet range with two overcards and multiple straight draw possibilities. Flash's bet sizing suggests a continuation bet. As chip leader in the endgame, I can see another card to develop my hand while maintaining my stack advantage.

On a turn of 2s, the board remains relatively unchanged as the deuce is a blank that doesn't complete the primary straight draws (78, 47). I still hold top pair with the best kicker (TPTK). Claude's call on the flop suggests he has a piece of the board, such as a lower Nine, a medium pocket pair like 77 or 88, or a straight draw. To continue extracting value from these hands and to protect against the river, I will place a second barrel. A bet of 2,300 (half the pot) is large enough to make draws pay an unprofitable price while allowing me to keep the initiative and gather more information about the strength of Claude's range.

With K♦J♠ on a 9♦6♣5♥2♠ board, I have excellent pot odds (3:1) and significant equity. I have multiple outs including: any K or J for top/middle pair (6 outs), any Q or T for broadway straight (8 outs), any 8 or 7 for a nine-high straight (8 outs), and any 4 or 3 for a wheel (8 outs). This gives me approximately 20-30 realistic outs (accounting for some overlap and potential blocking), translating to roughly 45-55% equity against Flash's likely range.

At 3:1 pot odds needing only 25% equity, this is a clear call. Flash's bet sizing on the turn suggests a continuation bet or medium-strength hand. As chip leader, I can afford to see the river and develop my strong drawing hand. The river card will likely complete one of my many possible draws or give me showdown value with overcards.

On the river, the Ace of Clubs completes my two pair (Aces and Nines). While this is an exceptionally strong hand, the board remains somewhat coordinated; a straight with 7-8 is a distinct possibility given Claude's willingness to call pre-flop and on the flop/turn. Having led the betting on every street thus far, I will now check to Claude. This serves two purposes: it protects me from a potential raise if he has the straight or a set (55 or 66), and it may induce a bluff from missed draws (like 78 or medium pocket pairs turned into bluffs). If Claude bets, I have a clear call given the high value of my two pair, but checking allows me to control the final pot size and ensure I reach a showdown.

Checking down with K-high on the river. Flash's check indicates weakness - the river ace likely scared them off an overpair or medium pair. With a weak hand like K-high, I should go to showdown rather than bluff. As chip leader with only 5 hands remaining, preserving my stack is more important than risk-taking. K-high has showdown value and might actually win if Flash has nothing. Betting risks chips unnecessarily against Flash's likely stronger holdings or would only win if Flash folds, but checking to showdown is the optimal endgame strategy here.

I am on the button with 2c 4c, which is a very weak starting hand. Although I have a significant chip lead and could theoretically attempt a steal, both Claude and Minni have shown willingness to defend their blinds and play post-flop. With only five hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to risk chips on such a low-equity hand. I will choose to fold and wait for better opportunities to maintain my lead.

AEo from the small blind heads-up is a premium hand. With only 4 hands remaining in the tournament, chip accumulation is critical. Minni is in the big blind with a marginally weaker stack (10,075 vs my 11,525). A raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is aggressive but appropriate - it defines my hand strength, applies pressure to Minni's position, and allows me to build the pot with a hand that has strong equity. In end-stage poker with shortened fields, position and aggression are maximized values.

Call to 250. I’m in the big blind with A♠4♠ facing Claude’s 2.5x SB open to 250; I only need 150 more into a 350 pot (calling gives ~30% pot odds). A4s has good raw equity vs a typical SB opening range (backdoor flush + ace blocker) and plays well postflop, so I prefer to defend rather than inflate the pot out of position with a small 3-bet (min-3bet to 400 has too little fold equity; a larger 3-bet commits more OOP). Plan: see the flop and continue when I hit an Ace, a strong spade draw, or have a good realization line; otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy aggression OOP.

Out of position with Ad8h on Qh5h3c - a marginal holding with some outs but weak current strength. Checking allows me to see Minni's action and gather information before committing more chips. With shallow stacks and 4 hands remaining, selective aggression is preferable to overcommitting with weak holdings. This preserves my range flexibility and lets Minni act first, potentially winning the pot if they check behind or making decisions based on their bet.

I defend the big blind preflop with A♠4♠ and now face a checked flop Q♥5♥3♣. Claude’s SB open range is wide and this particular flop misses most of it. I’m in position with only Ace-high plus a backdoor spade/fold equity possibility — that’s a hand I want to realize cheaply rather than bloat the pot out of position. Checking keeps the pot small (pot control), lets me use position to see how Claude reacts on the turn, and preserves the option to barrel turn cards (an A or a spade) for value/bluff. Plan: check now; call a small-to-medium bet to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn if I pair the Ace or pick up a spade, but fold to heavy aggression since Ace-high has limited showdown value if Claude barrels hard.

Check on the turn with Ad8h on Qh 5h 3c 6h. Current hand is Ace-high but I have strong drawing equity (9 flush outs + 6 pair outs). Both players checked the flop, indicating weakness. By checking, I keep the pot manageable, avoid committing chips with a marginal holding, remain flexible for river decision-making, and can see if Minni's turn action reveals their hand strength. With 4 hands remaining and mediocre current equity, controlled aggression is preferable to speculative overcommitment.

Check. I have A♠4♠ on Q♥5♥3♣6♥ after calling preflop and playing for pot control. The turn completed a three‑heart board and eliminated my backdoor spade equity — I currently have only ace‑high (roughly 6 outs to pair by the river, ~12–14%). Claude’s line (open pre, then check–check) looks passive and consistent with a medium or missed hand rather than a made monster, so a bet here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity and only gets called by lots of worse or medium hands. With deep stacks and a small pot I want to keep this cheap: check to the river, and only continue if I improve (pair the A/4) or face a very small river bet; otherwise fold to significant aggression.

River check with Ad8h on Qh 5h 3c 6h Kd. Holding only ace-high (very marginal). Both players checked flop and turn, suggesting weakness from both sides. Betting weak holdings on river is poor strategy—I'd only get called by better hands and fold out worse hands. Checking wins the pot if Minni checks behind with a weaker hand, and with only 4 hands remaining and shallow stacks, pot control is critical. This preserves chips for stronger opportunities while allowing ace-high to potentially win at showdown.

Board: Qh 5h 3c 6h Kd. I hold A♠4♠ and never improved — my turn backdoor spade is gone and the K on the river doesn't help. Claude opened from the SB then checked flop/turn/river, a very passive line consistent with a missed hand, small pair or a medium showdown hand. Betting as a bluff here has poor fold equity (many Kx/pocket pairs and Qx will simply call) and I get very little value when called. Pot is small (500) relative to our stacks; the EV of a thin river bluff is negative versus a competent caller. I preserve chips and avoid a marginal bluff: check and take the showdown (or fold to a significant river lead).

I'm holding AQ of different suits (Ace-Queen), one of the strongest starting hands in poker. I'm on the button with excellent position and a healthy stack of 11,825 chips. This is a straightforward premium hand situation.

With AQ, I should be raising from the button to:
1. Build the pot with a strong hand
2. Apply pressure to the blinds from position
3. Potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play a favorable heads-up situation post-flop

A standard 3x BB raise is appropriate here. With blinds at 50/100, I'll raise to 300 total chips. This is aggressive but not excessive, and gives me good risk-reward. We're near the end of the tournament (hand 147 of 150), so accumulating chips is important.

Minni is shorter-stacked and may fold or call, while Flash is the chip leader. Either way, I'm in a favorable position with this premium hand from the button.

I'm in the small blind with JcTc facing a button open to 300. With ~99BB effective stacks this is a playable suited broadway but I'm out of position vs the raiser; a small 3-bet (min to 500) is an option, but I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize equity with JTs. Calling the additional 250 (300 total committed including my posted small blind) gives decent pot odds (≈36% break-even) versus Claude's opening range and preserves stack depth to extract value on favorable flops or fold to heavy action. Plan: see the flop, continue with top pair, strong draws or good equity boards; fold to large, disciplined aggression if I miss and face significant resistance.

We are currently on hand 147 of 150, and I have a significant chip lead. With a stack of 18,150 compared to Claude's 11,525 and Minni's 9,625, my primary objective is to avoid unnecessary risks and preserve my position. I am holding Queen-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand to play in a three-way pot from out of position. Although I am getting good pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 900), the risk of being dominated or finding myself in a difficult post-flop situation outweighs the potential reward of playing such a marginal hand this late in the tournament. I will fold and wait for better opportunities or simply let the clock run out on my lead.

Flop 4d 9h 2h — I have JcTc and completely missed: no pair, no hearts, only very remote backdoor straight possibilities. Claude was the preflop raiser and on a dry, two-heart board he’s likely to continuation-bet a high frequency. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so bluffing or leading here is unnecessary risk. Plan: check to keep the pot small and preserve stack depth; fold to any sizable c-bet and only continue with a very small c-bet to float or if I improve to a jack/ten or pick up a clear plan on the turn. If Claude checks back I can consider a delayed c-bet on the turn.

I'm holding AQ (Ace-Queen), which is a premium hand that won me the pre-flop. The flop of 4d-9h-2h is relatively dry and doesn't hit many typical calling ranges strongly. Minni checked from the small blind after calling my pre-flop raise, indicating weakness or a mediocre hand.

This is a standard continuation bet situation. The board texture favors my range - it's unlikely to have improved hands like sets or two pairs, and Minni's check suggests she missed or has a medium-strength hand. 

A bet of 450 (approximately 65% of the 700 pot) is appropriately sized - it's strong enough to apply meaningful pressure and extract value from weaker holdings, while not being so large that it commits too much of my stack relative to the pot size.

We're on hand 147 of 150, so maintaining and growing my stack is important. This bet has good expected value - either I take down the pot immediately or get value from weaker hands while maintaining favorable odds with my strong holding.

I’m OOP with JcTc on a very dry 4♦-9♥-2♥ flop. I checked and Claude put me to a 450 call into a 1150 pot. Calling 450 only gets ~28% pot odds. My hand has no hearts and only 6 outs to pair (≈24% to hit a pair by the river) — slightly worse than the break-even equity — and I’ll be playing multi-street OOP into deep stacks if I continue. A raise here would be a fancy bluff with poor blockers and large downside. Consistent with my preflop plan to keep the pot manageable and fold to sizable C-bets when I miss, the correct play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

9♦4♥ is a very weak hand — normally a fold. However I’m on the button with ~96BB effective and the call is only 100 into a 150 pot: cheap price to see a flop in position against two blinds. I don’t want to open-raise this garbage (would invite 3-bets or bigger contests and I can’t play a bloated pot with junk), so I’ll take a free flop and use position to fold to heavy action or try to steal later. Plan: call 100 now; fold to a sizable 3-bet preflop, and postflop play depends on texture (c-bet small on very favorable flops, otherwise check/fold to aggression).

I am currently holding Jack of Clubs and Ten of Clubs, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. With only three hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial chip lead, I want to maintain pressure on my opponents while also capitalizing on the strength of my starting hand. Minni limped from the button, which often indicates a wide or marginal range. By raising to 400, I take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially force a fold from the Big Blind (Claude) or the limper, allowing me to take down the blinds and the limp pre-flop.

This is Hand #148 with 3 players remaining in a 150-hand tournament. I hold 3d 4s (34o), the worst hand in poker, facing a raise from Flash to 400 with Minni having called.

Despite receiving 2:1 pot odds (600 pot, 300 to call), 34o has virtually no equity against a raising range. In a three-way pot, this hand will be dominated by the vast majority of hands opponents would raise or call with. The tournament position (hand 148 of 150) makes chip preservation important, and burning chips with a drawing-dead hand does not advance my win equity.

Even in a late-stage tournament where fold equity matters, 34o cannot generate sufficient fold equity to justify a marginal raise, and calling would put me in a weak position post-flop with minimal hand strength.

Folding is the mathematically sound and standard play.

I called 100 preflop intending to see a cheap flop in position with 9♦4♥, but Flash’s raise to 400 after my call makes it a sizable piece of aggression — I must commit 300 more (400 total) to continue. This hand has very poor equity and plays horribly out of position postflop against an aggressor. I stick to my plan: fold to substantial preflop aggression, protect my ~95BB stack, and wait for a better spot to exploit position or weaker ranges.

I am the chip leader with two hands remaining in the tournament. I hold Queen-Four suited on the button. This is a standard opening hand in three-handed play to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open. If I face significant resistance (a large 3-bet), I can comfortably fold to protect my lead, but raising here is the correct aggressive play to maintain my stack.

Calling with J6d from the small blind against a button raise. The pot odds (1.8:1) justify the call, the hand has reasonable showdown value with an overcard and flush draw potential, and postflop play will be favorable with position against likely multiple opponents. This is the final hand and I want to maximize value with playable hands while managing risk efficiently.

I’m in the big blind with 9♠2♠ facing a button raise to 300 and the small blind calling. 9-2s is one of the weakest playable suits — its only real playability is a backdoor flush and very low pair equity. In a 3-way pot its speculative value is poor and it’s likely dominated by better spades. To call I must put in 200 more for a pot that will be ~900 (requiring ~22% equity); my equity against a raiser+caller is well below that. A light 3-bet into two players is also a bad idea (not credible and unnecessarily commits chips). Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Checking on the flop with J♦-6♦ on K♦-7♥-8♣. This is hand 149 (final hand essentially). I have a strong flush draw (9 outs) but weak current hand strength (J-high). Out of position against Flash's c-bet threat. Checking allows me to see Flash's action, control pot size with my drawing hand, and gather information. This is superior to betting or raising out of position with a draw when Flash has the initiative.

I am currently the chip leader with only two hands remaining in the tournament. My primary objective is to maintain this lead and avoid any unnecessary large confrontations that could jeopardize my position. On this King-high board, my Queen-high has very little equity, and Claude's call from the small blind suggests he could have a variety of hands that hit this board or medium pocket pairs. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, minimize my risk, and give myself a chance to see a free turn card which could potentially improve my hand or allow for a cheaper showdown.

Betting 400 on the turn with J♦6♦ against Flash's checked flop and checked turn. The double-check indicates weakness. I have a strong 9-card flush draw plus additional outs (J's and 6's) for ~28-30% equity on the river. Betting applies fold equity pressure on a weak hand while maintaining value if Flash calls. This aggressive turn play is appropriate given the near-final position of this hand and Flash's demonstrated weakness. If called, I still have substantial equity with multiple ways to improve on the river.

The Queen on the turn improved my hand to second pair. Although Claude is leading into me after checking the flop, which could signify he also improved or was slow-playing a King, the price to call (400 into 1,100) is reasonable given the strength of my hand. My goal remains to protect my significant chip lead with only one hand remaining after this one. By calling, I maintain control over the pot size and avoid a high-risk confrontation while still seeing the river with a showdown-capable hand.

River with J♦6♦ on K♦ 7♥ 8♣ Q♣ K♠. I have jack-high with a missed flush draw. Flash called my aggressive turn bet, suggesting a legitimate holding (most likely a King given the preflop raise). The duplicated King on the river is terrible for my draw and likely improved Flash's hand. With no made hand and poor showdown value, I have no reason to put more chips in the pot. Checking allows me to see Flash's action and fold if they bet, preserving chips for position. This is optimal strategy on the final hand with a completely missed draw.

The river King pairs the board, which improves my hand to two pair, Kings and Queens. However, any King in Claude's hand now has three-of-a-kind. Claude's check on the river after betting the turn could be a trap with a King or a sign that he is worried about the King. Since I am the chip leader with only one hand remaining in the tournament after this, my priority is to protect my stack and avoid unnecessary risk. I have significant showdown value with my Queen, but betting is unlikely to get called by a worse hand and risks a check-raise from a King. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free and maintain my dominant position for the final hand.

Js Qh is a premium broadway hand that plays exceptionally well in 3-handed play. From the button with the best positional advantage, I should raise to apply pressure and build the pot with a strong holding. A raise to 350 (3.5x BB) is standard aggressive sizing that takes advantage of my position and hand strength while maintaining reasonable pot/stack ratios.

Claude raised to 350 from the button; pot is 500 and I (SB) must call 300 more. I hold J♣4♣ — suited but a weak, often dominated hand. Calling 300 into 500 needs ~37.5% equity (300/(500+300)), and my equity vs a button open (plus the BB yet to act) is well below that in a likely multiway or out-of-position scenario. A 3-bet bluff here is thin with two opponents and deep stacks; better to preserve chips and wait for a clearer spot. Fold.

On the final hand of the tournament, I hold Ace-Jack offsuit in the big blind, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Claude opened from the button for 350. AJo is well ahead of a typical button opening range. To maximize my stack and end the session strongly, I will put in a 3-bet to 1050. This puts pressure on Claude's wider opening range and builds a pot for my high-equity hand.

I have Js Qh, a premium broadway hand that plays well in this 3-handed spot. Flash's 3x reraise to 1,050 from the BB could represent a range—either a strong hand or position-based aggression given his large stack advantage (18,100 vs my 11,075). Calling 700 more into a 1,450 pot is reasonable. I maintain flexibility post-flop with good equity against most of Flash's likely range, and I retain the option to fold if the board is particularly unfavorable. Given this is the final hand, building a big pot with a strong broadway hand is acceptable, but reraising would commit too much with uncertain equity. A call keeps me in the hand with reasonable pot odds while preserving my stack integrity.

On the flop of 7c-7s-Ah, I have top pair with a Jack kicker. This is a very strong hand on a dry, paired board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I will follow up with a continuation bet. This bet aims to get value from weaker Aces (like A10, A9, or A8) and middle pocket pairs that Claude might call with once. Given the pot size is 2150, a bet of 750 (about 1/3 pot) is a standard sizing to maintain pressure while keeping the range wide.

Folding QJ on the 77A flop against Flash's 3-bet line. While broadway cards are generally strong, this specific board is dry and unhelpful. Against Flash's pre-flop 3-bet range (which likely includes AA, AK, AQ, pocket pairs), my unpaired QJ has insufficient equity (~10-15%) to justify calling 750 into a 3,650 pot when I need 17%+ equity to break even. The small c-bet sizing doesn't change the fundamental weakness of my holding. Preserving my 10,375 chip stack with a fold is the mathematically correct decision on the final hand.
